source.title: Handshake escrow and client-abort semantics for streaming sessions (OpenAI compatibility)
source.author: Diego Morales
source.description: Motivation: intermittent client disconnects during long-running streaming responses cause two classes of problems: 1) clients that reconnect within a short window are unable to resume because the server immediately tears down session state; 2) client-initiated cancels (or network-triggered resets) have ambiguous semantics with respect to tool/function call idempotency and structured output flushing. This PR introduces a handshake escrow token and clarified client-abort semantics to make resume/reconnect and abort behavior deterministic and OpenAI-compatible. Key changes: - Introduce a short-lived session escrow ticket (Escrow-Token) retained server-side for a configurable grace window to support reconnect-and-resume for active stream sessions. The token is returned in initial stream response headers and via the OpenAI-compat protocol extension. - Define deterministic semantics for client-initiated aborts: a cancelling client sends a terminal CANCEL event; server replies with a 499-equivalent closure in the OpenAI adapter and marks the session state as aborted but keeps an abort-marker for the escrow window to allow idempotent retries for in-flight tool calls. - On reconnect-with-ticket, runtime replays the last stable checkpointed structured-output frame (if any) and resumes token emission; if the abort-marker is present the reconnect is rejected unless the reconnect explicitly requests idempotent-retry scope. - Add explicit flush semantics for structured outputs and tool-call boundaries: runtime now ensures a terminal JSON frame is emitted and persisted before freeing durable KV for that session. This reduces split-output and truncation cases seen in production. - Compatibility mapping: map client cancel to HTTP/499 in our OpenAI adapter and document the header-based Escrow-Token workflow for clients migrating from the legacy resume token. - Safety: by default escrow window is 30s, configurable per-tenant; escrow tickets are single-use and rotated on successful resume. - Tests and docs: unit tests for handshake lifecycle, integration streaming smoke tests for reconnect/resume, updated API docs and SDK examples (Python/TS) showing Escrow-Token usage and abort/retry patterns. The description below includes a short migration checklist and operational knobs.
commits: feat(streaming): add handshake escrow ticket lifecycle and server retention logic
fix(streaming): map client cancel to 499 in openai adapter and emit Escrow-Token header
feat(runtime): persist structured-output checkpoints on tool-call boundaries
test(streaming): add reconnect/resume + abort unit and integration tests
docs: document escrow ticket workflow and SDK migration snippets
diff_snippets:
--- a/runtimes/serving/streaming/handshake.go
+++ b/runtimes/serving/streaming/handshake.go
@@ -45,6 +45,24 @@
 func startStream(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) {
+    // create escrow ticket for session resume within grace window
+    escrow := escrowManager.Create(r.Context(), sessionID, opts.EscrowWindow)
+    w.Header().Set("Escrow-Token", escrow.Token)
+    // checkpoint any pre-stream structured output
+    checkpointManager.Save(sessionID, initialFrame)
     
 }

--- a/adapters/openai/streaming_adapter.go
+++ b/adapters/openai/streaming_adapter.go
@@ -112,7 +112,11 @@
 func handleCancel(conn *Conn) {
-    conn.Close()
+    // mark the session as client-aborted but keep an abort marker for the escrow window
+    runtime.MarkAborted(conn.SessionID)
+    conn.EmitStatus("cancelled")
+    // adapter maps this to 499 for OSS compatibility
+    conn.SendHTTPStatus(499)
+    conn.Close()
 }
review_comments: Amir Shah: Nice isolation of escrow lifecycle. A few questions: how do we avoid unbounded memory retention if many clients disconnect?
Diego Morales: The escrow manager uses a TTL job with small in-memory index + backing in Redis. Default TTL 30s and per-tenant cap on concurrent escrows. Added infra config file to tune.
Leah Chen: We need to be explicit about SDK surface—are we returning a header only, or also emitting a JSON control frame?
Diego Morales: We return both: Escrow-Token header for simple HTTP clients and a control frame for streaming websocket/grpc flows. Added examples in both Python and TS SDKs.
Priya Natarajan: Edge case: what happens if a tool-call had side effects and client cancels mid-call? Should we default to retry or surface a nondeterministic error?

Diego Morales: We surface an abort-marker and allow explicit idempotent-retry on reconnect. We also added guidance in docs: recommend using idempotency tokens for tool calls where side effects matter; we do not auto-retry side-effectful calls without explicit retry intent.
ci_checks: perf: lightweight end-to-end bench — stable (no regression)
source.release_notes: Improves streaming session resilience: adds Escrow-Token for reconnect-and-resume, clarifies client-cancel semantics and OpenAI compatibility mapping (client cancel -> HTTP 499). Default escrow window 30s. SDK examples updated.
source.migration_notes: Clients that rely on resume should: 1) read Escrow-Token from initial response headers or control frame; 2) on reconnect include Escrow-Token in Resume-Request header/frame; 3) handle 499 as a cancel signal and consult docs for idempotent-retry flow. Server admins can tune escrow_window and per-tenant caps in infra config.
source.operational_notes: Default escrow retention is short (30s). High-scale tenants should increase Redis eviction capacity or lower per-tenant escrow caps. Metrics exposed: streaming.escrow.created, streaming.escrow.expired, streaming.abort.marked. Alerts recommended: rate of escrow creations spiking without resumes.

source.title: add content-disposition/metadata guards and per-tool payload quotas for multipart uploads
source.author: Avery Chen
source.description: Motivation: Tool/file attachments used by the OpenAI-compatible tool-calling surface were allowing poorly formed multipart uploads and oversized payloads which caused streaming stalls and OOMs in the serving runtime. This PR introduces explicit guards around Content-Disposition and file metadata, per-tool and per-account payload quotas for multipart/form-data parts, improved multipart boundary handling to avoid partial-read hangs in stream mode, and small compatibility tweaks so the OpenAI compatibility layer surfaces a stable `files` metadata object for tool calls. Changes are intentionally conservative: they add validation and soft-failure modes (400 + diagnostics) by default and an env flag to opt into hard rejection for stricter enterprise deployments. The description below summarizes goals, changes, test coverage, and rollout plan.
commits: feat(api): validate Content-Disposition and filename charset across multipart parts
fix(stream): avoid blocking partial reads by enforcing boundary lookahead and backpressure tokens
feat(quota): implement per-tool and per-account multipart-part size quotas and aggregate request limits
chore(compat): emit stable files metadata in OpenAI compatibility responses for tool attachments
test: add integration tests for malformed boundaries, oversized part, and content-type mismatches
docs: add runtime config docs and migration notes
snippets:
// validator: check content-disposition filename and optional metadata
if !is_valid_filename(&cd.filename) { return Err(BadRequest("invalid filename charset")); }
review_thread: Jordan Patel: Can we make the default per-part quota conservative for hosted API customers? It should not break common plugin/tool attachments (images, small files).
Avery Chen: Yes — default per_part is 5MB and aggregate 50MB for hosted; documented and configurable via runtime_defaults.yaml. Enterprise customers can set stricter values.
Nina Suresh: The streaming fix looks good but please add a test that reproduces the partial-boundary hang we saw in perf-canary PR. I left a nit in boundary_reader.rs.
Avery Chen: Added the perf-canary style test and tightened the timeout logic; replied to the nit with a small refactor to make lookahead non-blocking.
Mateo Alvarez: Approving after CI passes. One last ask: include a brief note in the OpenAI-compat release notes about the `files` metadata shape so downstream SDKs can adapt.
Avery Chen: Added release_notes and a console docs entry per request. Thanks!
ci_checks: perf-canary: rerun required (initial run flagged non-deterministic boundary test) -> rerun passed
source.merge_outcome: merged on 2026-03-08 via squash; merge_commit_summary: squash of 6 commits, changelog updated
source.release_notes: Added safer multipart handling for tool/file attachments: validates Content-Disposition/filename, enforces per-part and per-request quotas (defaults: 5MB per part, 50MB aggregate for hosted), hardens multipart boundary streaming to prevent partial-read hangs, and exposes a stable `files` metadata object in the OpenAI compatibility layer. Configurable via services/config/runtime_defaults.yaml and runtime env flags. Backwards-compatible soft-failure mode used by default.
post_merge_actions: hotpath: deploy runtime config with quotas to staging and run perf-canary with tool-attachment workload
notify: add note to SDK teams about files metadata change and sample payloads
monitor: create dashboard for multipart_rejects and multipart_stream_hang_rate

source.title: Hybrid comm/compute: staggered NCCL streams + dynamic kernel placement for tensor-parallel workloads
source.author: Maya Patel
source.description:
Motivation: For TP models we observed tail latency spikes when large collective operations serialized across the default NCCL stream and blocked compute kernels on subsequent micro-batches. This PR implements a hybrid comm/compute execution strategy: staggered NCCL comm streams per tensor-parallel group, opportunistic kernel placement on secondary compute streams, and a lightweight dynamic placement heuristic that routes small-kernel work to a dedicated low-latency stream to maximize overlap without increasing memory pressure.

Summary of changes: implemented a per-TP-group stream allocator that staggers all-reduce/all-gather launches across NCCL streams (comm_stream_0..N) to reduce contention; added a kernel placement layer in the runtime scheduler that classifies kernels by inferred latency (fast vs slow) and assigns them to either the main compute stream or a fast-path microbatch stream; introduced a KV-cache prefetch gate that issues early prefetches on a priority stream when sequence prefix hits are predicted; added guards for CUDA Graph capture compatibility and a deterministic fallback for constrained environments.

Design notes and rationale: staggering is conservative by default (stride = 2) and can be tuned per-deployment via runtime config (SERVER_TP_COMM_STRIDE). The placement heuristic uses a small sampled histogram of kernel durations (collected during warmup) and a tunable threshold; thresholds are persisted in the worker cache and aged out after config changes. We avoid aggressive fusion for very large kernels because it increases register pressure and can reduce overall throughput on A100-like hardware. The KV prefetch gate reduces latency for long context prefix hits by pre-warming the attention KV buffers on the priority stream while the previous micro-batch tokens finish compute on the main stream.

Testing and benchmarks: microbench harness updated under perf/bench/tp to include a staggered-comm profile. Results on a 16xA100-80GB cluster (tensor-parallel 8 across 2 GPUs per node) show: 9-14% single-request p99 latency reduction for 1-4 token continuations, and 8-12% end-to-end throughput improvement for large-batch streaming workloads when using the default stride=2. Memory overhead observed ~1.7% due to additional stream state and a small histogram buffer. Bench scripts and raw data attached in the PR (perf/bench/results/pr-24672/).

Files touched: runtime scheduler, low-level NCCL wrapper, kernel metadata table, kvcache prefetch codepath, perf harness and tests, docs note in docs/perf/tp-comm-tuning.md.

Migration and rollout: feature guarded behind runtime flag tp.hybrid_comm.enabled=false by default on main. Canary rollout plan: enable on dedicated performance canaries (redwood/perf-canary) for 72h, monitor token-level latency traces and p99 regressions. Automatic rollback if p99 increases by >3% or if GPU OOMs are observed.

Checklist: - [x] Unit tests for stream allocator and placement heuristic - [x] Integration test (simulated multi-GPU TP in CI) - [x] Perf harness and baseline comparison - [x] Docs and runtime config entries - [x] Canary/rollback plan

Related work: builds on prior NCCL tuning efforts (see ENG-1684 and prior PRs) but differs by introducing dynamic kernel placement and KV prefetch gating. Addresses customer case SUP-455 that reported tail latency on TP configs.

Notes: kept the changes small and feature-flagged to reduce blast radius. This PR intentionally does not enable CUDA Graph capture when the hybrid comm path is active; that follow-up will require graph replay orchestration and is tracked as ENG-1899.
commits: 9c6f2d7: runtime: add per-tp-group NCCL stream allocator and config
b2a1f14: scheduler: kernel placement layer and microbatch fast-path stream
f3d0e89: kvcache: add prefetch gate and priority stream pre-warm
e4b7a22: perf: add staggered-comm microbench and result collection hooks
d5a4c01: tests: unit tests for allocator and placement heuristic
a1b2c3d: docs: add tp comm tuning notes and rollout guidance
review_comments: Liam Chen: Can you add a flamegraph from the warmup run to show overlap? The numbers look promising but we need evidence of reduced serialization.
Maya Patel: Uploaded perf traces and flamegraphs to perf/bench/results/pr-24672/traces.tar.gz and added a short explanation in the bench README.
Rosa Martínez: Concern about CUDA Graph compatibility — please ensure deterministic fallback and add a comment where graphs will be gated.
Maya Patel: Added graph-compat guard and unit test for fallback path. Documented follow-up ENG-1899.
Ethan Park: Verified unit tests and ran integration on 4xV100. Observed no regressions. Approving pending CI rerun for the flaky perf test.
CI: perf/bench/staggered_comm_profile failed on first run due to transient NVLink timing; re-run passed.
source.release_notes: Introduce hybrid comm/compute strategy for TP: staggered NCCL streams, dynamic kernel placement, and KV-cache prefetching. Default disabled; can be enabled via tp.hybrid_comm.enabled. Expected p99 latency and throughput improvements on multi-GPU TP workloads. See docs/perf/tp-comm-tuning.md for tuning knobs.
source.merge_comment: Merged after perf confirmation and CI green. Feature is feature-flagged and will be enabled in canary clusters per rollout plan.

source.title: compat: strict media negotiation + per-file ingestion-rate-limits for tool attachments
source.author: Rohan Patel
source.release_notes: Adds stricter media-type negotiation in the OpenAI-compat layer for tool/file attachments, plus configurable per-file ingestion rate limits to protect streaming and tool-call stability. No breaking changes for well-formed clients; some malformed/ambiguous uploads will now be rejected instead of auto-coerced.
source.description:
Context: Our OpenAI-compat layer has had recurring issues when clients upload ambiguous or binary attachments without clear content-type or length headers. These cause parsing retries, confused streaming boundaries, and occasional function_call failures when tool runtimes receive corrupted payloads. This PR introduces two complementary protections: (1) deterministic media negotiation and normalization so the compat layer always annotates attachments with a canonical content-type + checksum; (2) per-file ingestion rate limits and adaptive chunk checks to prevent slow/bursty clients from overwhelming streaming parsers and downstream tool runtimes.

What this changes (high level):
- Compat layer now enforces an Accept/Content-Type negotiation step for incoming attachments. Preferred content-types for tool calls are application/json (pointer/meta), application/octet-stream (binary payloads), and multipart/form-data for multi-part uploads. Ambiguous cases (e.g. text/plain without charset for likely binary data) are rejected with a 400 and a clear error that lists accepted types.
- Attachments without a declared content-type will trigger a lightweight sniff: if first 512 bytes look like UTF-8 JSON/text we label application/json or text/plain; otherwise we label application/octet-stream. Sniffing is deterministic and logged for audits.
- Each file stream now carries an enforced per-file ingress rate limit (configurable per-customer and per-route). By default set to 4 MB/s for hosted API and 8 MB/s for Dedicated. Exceeding the rate returns a 429 with Retry-After and a hint header (X-File-Rate-Limit).
- For streaming uploads we added a small windowing protocol: the compat proxy tolerates out-of-order chunk arrival within a 500ms reorder window, but rejects incremental chunks that lack proper boundary markers when rate limits are tripped. This significantly reduces repeated partial-parsing in the serving runtime.
- For function/tool calls that reference files, we now require file metadata (content_type, size, sha256) in the function_call arguments. If missing, the compat layer computes sha256 and injects metadata before handing to the tool runtime. This prevents tool runtimes from having to re-read streams and reduces platform I/O.

Why this is safe and backward compatible:
- Well-formed clients that already send content-type and standard multipart/form-data will be unaffected.
- Clients that relied on opaque coercion of text/plain->binary will now get a clear error and guidance to set Content-Type or wrap the file as base64 in JSON. The error message includes a one-line migration hint to keep developer friction low.

Tests & validation included:
- Unit tests for negotiation logic, sniff heuristics, and sha256 injection.
- Integration tests that exercise multipart uploads, chunked transfer-encoding streams, and slow-client scenarios using our perf-canary harness.
- End-to-end tests: function_call with file refs across redwood (compat) -> runtime -> tool-invoker. Added a compatibility matrix test that runs against a matrix of SDK versions (python:1.x/2.x, ts:1.x) to ensure we don't regress client expectations.

Checklist: 
- [x] Implement negotiation + sniffing code in compat proxy
- [x] Add per-file rate-limiter middleware with config knobs on route/customer level
- [x] Compute and inject file metadata (sha256, content_type) for function_call refs
- [x] Add logging + audit events for sniffing decisions
- [x] Add unit/integration/e2e tests
- [x] Update docs/compat-guide (short migration blurb)

Related: ENG-2330 (rate limiting webhooks influenced design), PM-411 (docs/migration text), SUP-981 (customer incident that motivated stricter errors).
commits: b1e9c5f: compat: add media negotiation runner and sniff heuristics
9f4a8d2: middleware: per-file rate limiter + config hooks
6c7d3a1: inject: compute sha256 for files referenced in function_call payloads
2a4b7e9: tests: add e2e compat matrix and slow-client scenarios
d8f1a0b: docs: compatibility guide and migration hint
review_conversation: Ava Chen (reviewer): Please add a short migration note in docs/compatibility that callers should set Content-Type or base64-encode ambiguous files. Also call out default rate limits for Hosted vs Dedicated.

Rohan Patel (author): Added docs/compatibility/attachments.md and included default values in infra/config/defaults.yaml. I also added audit events when sniffing is used so we can count how often clients omit Content-Type.

Marta Novak (reviewer): Reasonable. Please add a unit test that specifically asserts that text/plain short snippets are not misclassified as binary when the first bytes are ASCII but content-length is > 1MB.
Rohan Patel (author): Added TestSniffLargeAsciiInBinary to services/compat/tests/sniff_test.go and updated the heuristics to consider size heuristics for ASCII-only streams. CI green after change.
Diego Alvarez (approver): Verified e2e with dedicated config. Metrics look stable and there is a small perf win from avoiding re-reads in runtime. Approving for squash-merge.
testing_summary: Unit: 72 new tests covering sniff heuristics, edge cases for content-type absence, and per-file limiter behavior (including burst and slow-client tests).
Integration: multi-part uploads + streaming chunk reorder scenarios exercised in a mock runtime that simulates slow disk and intermittent network.
E2E: compatibility matrix runs across python/ts SDK versions and a Dedicated profile run validating higher default rate-limit.

title: compat: strict media negotiation + per-file ingestion-rate-limits for tool attachments
preview:
Context: Our OpenAI-compat layer has had recurring issues when clients upload ambiguous or binary attachments without clear content-type or length headers. These cause parsing retries, confused streaming boundaries, and occasional function_call failures when tool runtimes receive corrupted payloads. This PR introduces two complementary protections: (1) deterministic media negotiation and normalization so the compat layer always annotates attachments with a canonical content-type + checksum; (2) per-file ingestion rate limits and adaptive chunk checks to prevent slow/bursty clients from overwhelming streaming parsers and downstream tool runtimes.

What this changes (high level):
- Compat layer now enforces an Accept/Content-Type negotiation step for incoming attachments. Preferred content-types for tool calls are application/json (pointer/meta), application/octet-stream (binary payloads), and multipart/form-data for multi-part uploads. Ambiguous cases (e.g. text/plain without charset for likely binary data) are rejected with a 400 and a clear error that lists accepted types.
- Attachments without a declared content-type will trigger a lightweight sniff: if first 512 bytes look like UTF-8 JSON/text we label application/json or text/plain; otherwise we label application/octet-stream. Sniffing is deterministic and logged for audits.
- Each file stream now carries an enforced per-file ingress rate limit (configurable per-customer and per-route). By default set to 4 MB/s for hosted API and 8 MB/s for Dedicated. Exceeding the rate returns a 429 with Retry-After and a hint header (X-File-Rate-Limit).
- For streaming uploads we added a small windowing protocol: the compat proxy tolerates out-of-order chunk arrival within a 500ms reorder window, but rejects incremental chunks that lack proper boundary markers when rate limits are tripped. This significantly reduces repeated partial-parsing in the serving runtime.
- For function/tool calls that reference files, we now require file metadata (content_type, size, sha256) in the function_call arguments. If missing, the compat layer computes sha256 and injects metadata before handing to the tool runtime. This prevents tool runtimes from having to re-read streams and reduces platform I/O.

Why this is safe and backward compatible:
- Well-formed clients that already send content-type and standard multipart/form-data will be unaffected.
- Clients that relied on opaque coercion of text/plain->binary will now get a clear error and guidance to set Content-Type or wrap the file as base64 in JSON. The error message includes a one-line migration hint to keep developer friction low.

Tests & validation included:
- Unit tests for negotiation logic, sniff heuristics, and sha256 injection.
- Integration tests that exercise multipart uploads, chunked transfer-encoding streams, and slow-client scenarios using our perf-canary harness.
- End-to-end tests: function_call with file refs across redwood (compat) -> runtime -> tool-invoker. Added a compatibility matrix test that runs against a matrix of SDK versions (python:1.x/2.x, ts:1.x) to ensure we don't regress client expectations.

Checklist: 
- [x] Implement negotiation + sniffing code in compat proxy
- [x] Add per-file rate-limiter middleware with config knobs on route/customer level
- [x] Compute and inject file metadata (sha256, content_type) for function_call refs
- [x] Add logging + audit events for sniffing decisions
- [x] Add unit/integration/e2e tests
- [x] Update docs/compat-guide (short migration blurb)

Related: ENG-2330 (rate limiting webhooks influenced design), PM-411 (docs/migration text), SUP-981 (customer incident that motivated stricter errors).

source.title: v1/chat: stabilize streaming handshake; enforce stop-token precedence and introduce role-alias mapping
source.author: Ava Chen
source.description: Context: Customers integrating via the OpenAI /v1/chat.completions surface have seen two classes of issues in production: 1) transient stream disconnects during tool/function call handoffs (client resumes see inconsistent framing), and 2) ambiguity in how stop tokens are applied when stop arrays are provided alongside role-level stop hints or tool-generated token sequences. This PR unifies several compatibility behaviors to reduce surprise for downstream clients and to harden streaming handshakes during function/tool call routing. Motivation: Provide predictable, backward-compatible semantics for v1 chat parity while reducing the failure surface during streaming toolcalls. Goals: (a) stabilize the streaming handshake so that tool/function call initiation and the subsequent resumption of the assistant stream never produce overlapping or dropped deltas, (b) define and implement a deterministic stop-token precedence order (explicit request param > tool-produced stop sequence > role-level hints), (c) add an opt-in role_alias mapping so customers sending alternative role names (e.g., "assistant_bot", "system_agent") map to canonical OpenAI roles for compatibility, and (d) wrap logprobs and structured scores in a deterministic envelope that matches OpenAI parity expectations when streaming deltas are emitted. Changes: - runtime/stream: implement a two-phase handshake for toolcall handoff with explicit ACK/FIN markers and idempotent resume tokens so retries do not duplicate tokens - api/openai_compat: introduce a stop precedence resolver that accepts stop arrays and role/tool hints and returns the canonical stop set applied to generation - api/openai_compat: add role_alias config parsing and mapping; default mappings included and overridable via server config - models/compat: attach a small envelope to streaming delta messages: choices[].delta.{content,role,tool_call,logprobs} to avoid ambiguous flattened fields - sdk/python: update OpenAI-compat adapter to accept role_alias and expose resolved_stop in response metadata - tests: new integration tests exercising stream resume, stop precedence and role aliasing Backwards compatibility and rollout: default behavior is backward-compatible; the stop precedence policy is additive and only changes behavior when multiple stop sources are present (rare). Role-alias mapping is opt-in per-tenant via configuration flag; we set sane defaults for common aliases. The streaming handshake change is released behind a runtime feature-flag; feature is enabled for canary tenants and then rolled out to all after 72h of telemetry. Checklist: [x] unit tests for precedence resolver [x] integration test for stream resume [x] SDK adapter updates [x] e2e smoke in Canary [x] docs note in openai/v1_chat.md Testing: CI includes unit and integration runs. Canary telemetry evaluated for token duplication and error rates for 72h. Observability: added new metrics stream.handshake_retries, stream.duplicate_delta_count, compat.role_alias_mappings_applied Related: ENG-1542 (stream stability), ENG-1588 (openai compat stop semantics), SUP-4321 customer report Refs: docs/openai/v1_chat.md, runtime/stream/README.md
commits: 9c4f2d1: stream: add idempotent resume tokens and ACK/FIN markers
1b7a6e3: openai_compat: stop precedence resolver and tests
3d5f8a0: api: role_alias parsing and default mapping table
4f0b2c9: sdk/python: expose resolved_stop and update examples
a7e1b56: tests: integration for stream resume + stop interactions
source.discussion: Jonah Ruiz: The handshake ACK/FIN design looks good but we need to ensure the resume token doesn't leak sensitive session ids. Ava Chen: switched resume token to a hash-based opaque ID and added a TTL; added tests to assert no raw session ids make it to wire. Priya Nambiar: request to add more telemetry around duplicate deltas and to gate rollout on 0 duplication in Canary for 24h. Ava Chen: added metrics and modified Canary rollout to 72h; will hold if duplicate_delta_count > 0. Luca Moretti: minor nit on naming stop precedence function; suggested stop_resolver.resolve_precedence -> renamed to resolve_stop_order. PR Author responses addressed; reviewers approved on 2026-03-02. CI initially failed a flakey test (test_stream_reconnect) on first run; rerun passed. Merge strategy: squash; changelog entry added to docs and release notes updated.
source.release_notes:
- Stabilize streaming handshake for tool/function handoffs to prevent duplicated or dropped deltas.
- Define stop-token precedence (explicit param > tool sequences > role hints) to remove ambiguity in mixed stop sources.
- Add role_alias mapping to improve compatibility with non-canonical role names from third-party clients.
- Emit structured logprobs in choices[].delta.logprobs during streaming to match OpenAI parity formats.
source.notes_for_ops: Feature gated behind runtime flag 'enable_chatv1_stream_handshake_v2'; enable in Canary first. Monitor metrics: stream.handshake_retries, stream.duplicate_delta_count. Rollback: disable feature flag and invalidate resume tokens by bumping resume-token-salt. Estimated rollout impact: low, opt-in role alias mapping disabled by default for enterprise tenants.
source.risk_assessment: Medium: changes affect runtime message framing and stop semantics. Mitigations include feature flag, canary rollout, and conservative default stop precedence. Extensive unit/integration tests added.
source.testing_summary: Local unit tests: 98/98 passing. Integration test suite: 24 new tests added; CI run after fixes: all green. Canary telemetry evaluated for 72h before full rollout. Manual smoke tested with python sdk adapter examples.

source.title: Optimize INT8 dequant-fusion kernel and smoothquant scheduling to reduce p99 and token cost
source.author: Ethan Zhao
source.release_notes: Performance: Improved INT8/FP8 dequant fusion paths and scheduling; lower p99 latency on common seq-len workloads; no API changes.
source.description:
Motivation: For the quantized inference stack we've been shipping two main bottlenecks: (1) the dequantization+matmul fusion path for INT8 kernels introduces extra memory traffic and kernel launches at each attention and FFN block; (2) SmoothQuant scaling interacts poorly with our scheduler and continuous-batching heuristics, causing variable p99 regressions under mixed-sequence workloads. This PR introduces three coordinated changes: 1) a fused dequantize+gemm kernel that accepts packed INT8 weights and produces FP16 accumulation with optional requantization hooks; 2) a scheduling tweak that biases smoothquant scaling application to the slower stage of the pipeline and batches compatible sequence shapes together more aggressively; 3) a lightweight KV-cache-aware fast path that skips per-step dequant for cached KV blocks and reuses FP16 cached keys when the model quantization profile allows it.
High-level summary of changes: - Implement fused_int8_dequant_gemm CUDA kernel with vectorized loads, int8->fp16 accumulation, and single-kernel scale+bias application; exposed via runtime/kernel_registry. - Add quant_profile metadata to model descriptors to select the optimal kernel variant at load time (INT8_PACKED, FP8_DYNAMIC, FP16_FALLBACK). - Scheduler: introduce SequenceShapeAffinity rule to group similar-length requests for continuous batching; add SmoothQuantWindow heuristic to only apply per-layer scaling on first N tokens of a request when streaming is enabled. - KV cache: store an optional FP16 shadow of int8-packed KV blocks for reuse; add LRU soft cap and transparent eviction. - Telemetry: new perf counters (kernel_time_fused_int8, kv_shadow_hit, smoothquant_applied) and unit tests.
Performance numbers (microbench on T4 and A10G; averaged across 50k requests of mixed lengths 16/128/512): - Baseline (old INT8 path): p50 12.8ms p95 38.2ms p99 78.5ms throughput 22 toks/s - This PR (fused + scheduling + kv-shadow): p50 11.4ms p95 29.6ms p99 49.3ms throughput 28 toks/s - Relative: p99 reduced ~37%, throughput +27%, token GPU time reduced ~18%.
Safety and compatibility: default model load remains unchanged; INT8 packed models must declare quant_profile or fallback to old path. This avoids accidental perf regressions for older deployments.
Checklist: - [x] Kernel implementation + unit tests - [x] Scheduler heuristics and e2e perf harness - [x] KV-shadow LRU + eviction tests - [x] Telemetry and console dashboard metric wiring - [x] Backwards-compatible model descriptor changes - [x] Integration smoke runs on Dedicated cluster
Implementation notes: commits include a sequence of focused patches (see commits field). The fused kernel uses 128-bit vector loads and tail-handling similar to other fused kernels in kernels/cuda/; we add a small inline assembly path for faster requant when supported by arch. The scheduler change is additive: SequenceShapeAffinity is a soft hint; it will not prevent immediate dispatch when latency SLO is tight. KV-shadow stores fp16 only when the quant_profile allows dequant-lossless shadowing.
Files changed (high-level): - runtime/kernel_registry.py: register fused_int8_dequant_gemm - kernels/cuda/fused_int8_dequant_gemm.cu: new kernel implementation and tests - runtime/scheduler/affinity.py: SequenceShapeAffinity and SmoothQuantWindow heuristics - runtime/kv_cache/kv_shadow.py: FP16 shadow store, eviction policy - model/quant_profiles.py: quant_profile schema and loaders - telemetry/metrics.py: new counters and tagging - tests/perf/test_quant_kernels.py: microbench harness and assertions - docs/quantization.md: updated guidance and model packaging notes
Commits (summary): - a1c3f8a: initial fused kernel prototype and unit tests - b4d2e10: vectorization, tail handling, and arch-specific paths - c7f9b01: expose kernel via kernel_registry and add quant_profile selector - d2a8f44: implement SequenceShapeAffinity scheduler rule and tests - e5b1c3f: KV-shadow store with LRU and eviction unit tests - f1039d7: telemetry counters and console dashboard wires - f8b6a22: perf tuning, CI fixes, and documentation
Update/Review conversation (selected): - Priya Natarajan (reviewer): "Great direction. My main concern is memory overhead for the FP16 KV shadow under high-concurrency. Can we bound memory and prioritize eviction for older sequences?" Author reply (Ethan): "Answered: KV-shadow uses an LRU soft cap with eviction triggered at 0.6*mem_limit and immediate reclamation to 0.8*mem_limit to avoid thrashing; added bench in tests to ensure p99 doesn't regress under pressure." - Liam O'Connor: "Kernel looks good; please add more comments around the arch-specific asm path and a fallback when not supported." Author: "Added comments and a runtime is_x86_avx512 check; fallback to scalar path included." - Sara Kim: "How does this interact with canary rollout? We need a safe toggle in runtime to opt-out quickly." Author: "Added runtime feature flag runtime.flags.quant_fused_enabled and a per-deployment override exposed to Dedicated/Private configs; tests added."
CI checks summary: - linux-build: pass - unit-tests: pass - perf-regression: pass (bench run) - flake8/lint: pass - integration-smoke(dedicated): pass
Merge outcome: merged via squash on 2026-03-05 by Ethan Zhao; merge commit f9c2d11. Post-merge actions: - Backfill quant_profile to community model registry (docs/quantization.md has migration steps) - Dashboard alert to watch kernel_time_fused_int8 p99 for next 72 hours - Coordinate with Customer Success for a small set of Dedicated customers to enable feature flag during next maintenance window.

This PR introduces three coordinated changes: 1) a fused dequantize+gemm kernel that accepts packed INT8 weights and produces FP16 accumulation with optional requantization hooks; 2) a scheduling tweak that biases smoothquant scaling application to the slower stage of the pipeline and batches compatible sequence shapes together more aggressively; 3) a lightweight KV-cache-aware fast path that skips per-step dequant for cached KV blocks and reuses FP16 cached keys when the model quantization profile allows it.
High-level summary of changes: - Implement fused_int8_dequant_gemm CUDA kernel with vectorized loads, int8->fp16 accumulation, and single-kernel scale+bias application; exposed via runtime/kernel_registry. - Add quant_profile metadata to model descriptors to select the optimal kernel variant at load time (INT8_PACKED, FP8_DYNAMIC, FP16_FALLBACK). - Scheduler: introduce SequenceShapeAffinity rule to group similar-length requests for continuous batching; add SmoothQuantWindow heuristic to only apply per-layer scaling on first N tokens of a request when streaming is enabled. - KV cache: store an optional FP16 shadow of int8-packed KV blocks for reuse; add LRU soft cap and transparent eviction. - Telemetry: new perf counters (kernel_time_fused_int8, kv_shadow_hit, smoothquant_applied) and unit tests.
Performance numbers (microbench on T4 and A10G; averaged across 50k requests of mixed lengths 16/128/512): - Baseline (old INT8 path): p50 12.8ms p95 38.2ms p99 78.5ms throughput 22 toks/s - This PR (fused + scheduling + kv-shadow): p50 11.4ms p95 29.6ms p99 49.3ms throughput 28 toks/s - Relative: p99 reduced ~37%, throughput +27%, token GPU time reduced ~18%.
Safety and compatibility: default model load remains unchanged; INT8 packed models must declare quant_profile or fallback to old path. This avoids accidental perf regressions for older deployments.
Checklist: - [x] Kernel implementation + unit tests - [x] Scheduler heuristics and e2e perf harness - [x] KV-shadow LRU + eviction tests - [x] Telemetry and console dashboard metric wiring - [x] Backwards-compatible model descriptor changes - [x] Integration smoke runs on Dedicated cluster
Implementation notes: commits include a sequence of focused patches (see commits field). The fused kernel uses 128-bit vector loads and tail-handling similar to other fused kernels in kernels/cuda/; we add a small inline assembly path for faster requant when supported by arch. The scheduler change is additive: SequenceShapeAffinity is a soft hint; it will not prevent immediate dispatch when latency SLO is tight. KV-shadow stores fp16 only when the quant_profile allows dequant-lossless shadowing.
Files changed (high-level): - runtime/kernel_registry.py: register fused_int8_dequant_gemm - kernels/cuda/fused_int8_dequant_gemm.cu: new kernel implementation and tests - runtime/scheduler/affinity.py: SequenceShapeAffinity and SmoothQuantWindow heuristics - runtime/kv_cache/kv_shadow.py: FP16 shadow store, eviction policy - model/quant_profiles.py: quant_profile schema and loaders - telemetry/metrics.py: new counters and tagging - tests/perf/test_quant_kernels.py: microbench harness and assertions - docs/quantization.md: updated guidance and model packaging notes
Commits (summary): - a1c3f8a: initial fused kernel prototype and unit tests - b4d2e10: vectorization, tail handling, and arch-specific paths - c7f9b01: expose kernel via kernel_registry and add quant_profile selector - d2a8f44: implement SequenceShapeAffinity scheduler rule and tests - e5b1c3f: KV-shadow store with LRU and eviction unit tests - f1039d7: telemetry counters and console dashboard wires - f8b6a22: perf tuning, CI fixes, and documentation
Update/Review conversation (selected): - Priya Natarajan (reviewer): "Great direction. My main concern is memory overhead for the FP16 KV shadow under high-concurrency. Can we bound memory and prioritize eviction for older sequences?" Author reply (Ethan): "Answered: KV-shadow uses an LRU soft cap with eviction triggered at 0.6*mem_limit and immediate reclamation to 0.8*mem_limit to avoid thrashing; added bench in tests to ensure p99 doesn't regress under pressure." - Liam O'Connor: "Kernel looks good; please add more comments around the arch-specific asm path and a fallback when not supported." Author: "Added comments and a runtime is_x86_avx512 check; fallback to scalar path included." - Sara Kim: "How does this interact with canary rollout? We need a safe toggle in runtime to opt-out quickly." Author: "Added runtime feature flag runtime.flags.quant_fused_enabled and a per-deployment override exposed to Dedicated/Private configs; tests added."
CI checks summary: - linux-build: pass - unit-tests: pass - perf-regression: pass (bench run) - flake8/lint: pass - integration-smoke(dedicated): pass
Merge outcome: merged via squash on 2026-03-05 by Ethan Zhao; merge commit f9c2d11. Post-merge actions: - Backfill quant_profile to community model registry (docs/quantization.md has migration steps) - Dashboard alert to watch kernel_time_fused_int8 p99 for next 72 hours - Coordinate with Customer Success for a small set of Dedicated customers to enable feature flag during next maintenance window.

source.title: hybrid-staged-canary-controller-with-statistical-gates-and-fallthrough-routing
source.author: Marisol Vega
source.description: Motivation: enable safer multi-stage experiments and automated rollback by introducing a hybrid staged canary controller that combines time/traffic phasing, Bayesian statistical gates, and deterministic fallthrough routing. This PR implements a controller that can run A/B tests, shadow trials, and progressive canaries using a configurable stability window, significance threshold, and quarantine queue. Key features: 1) BayesianSignificanceEngine (configurable priors) to decide pass/fail for metric families; 2) HealthSnapshot store that captures per-route histograms and model KV cache hit rates at sample intervals; 3) WeightDecaySchedule to gradually reduce canary traffic on persistent regressions before triggering full rollback; 4) FallthroughRouting that routes traffic to a fallback model variant instantly on hard failures while preserving request affinity; 5) QuarantineQueue and automated re-evaluation that isolates problematic model variants and automatically schedules a re-test after a cool-down with adjusted batch/quant settings; 6) DriftDetector to abort experiments when input distribution divergence exceeds threshold; 7) Integration hooks for Redwood Optimize to emit suggested quantization/batching profiles for canaries. Checklist: - [x] controller implementation and reconciliation loop - [x] Bayesian engine + unit tests - [x] health snapshot persistence and API - [x] fallthrough routing and affinity preservation - [x] quarantine queue and automated re-test job - [x] shadow traffic passthrough mode - [x] docs and runbook updates - [x] e2e smoke test in perf-canary harness How to test locally: run ./hack/dev-canary with two local model variants, set linear key ENG-9123 as test target, verify that induced regression (synthetic label noise) triggers weight decay then quarantine and eventual rollback. Backward compatibility: defaults preserve previous canary semantics if hybrid_mode=false. Migration: adds CRD fields canary.strategy and canary.statsEngine but controller tolerates absent fields. Related: ENG-9123 for rollout design, ENG-9188 for metric extraction, SUP-447 incident notes. Impact: improves safety and reduces noisy manual rollbacks; expected to reduce manual interventions by ~40% for staged rollouts (internal estimate).
commits: a7d9f3b: controller: initial reconciliation loop and CRD scaffolding
b2c1f21: stats: BayesianSignificanceEngine and unit tests
c9f4d66: snapshot: health snapshot store + retention policy
d1a2b34: routing: fallthrough routing and affinity token propagation
e4f0c9e: quarantine: queue implementation and re-eval job
f5a7e2b: integration: Optimize hooks and suggested-config emitter
0a1b2c3: docs: rollout runbook and examples
1c2d3e4: tests: e2e smoke and perf-canary harness updates
review_conversation: Ethan Park (reviewer) - 2026-02-20: Nit: prefer context-based timeouts in controller.go instead of fixed sleeps. Also please add a comment explaining the prior choice in BayesianSignificanceEngine.

Marisol Vega (author) - 2026-02-21: Addressed both points: replaced fixed sleeps with context-aware timers and added comments explaining weakly informative priors (beta(2,2)) used for early-stage canaries.

Lena Cho (reviewer) - 2026-02-23: Question: how does fallthrough routing handle streamed responses and partial outputs? Could a fallback produce different tokenization that breaks client expectations?

Marisol Vega (author) - 2026-02-24: Fallthrough preserves request affinity and uses a header to indicate fallback; streaming mode will flush current partials and re-issue to fallback variant to avoid mixed-token responses. Added protocol-level test and doc note.
Ravi Malhotra (reviewer) - 2026-02-27: Approve after small test harness fix and add a safety toggle for auto-quarantine.
CI - 2026-02-28: build failed on lint (gofmt) on pkg/rollout/routing/fallthrough.go; author pushed fix and re-ran CI.
CI - 2026-03-01: all checks passed (unit, integration, lint, e2e smoke).
Maintainer Merge - 2026-03-05: merged via squash (merge_method=squash).
source.release_notes: Adds a hybrid staged canary controller with Bayesian statistical gates, fallthrough routing, quarantine/re-test automation, and integration points for Optimize suggestions. Provides safer automated rollbacks and input-drift based aborts; defaults maintain backward compatibility.
source.security_implications: No new broad network surface exposed; controller CRDs respect RBAC and only rollout-admin role may toggle auto-quarantine. Added audit events for quarantine and rollback actions.
source.perf_impact: Introduces lightweight snapshotting (~5-10ms per sample) and an optional drift detector (configurable sampling rate). Expected negligible p95 impact when run at recommended sample rates.

source.title: fragmented-kernel-workspace-pool-kv-rolling-reserve-and-adaptive-batching-prioritizer
source.author: Evan Chen
source.description: Motivation: Under heavy continuous-batching workloads we observe fragmented temporary workspace allocations and conservative KV cache reservations that inflate peak GPU memory by ~15-25%. This PR introduces a kernel-fragment pool allocator, a compacting fragment coalescer, a KV rolling-reserve policy with age-based demotion, and an adaptive batching prioritizer that protects tail latency while increasing packing efficiency. Goals: 1) reduce peak transient workspace (scratch) footprint, 2) lower KV cache reserved overhead for workloads with skewed hotsets, 3) keep 95p/99p latency equal or improved under steady-state continuous batching. Changes (high level): - runtime/worker: implement FragmentPool allocator that suballocates kernel workspace into variable-length fragments mapped to kernel affinity classes; supports fast reuse and best-fit coalescing on free. - runtime/scheduler: add AdaptiveBatchPrioritizer which reorders queued requests to reduce internal fragmentation and avoid long-lived large-activation requests from blocking smaller quick requests (tail-latency guardrails). - runtime/kvcache: KV rolling-reserve: reserve window size per-model by observed hit-age histogram; demote rarely accessed entries from reserved to spillable on a time-decay basis. - runtime/alloc: optional lightweight compaction pass that coalesces adjacent free fragments during low-load periods. - tests & tooling: new microbench harnesses for fragmented allocation patterns, end-to-end perf scenario files, and flamegraph capture hooks for workspace churn. Implementation notes: - FragmentPool is lock-free per-GPU-stream for fast path allocation; fallback to pooled mutex for rare large allocations. - Coalescing is limited to a short-duration cooperative pass triggered by low utilization to avoid impacting latency. - Rolling-reserve tracks per-key last-access epoch and uses a small LRU-friendly structure to decide demotion; no synchronous CPU writeback in hot path. Performance summary (measured on 8xA100, config: steady continuous batching, mixed length): - Peak GPU memory usage: -18% median, -23% under the 95th percentile scenarios. - 95p latency: -12% (reduction) for short requests; unchanged for long requests. - Throughput (tokens/sec): +6% due to improved packing and reduced stalls. Safety and fallback: All features are disabled by default for existing customers; new flags are behind runtime flags and can be toggled per-deployment. Rolling-reserve defaults to conservative limits; fragmentation coalescer disabled on single-GPU hosts. Checklist: - [x] unit tests for FragmentPool api - [x] integration tests for scheduler reordering - [x] KV reserve regression tests - [x] perf harness runs (attached) - [x] docs entry for runtime flags Migration/compatibility: No API changes; runtime flags control behavior. Observability: added metrics: fragpool.alloc_count, fragpool.coalesce_time_ms, kv.reserve_demotions, batch.prioritizer_reorders. Related: ENG-5643 (workspaces), ENG-5711 (KV hotset sizing).
commits: feat(runtime): add FragmentPool allocator with best-fit fragment selection
perf(scheduler): implement AdaptiveBatchPrioritizer and integrate with dispatcher
feat(kv): rolling-reserve policy with age-based demotion and lightweight tracking
test: add fragmented-allocation microbench and end-to-end continuous-batching scenario
chore: add metrics and flamegraph hooks for workspace churn
fix: guard coalescer behind low-utilization window to avoid latency regressions
docs: runtime flags and rollout guidance
changed_files: runtime/worker/fragment_pool.cc — new allocator: fragment headers, free-lists per-size-class, fast-path lock-free allocations
runtime/worker/fragment_pool.h — API + configuration knobs for max_fragment_size and coalesce_threshold
runtime/scheduler/adaptive_prioritizer.cc — request scoring, tail-latency guardrail, unit tests
runtime/kv/rolling_reserve.cc — per-key epoch tracking and demotion logic
runtime/metrics/registry.cc — add fragpool.* and kv.reserve_demotions metrics
tools/perf/bench_frag_alloc.yaml — new perf scenario for fragmentation patterns
tests/integration/continuous_batching_frag_test.cc — integration tests for memory and latency assertions
review_comments: Priya: Nice end-to-end numbers. Can you add a TTL for the age histogram to bound memory of the tracking structure?
Author(Evan): Added a bounded epoch ring buffer and hard cap with eviction; described in comments and unit tests (see rolling_reserve.cc).
Luca: Concern about coalescer causing jitter. Suggest limiting to when 1) queue length < X and 2) no high-priority in-progress requests.
Author: Coalescer now checks low_utilization_window and active_high_prio_count before running; added test that simulates jitter-sensitive path.
Maya: Please add a short note to rollout docs about disabling per-deployment and expected safety checks.
Author: docs/runtime_flags.md updated with rollout steps and suggested guard rails.
Priya: CI flakes on Windows GPU job; unrelated build infrastructure timeout — retriggered and passed.
Luca: Approve after the small nits were addressed.
Priya: LGTM.
source.merge_outcome: merged (squash) on 2026-03-05 by release-bot; merge commit includes summarized perf numbers and docs link
source.release_notes:
- Introduces FragmentPool allocator that reduces transient workspace usage and improves packing.
- Adds KV rolling-reserve policy to lower reserved cache footprint for skewed workloads.
- Introduces AdaptiveBatchPrioritizer to improve packing efficiency while protecting tail latency.
- Runtime flags added to opt-in and tune behavior: --fragpool-enabled, --frag-coalesce-window, --kv-rolling-reserve.
Notes: Disabled by default; recommend gradual rollout with perf monitoring and flamegraph capture.

source.title: tokenized-fairness-scaler-and-util-gap-reporter
source.author: Priya Kaur
source.release_notes: Introduces a queue-latency-aware autoscaler that allocates tokenized burst credits to dedicated tenants and a Utilization Gap Reporter that surfaces sustained under/over-utilization. Backwards-compatible config flags; rollout gated behind feature flag and 2-step canary.
source.description:
Context and motivation: customers on Dedicated have asked for better fairness when bursts co-exist with steady throughput tenants. Existing burst pools and reservations ensure throughput but can starve latency-sensitive tenants during microbursts. This PR implements a tokenized fairness scaler: a lightweight token accounting layer that mints per-tenant 'burst tokens' proportional to committed throughput and dynamically redistributes spare tokens based on queue-latency buckets.

Design summary: the scaler observes per-tenant request queue latency percentiles (p50/p95/p99) and token consumption rate. When a tenant's p95 exceeds its latency target and tokens are available in the cluster pool, the allocator grants ephemeral tokens that raise the tenant's concurrency cap for short windows (configurable TTL). Tokens are reclaimed proactively when latency recovers or when they expire. Token minting respects tenant share weights and a soft floor so smaller tenants cannot be starved.

Utilization Gap Reporter: a new telemetry pipeline sampler that computes 'util-gap' = committed_capacity - observed_effective_throughput over rolling windows. Significant positive gap (>X%) indicates underutilized reserved capacity; negative gap suggests chronic oversubscription and triggers alerts. The reporter aggregates per-tenant and per-cluster signals and emits events to the Console and to the autoscaler's feedback loop.

Key behaviors and safety: - Autoscaler only grants tokens if cluster headroom >= headroom_threshold config. - Tokens are time-limited (default 30s) and double-checked before increasing concurrency to avoid drift. - Manual overrides: operators can pin token budgets per-tenant via the Dedicated Console. - Feature gated: flag 'tokenized_fair_scaler_v1' controls rollout.

Files of note: see changed files list below for implementation and tests.

Testing and validation: - Unit tests for token accounting, TTL expiry, and reclaim logic. - Integration tests with synthetic traffic generator that simulates bursty and steady tenants and validates latency SLOs improve without increasing aggregate CPU/Tensor utilization. - Canary plan: deploy to a single Dedicated cluster with low-risk tenants (internal sandbox), monitor util-gap and latency deltas for 48h, then expand to two clusters before full rollout.

Checklist: - [x] Design doc linked (ENG-1842 tokenized-fairness-scaler) - [x] Unit & integration tests - [x] Telemetry for util-gap metrics - [x] Console toggles and tenant budget API - [x] Feature flag behind canary - [x] Safety tests and chaos run

Related: references existing autoscaler work (ENG-1532) but intentionally keeps runtime changes minimal to reduce risk.
commits: c1a4d9f: feat(autoscaler): add token ledger and allocator core
d2b7e33: feat(telemetry): util-gap reporter and aggregation pipeline
a9f4b11: test(autoscaler): unit tests for token expiry and reclaim
f7c3e22: infra: add feature flag and console wiring
b6d5c44: docs: runtime config and rollout notes
review_comments: Ethan Park: Nice separation of concerns. Two asks: 1) add a bounded backoff when cluster headroom flips rapidly; 2) be explicit about how token grants interact with existing reservation credits.

Priya Kaur: @Ethan added a 5s jittered debounce for headroom flips and a revocation hook that consults reservation credits before granting. See allocator.go lines ~210-245. Added unit tests that exercise the interaction.
María López: Please add observability to show token churn rate (tokens/sec) and per-tenant average grant duration. Also suggest a lower default TTL (15s) for initial canary.
Priya Kaur: Added metrics token_churn_count and avg_grant_duration_ms. Lowered default TTL in feature flag for canary. Updated docs explaining canary defaults.
Zoe Wang: Integration tests look good but add an assertion that total cluster GPU utilization does not exceed baseline + 5% across canary scenario.
Priya Kaur: Added assertion in canary harness; ran locally and integrated into CI.
source.change_summary: Adds tokenized fairness autoscaler that grants ephemeral burst tokens based on queue latency, plus a Utilization Gap Reporter for reserved capacity visibility. Backwards-compatible, feature-flagged rollout.

Related: references existing autoscaler work (ENG-1532) but intentionally keeps runtime changes minimal to reduce risk.
text:
Context and motivation: customers on Dedicated have asked for better fairness when bursts co-exist with steady throughput tenants. Existing burst pools and reservations ensure throughput but can starve latency-sensitive tenants during microbursts. This PR implements a tokenized fairness scaler: a lightweight token accounting layer that mints per-tenant 'burst tokens' proportional to committed throughput and dynamically redistributes spare tokens based on queue-latency buckets.

Design summary: the scaler observes per-tenant request queue latency percentiles (p50/p95/p99) and token consumption rate. When a tenant's p95 exceeds its latency target and tokens are available in the cluster pool, the allocator grants ephemeral tokens that raise the tenant's concurrency cap for short windows (configurable TTL). Tokens are reclaimed proactively when latency recovers or when they expire. Token minting respects tenant share weights and a soft floor so smaller tenants cannot be starved.

Utilization Gap Reporter: a new telemetry pipeline sampler that computes 'util-gap' = committed_capacity - observed_effective_throughput over rolling windows. Significant positive gap (>X%) indicates underutilized reserved capacity; negative gap suggests chronic oversubscription and triggers alerts. The reporter aggregates per-tenant and per-cluster signals and emits events to the Console and to the autoscaler's feedback loop.

Key behaviors and safety: - Autoscaler only grants tokens if cluster headroom >= headroom_threshold config. - Tokens are time-limited (default 30s) and double-checked before increasing concurrency to avoid drift. - Manual overrides: operators can pin token budgets per-tenant via the Dedicated Console. - Feature gated: flag 'tokenized_fair_scaler_v1' controls rollout.

Files of note: see changed files list below for implementation and tests.

Testing and validation: - Unit tests for token accounting, TTL expiry, and reclaim logic. - Integration tests with synthetic traffic generator that simulates bursty and steady tenants and validates latency SLOs improve without increasing aggregate CPU/Tensor utilization. - Canary plan: deploy to a single Dedicated cluster with low-risk tenants (internal sandbox), monitor util-gap and latency deltas for 48h, then expand to two clusters before full rollout.

Checklist: - [x] Design doc linked (ENG-1842 tokenized-fairness-scaler) - [x] Unit & integration tests - [x] Telemetry for util-gap metrics - [x] Console toggles and tenant budget API - [x] Feature flag behind canary - [x] Safety tests and chaos run

Related: references existing autoscaler work (ENG-1532) but intentionally keeps runtime changes minimal to reduce risk.

source.title: per-layer kernel occupancy probes + adaptive batch-window tracer (KV-stamp profiler)
source.author: Maya Patel
source.release_notes: Adds non-invasive runtime probes for per-layer kernel occupancy and an adaptive batch-window tracer; exposes dashboard metrics and a microbench harness. Backwards compatible; off by default.
source.description:
Summary: Instrumentation and microbench additions to help diagnose layer-level kernel occupancy and batch-window interactions with KV-cache. This PR introduces a lightweight per-layer occupancy probe, a stamp-based KV-cache sampler ("kv-stamp profiler"), and an adaptive batch-window tracer that records contiguous request grouping behavior used by the continuous-batching scheduler. All probes are zero-cost when disabled and are guarded behind runtime flags and RBAC-controlled debug endpoints.

Motivation and context: We observed tail-latency regressions under mixed request shapes where a few large layers dominated GPU occupancy and caused scheduler-induced head-of-line blocking. Existing coarse counters were insufficient to map slowdowns to individual kernel launches or to correlate KV-cache reuse with batch-window size. This work aims to give engineers the tools to: (1) attribute occupancy regressions to specific model layers/kernels, (2) measure the effective batch-window (number of requests grouped per dispatch) over time, and (3) correlate KV-cache hit patterns with per-layer hot paths.

What changed (high level):
- runtime/probes/occupancy_probe.h/.cc: per-layer occupancy probe that hooks into kernel dispatch points and records occupancy (SM utilization estimate) + launch duration. Exports a compact proto-friendly record for tracing.
- runtime/probes/kv_stamp_profiler.h/.cc: stamp-based KV-cache sampler that attaches a short-lived token to requests and samples cache hits/misses across layers to compute reuse windows.
- runtime/scheduler/batch_window_tracer.cc: adaptive tracer that logs when the scheduler decides to flush a batch, capturing batch composition, sequence lengths, and KV stamps.
- tools/microbench/occupancy_benchdriver: small microbench harness to replay recorded traces at configurable QPS and report distribution of occupancy and batch-window sizes.
- observability/dashboards/occupancy-dashboard.json: Grafana panel definitions and Query Language (PromQL/SQL) mappings to visualize per-layer occupancy heatmap and KV reuse curves.
- controlplane: added debug endpoint /debug/probes to enable/disable probes and stream sampler snapshots (RBAC-protected).

Commits (summary):
- 9f3d2a8: add occupancy probe skeleton and proto definitions
- 1b4a7c2: implement kernel hookpoints and SM-util estimators
- 6c8ef91: add KV-stamp sampling and tag propagation through request context
- d4a11b3: integrate batch-window tracer into continuous batching scheduler
- aebb2f0: microbench harness + runner + synthetic workload generator
- be3c442: dashboards and CI runbook instructions
- c7f0d9a: tests, docs, and RBAC debug endpoint

Files changed (select highlights):
- runtime/probes/occupancy_probe.h (new)
- runtime/probes/occupancy_probe.cc (new)
- runtime/probes/kv_stamp_profiler.h (new)
- runtime/probes/kv_stamp_profiler.cc (new)
- runtime/scheduler/batch_window_tracer.cc (modified)
- tools/microbench/occupancy_benchdriver/main.cc (new)
- observability/dashboards/occupancy-dashboard.json (new)
- controlplane/http/debug_handlers.cc (modified)
- docs/observability/occupancy.md (new)

Testing and results: Unit and integration tests were added for probe lifecycle and stamp propagation. Microbench harness reproduces a regression scenario: mixed traffic with occasional long sequences. Before instrumentation + adaptive tracer, under synthetic 500 qps workload with 5% long requests we observed 99th latency = 312 ms and visible spike in SM contention. After adapting scheduler thresholds (guided by the tracer insights) and enabling KV-stamp-aware coalescing, end-to-end 99th latency dropped to 245 ms (-21%), and average per-layer hotpath occupancy variance reduced by ~34% in the bench runs. Note: these numbers are from the microbench harness on a p4d instance; production effects would vary.

Backward compatibility and safety: All probes are opt-in via the /debug/probes endpoint and a runtime config flag (runtime.probes.enabled=false by default). The probe code avoids allocations on the hot path by using thread-local ring buffers and a lock-free aggregator. The control plane enforces that only users with the observability:admin role can toggle sampling.

Review conversation (selected excerpts):
- Jordan Lee (reviewer): "Nice work — this gives the visibility we needed. A couple of asks: (1) ensure SM-estimator doesn't allocate on every kernel path, (2) add a unit test for stamp propagation across async cuda streams."
- Maya Patel (author): "Addressed both: estimator uses a precomputed LUT and TLS, added stream-propagation test in runtime/probes/tests/kv_stamp_test.cc."
- Priya Natarajan (reviewer): "Grafana panels look good, but let's avoid adding expensive dashboard queries to the default ingest. Recommend materializing a short-term rollup table in the observability pipeline."
- Maya Patel: "Agreed — changed dashboard to use pre-aggregated metrics (probe_occupancy_bucket) and added guidance in docs/observability/occupancy.md."
- Luis Gomez (reviewer & approver): "Approving after small nits. Please document RBAC access in the ops runbook."
- Ops comment (internal): "Temporarily disable probes in canary until we've validated throughput impact at production load."

CI summary: Initial CI run reported flaky unit on runtime/scheduler tests (timing-dependent) which was fixed by introducing a deterministic clock in the test harness. Full CI (build, unit, integration, lint) all passed on final run. Perf check (microbench pipeline) executed on the perf-lab pool; results above included.

Follow-ups and TODOs:
- ENG-3922: Add continuous integration job to run microbench harness nightly against representative instance types.
- ENG-3938: Add end-to-end alerting that surfaces sudden increases in per-layer occupancy variance.
- Investigate tighter kernel-level hooks for future reduction of estimator error under model parallelism.

Merge: Squash-merged into main on 2026-03-05 after approvals. Change is feature-flagged and shipped behind RBAC and runtime flag to minimize risk. The dashboards and docs are included in the same PR to make rollout and troubleshooting immediate.

Related work: complements earlier occupancy counters and tracing exports but focuses on correlating occupancy with KV-cache reuse and continuous-batching behavior. Distinct from prior NVTX/time-trace changes by providing a low-overhead sampled profiler and an adaptive tracer targeted at scheduler decision points.

1b4a7c2: implement kernel hookpoints and SM-util estimators
6c8ef91: add KV-stamp sampling and tag propagation
d4a11b3: integrate batch-window tracer into continuous batching scheduler
aebb2f0: microbench harness + runner
be3c442: dashboards and CI runbook instructions
c7f0d9a: tests, docs, and RBAC debug endpoint

source.title: add-inference-health-probe-and-sampled-kernel-queue-explorer
source.author: Priya Nandakumar
source.description: Adds a lightweight inference health probe and a sampled kernel-queue explorer to improve incident triage and long-tail cost attribution. Motivation: recurring short-lived kernel queue stalls were noisy in SLO pages and made it hard to trace whether slow tails were caused by model-level token spikes, kernel contention, or tenant-induced backpressure. This PR introduces: 1) inference_health_probe: periodic synthetic pings that exercise a small, deterministic token path and emit a health metric (inference_probe_latency_ms) plus an opaque probe_id for trace linking; 2) sampled_kernel_queue_explorer: a new trace sampler that, when a probe or real invocation exceeds a kernel-queue latency threshold, captures a compact kernel-queue snapshot and stores a compacted span with queue_depth, head_kernel_id, and estimated_token_cost_hint; 3) dashboard panels: a compact 'Probe Pulse' timeseries, kernel-queue heatmap (bucketed by queue depth and model shard), and a 'Probe vs Real' latency scatter for quick triage; 4) runbook/playbook: an Observability Surge Playbook page with automated escalation steps and quick links to tenant-level cost and KV cache hit panels. Implementation notes: probe logic is opt-in per deployment via config.inference.probe.enabled (false by default) and probes are low-rate by design (0.1 rps per region) to avoid billing noise; the sampled kernel snapshots are stored in the observability compact store with a capped reservoir to limit cardinality. Backwards compatibility: no changes to public API surfaces. Checklist: [x] add probe runtime hooks, [x] add metric and span schema, [x] add dashboard panels, [x] add playbook and runbook links, [x] update docs and config reference, [x] add unit tests and e2e sampler smoke test. Related: ENG-6123 for incident RCA and OBS-47 for dashboard request. Expected rollout: feature gated for 2 weeks, enable for staging then incrementally to prod with guardrail on stored snapshot volume.
commits: a1b2c3d: add probe framework and config flags
d4e5f6a: implement sampled kernel-queue snapshotter and compact span schema
f7a8b9c: add dashboard panels and dashboard JSON export
c1d2e3f: add playbook markdown and runbook links
b4c5d6e: tests: add unit tests for probe and sampler; e2e smoke test
review_comments: Ethan Park (reviewer): Can we avoid writing probe IDs to logs in plaintext? Suggest redacting before emit.
Priya Nandakumar (author): Good catch — switched to hash(probe_id) and updated telemetry docs to note non-identifying hash. Commits updated.
Marta Alvarez (reviewer): Dashboard looks good; add a per-model-shard breakdown and ensure color scale is log-friendly.
Priya Nandakumar (author): Added model_shard split and log-color scale; included in dashboard JSON commit.
Samir Khan (reviewer): Request: add a throttle to sampled snapshot writer to prevent hot tenants from saturating the compact store. Also add unit test to simulate burst.
Priya Nandakumar (author): Implemented reservoir cap and burst throttle; added tests. CI flagged a flake in sampler_e2e test on linux-arm, reran.
Marta Alvarez (approver): LGTM after CI green. Approving merge.
ci_checks: integration-sampler-smoke: pass (flaky on first run, rerun succeeded)
source.release_notes: Added optional inference health probe and kernel-queue sampling to improve triage. Includes dashboard panels and an Observability Surge Playbook. Opt-in via config.inference.probe.enabled.

source.title: Graceful disconnect: buffered tool-call retry and reconnect window for streaming API
source.author: Aisha Khan
source.description: Motivation: We observed customer incidents where short-lived client disconnects during multi-step function/tool-calling flows caused duplicate tool invocations or orphaned streaming sessions, leading to higher costs and inconsistent structured outputs. This PR introduces a server-side in-flight buffer and a short reconnect window with idempotency reconciliation to preserve end-to-end semantics while keeping latency and cost in check. Summary of changes: 1) Session manager: add a buffered-inflight queue that retains in-progress tool invocations for a configurable reconnect window (default 20s). Sessions now emit a compact session token (reconnect_token) on streaming start. 2) Tool invocation idempotency: introduce X-Tool-Invocation-Id header and runtime-side idempotency table so duplicate replays are deduplicated; tools should return idempotency-safe responses. 3) Reconnect/reconcile flow: upon client reconnect with reconnect_token, server attempts to reconcile buffered inflight work with the client's last-ack position, returning any accumulated partial structured output and the current tool invocation states. If the client reconnects after the window, buffered items are aborted with graceful teardown events. 4) Soft-cancel semantics: new soft-cancel signal allows clients to request an in-flight tool call be aborted only if it has not reached an irreversible side-effect point; otherwise the server completes the call and returns final structured output. 5) SDK/compat: TypeScript and Python SDKs added helper APIs to expose reconnect_token and soft-cancel semantics while preserving OpenAI-compatible streaming events (partial chunks, function_call start/finish events). 6) Observability: new metrics (session.reconnect_attempts, tool.invocation.dedup_hits, session.buffered_items) and traces to aid debugging. 7) Tests: unit tests for reconcile/timeout logic, integration tests simulating flaky client disconnects, and an e2e test that ensures no duplicate tool executions for retries. Checklist: - [x] API design reviewed for OpenAI compatibility - [x] Runtime changes gated behind feature flag streaming_reconnect_v2 - [x] SDK helpers implemented and docstrings added - [x] Metrics and tracing added - [x] Tests included and green on CI Design notes: reconnect_token is short-lived (default TTL 20s) to avoid long-lived server buffering. The buffered queue is size-capped per-session and uses LRU eviction for low-memory impact; overflow causes immediate abort with structured error to client. Soft-cancel is advisory to the runtime and will translate to an AbortableContext inside tool runners; tools can opt-in to fast abort or honor idempotency markers and complete. Compatibility: OpenAI-compatible streaming events are preserved: the server emits a function_call.start event when a tool is invoked, intermittent partial function_call.content chunks during streaming, and function_call.finish when completed; on reconnect the server will stream any missed chunks and then resume new output. CI notes: initial run flagged a flaky e2e test that relied on timing; test was stabilized by switching to event-based waits and re-run. Merge: squash-merged to main on 2026-03-03 after approvals. Impact: reduces duplicate tool calls in flaky network scenarios, gives SDKs a clear reconnect primitive, and improves observability for streaming interruptions. Runtime defaults keep memory/cost bounded and the feature is controlled by feature flags for gradual rollout.
commit_summaries: session: add reconnect token + buffered in-flight queue and TTL logic
runtime: implement tool invocation idempotency table and X-Tool-Invocation-Id header handling
sdk-ts: expose reconnect token and softCancel() helper on StreamingClient
sdk-py: add StreamingClient.reconnect(token) convenience and tests
observability: emit session.reconnect_attempts and tool.invocation.dedup_hits
tests: integration + e2e for reconnect and de-dupe scenarios
docs: streaming reconnect API changes and migration notes
feature-flag: gating streaming_reconnect_v2 behind rollout flag
minor: linting and flake fixes
review_comments: Martin Li: Can we make the reconnect TTL configurable per-deployment? For high-throughput customers 20s may be too short.
Aisha Khan (author): TTL is configurable at deployment level via streaming_reconnect_ttl in the config; default 20s for hosted to limit memory. Added note in docs.
Sofia Ramirez: Please add more asserts to the e2e test that verify no duplicate external tool calls are made (mock the tool endpoint).
Aisha Khan (author): Added tool endpoint mock and assert in e2e. Also added metric that counts dedup hits to make it easier in prod.
Ben Carter: I worry about the LRU eviction semantics leaking user-visible errors in the middle of a flow. Can we emit an explicit structured error event before discarding?
Aisha Khan (author): Good point — we now emit session.buffer_overflow error event with structured error code and include a best-effort partial output snapshot.
source.release_notes: Introduce a reconnect token and buffered in-flight queue to make streaming sessions resilient to short client disconnects; add tool invocation idempotency markers and SDK helpers for reconnect and soft-cancel behavior. Feature gated behind streaming_reconnect_v2.

title: Graceful disconnect: buffered tool-call retry and reconnect window for streaming API

This PR introduces a server-side in-flight buffer and a short reconnect window with idempotency reconciliation to preserve end-to-end semantics while keeping latency and cost in check. Summary of changes: 1) Session manager: add a buffered-inflight queue that retains in-progress tool invocations for a configurable reconnect window (default 20s).
