source.title: Topology-aware peer balancing, async kernel dispatch, and KV-cache coalescing
source.author: Lina Ortega
source.release_notes: Improves multi-GPU tensor-parallel throughput and tail latency by introducing topology-aware peer selection, dynamic peer balancing, asynchronous kernel dispatch with stream priorities, and KV-cache request coalescing. No API changes. Rollout gated behind feature flag 'tp_topo_pb'.
source.description: Motivation: Multi-GPU tensor-parallel workloads suffer from suboptimal collective placement and small-KV churn when requests are micro-batched across heterogeneous racks. This PR implements three coordinated runtime optimizations to improve throughput and tail latency: (1) topology-aware peer balancing: build NVLink/NIC-aware ring/graph membership using a lightweight port-speed probe so collectives prefer intra-node/NVLink lanes and minimize cross-node hops, and dynamically rebalance peer pairs when per-step bandwidth estimates indicate hotlinks; (2) async kernel dispatch: move per-layer kernel launch to a dispatch thread pair that annotates CUDA streams with priority and orders kernels for overlap with NCCL comms, and add a non-blocking enqueue-locality check so compute work can start as soon as required dependencies are queued; (3) KV-cache coalescing at the request router: group concurrent small KV reads into coalesced bulk fetches and fold redundant cache population work to reduce memory traffic and small-launch overheads. Design notes: peer selection runs at model load and is re-evaluated when topology events are seen (PCIe/NVLink health changes, new GPUs added). Peer balancing uses a lightweight EWMA of measured pairwise bandwidths to pick an alternating pairing sequence for each micro-batch window. Async dispatch uses two prioritized CUDA streams per device (compute_high, compute_low) plus a comm stream; we rely on CUDA stream priorities instead of artificial host sleeps which reduces scheduling jitter. KV coalescer is conservatively enabled behind the feature flag tp_topo_pb and has a max-coalesce window (configurable) to avoid added latency for single-request critical paths. Implementation summary: runtime/scheduler: add TopologyProbe and PeerBalancer modules, extend DeviceResources to expose stream priorities; runtime/comm: new TopoRingBuilder that prefers NVLink edges, updates NCCL unique IDs to include pairing hints; runtime/dispatch: KernelDispatcher refactor, introduce DispatchFence for dependency tracking and switch to cudaLaunchKernelAsync for fused kernels; router/kv_cache: RequestCoalescer layer with in-process batching and de-duplication. Compatibility and safety: fallbacks to previous ring builder when topology probe fails or when running on unsupported drivers; coalescer has a conservative timeout and a per-model opt-out. Tests and validation: microbenchmarks using benchmark-lab show 18-25% throughput improvement on 8xA100 NVLink topology for half-precision decode workloads with 2048 seq; 95th percentile tail latency reduced by ~14-20% for 8k token streams when KV coalescing is enabled. Perf canary across 3 dedicated clusters showed stable error rates and no observable degradation in output quality. Rollout plan: feature-flagged in main, staged canary to dedicated-qa, then incremental ramp to production Dedicated pools over 72 hours with monitoring on NCCL error counters, GPU PCIe errors, end-to-end latency SLOs. Observability: emit metrics peer_balance/pair_changes, topo_probe/latency_ms, kvcoalesce/coalesce_rate, dispatcher/stream_stall_ms, and add tracing spans for coalescer windows. Documentation: updated runtime/README and redwood-docs/perf for recommended config knobs. CI and perf runs: included unit tests for peer balancer invariants and integration tests in perf-harness. This change is primarily internal runtime improvements; no user API changes expected.
commits: c1a8b9f  Add TopologyProbe and baseline unit tests
d4f3e12  Implement TopoRingBuilder and NVLink preference logic
e98fa2b  Refactor KernelDispatcher -> DispatchFence, add async launch
a07b3c1  Add RequestCoalescer and router integration
b5d2e7f  Tests: microbench harness, perf scripts, canary configs
f2c9a0d  Fix: fall back when topology probe unsupported; add feature flag
review_comments: Ethan Park: Can you describe failure modes when NVLink bandwidth is asymmetric? Recommended adding an EWMA and a cooldown window.
Lina Ortega: Responded that EWMA already in PeerBalancer and added cooldown; included comment in code and unit tests to validate cooldown behavior.
Priya Nair: Suggests adding a config toggle to limit coalesce window for low-latency flows; asked for a perf run showing single-request latency impact.
Lina Ortega: Added per-model opt-out and microbench that shows median single-request latency increase <1ms with coalesce window set to 2ms.
Lucas Meyer: Code review: requestCoalescer locking looks fine but suggest avoiding blocking on IO thread; recommended converting the final flush to a thread-pool callback.
Lina Ortega: Implemented thread-pool flush and re-ran CI; updated tests.
source.merge_commit_message: squash: topology-aware peer balancing + async kernel dispatch + KV coalescer - improves TP throughput and tail latency
source.notes: Hotfix/backout plan: feature flag allows immediate rollback; also added a health-check that will auto-disable peer balancing if NCCL errors exceed threshold. Monitoring dashboard created in observability-pack for rapid rollback.

source.title: Smoother autoscaler signal and gateway backpressure for dedicated pools
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.description:
Summary: Introduces a smoothing layer in the Dedicated Autoscaler and a lightweight backpressure signal propagated from the gateway to fleet controllers. This reduces autoscaler thrash on spiky traffic and gives gateways a controlled way to shed load gracefully against per-pool soft capacity and throughput guarantees.

Motivation: Customers on Dedicated have reported oscillations when short bursts cross autoscaler thresholds and then recede; this leads to unnecessary GPU spin-ups and billing variance, as well as transient error spikes. The gateway currently responds with 429s or fallback heuristics; we need a first-class, observable mechanism that cooperates with autoscaling and preserves headroom for SLA-critical requests.

Changes in this PR:
- Autoscaler: add Exponential Moving Average (EMA) smoothing of utilization and request rate with configurable half-life per-pool. New config fields: smoothing_window_ms, smoothing_alpha (derived).
- Soft capacity & grace tokens: each pool now tracks a small ephemeral token bucket (grace_tokens) to allow short bursts without triggering scale-up, decaying over time and replenished by underutilization.
- Gateway backpressure: add a new X-Redwood-Backpressure header and a compact gRPC/backchannel (gateway->controller) event to advertise desired ingress rate reductions per-route when pool smoothed_utilization > threshold.
- Controller/gateway handshake: gateway subscribes to per-pool backpressure_quota updates; controllers include backpressure_rate in scheduling decisions and expose metrics.
- Telemetry: new metrics smoothed_utilization, grace_tokens_available, backpressure_rate, backpressure_rejections. Add Prometheus labels for pool_id, model_id, and region.
- API schema: extend dedicated pool spec with smoothing and backpressure knobs; added validation and defaults.
- Console/docs: admin UI now surfaces smoothing window and current backpressure state with guidance.
- Tests: unit tests for smoothing math, integration e2e that injects burst traffic and asserts reduced scale-up churn and controlled 429 behavior.

Checklist:
- [x] Design review with SRE and Runtime teams (ENG-2147)
- [x] Unit tests for smoothing and token bucket
- [x] Integration e2e simulating bursty traffic
- [x] Telemetry dashboards updated
- [x] Migration notes and rollout plan
- [x] Backwards-compatible API schema changes (defaults safe)

Implementation notes:
- EMA parameters chosen to have ~30s half-life by default; pools serving low-latency workloads can set shorter windows.
- Grace tokens default to 2 tokens per pool; a token represents ~1s worth of headroom at pool's nominal throughput.
- Backpressure is advisory: scheduler will attempt to respect backpressure_rate before rejecting; only when backpressure persists will the gateway return 429s.
- To avoid unsafe throttling loops, backpressure updates include a monotonic seq and TTL.

Breaking changes: none expected; all new fields are optional with safe defaults.

Rollout plan: staged rollout: 1) feature gated off by default; 2) enable on Canary Dedicated clusters for 48h; 3) enable for opt-in customers with console toggle; 4) enable by default after 2 weeks of stable metrics.

Migration: API customers can ignore new fields; operators upgrading on-prem should ensure controllers and gateway are updated together.

Testing:
- Unit and integration tests added under tests/e2e/dedicated_smoothing_test.go
- Load test harness reproduces 1-2 minute burst profiles and asserts scale-up events reduced by >=50% while 95th-p99 latency impact <5% in our baseline.

Commits (high level):
- 0f9a8b2: controller: add smoothing math and metrics
- 1b3d2c4: gateway: backpressure header + backchannel client
- 8a7f6e1: api: add dedicated pool schema changes + validation
- c3e4d5b: telemetry: register new metrics and dashboards
- d4f2a9f: console: show per-pool smoothing/backpressure state
- f5b6c7d: tests: unit + e2e harness for burst scenarios

Files changed (high level):
- controller/autoscaler/smoothing.go: implement EMA and smoothing helpers (approx. 220 LOC)
- pkg/allocator/soft_capacity.go: grace tokens and soft capacity decisions
- gateway/backpressure/handler.go: header parsing and backchannel emitter
- api/config/v1/dedicated_pool.yaml: schema additions and docs
- telemetry/metrics_registry.go: new metric registration
- console/src/pages/pool/BackpressureCard.tsx: UI surface
- tests/e2e/dedicated_smoothing_test.go: load test harness
- helm/charts/redwood/templates/pool-config.yaml: default knobs
- ops/rollout/2026-03-dedicated-smoothing.md: rollout instructions

Small illustrative snippets:
- smoothing.go (EMA core): "smoothed = alpha*instant + (1-alpha)*smoothed"
- handler.go (gateway header): "if hdr := req.Header.Get(\"X-Redwood-Backpressure\"); hdr != \"\" { parseAndApply(hdr) }"

CI and checks:
- CI: go build, unit tests, linters (golangci), frontend unit tests
- e2e pipeline run on canary (k8s)
- Initial CI run: failed flakiness in e2e; reran and passed on second attempt.

Review thread summary:
- Marco Alvarez (SRE): requested clearer backpressure-to-scheduler contract and TTL handling. Response: added monotonic seq + TTL and explicit scheduler fallback behavior (controller will not reduce scheduling below 50% of nominal unless pool explicitly marked best-effort).
- Leah Kim (Runtime): asked for benchmark numbers for default EMA half-life; Response: added results from synthetic bursts showing 60% reduction in scale-ups with 30s half-life.
- Owen Patel (Security): requested audit logging for backpressure events; Response: added structured audit logs under telemetry/backpressure_events.
- Reviewer approvals: Marco Alvarez approved after TTL addition; Leah Kim approved after benchmark numbers; Owen Patel approved after audit logs.

Merge and post-merge actions:
- Merge method: squash and merge
- Post-merge: created a follow-up issue to add gauntlet tests against real customer workloads (ENG-2210)
- Updated canary dashboards and set alert: DedicatedPool.SmoothedUtilization > 95% for 5m -> page oncall

Risks and mitigations:
- Risk: misconfigured smoothing window could delay needed scale-ups -> mitigation: default conservative half-life and admin override to disable smoothing per pool.
- Risk: backpressure loops between gateway and controller -> mitigation: monotonic seq + TTL + min-scheduling floor.

Release notes: "Improves autoscaler stability by smoothing utilization signals and introduces an advisory backpressure mechanism from gateway to controllers to reduce churn on short bursts. Operators: feature is opt-in and has safe defaults; see admin docs for tuning guidance."

CI status: pass

1b3d2c4 gateway: backpressure header + backchannel client
8a7f6e1 api: add dedicated pool schema changes + validation
c3e4d5b telemetry: register new metrics and dashboards
d4f2a9f console: show per-pool smoothing/backpressure state
f5b6c7d tests: unit + e2e harness for burst scenarios

- Grace tokens default to 2 tokens per pool; a token represents ~1s worth of headroom at pool's nominal throughput.
- Backpressure is advisory: scheduler will attempt to respect backpressure_rate before rejecting; only when backpressure persists will the gateway return 429s.
- To avoid unsafe throttling loops, backpressure updates include a monotonic seq and TTL.

Breaking changes: none expected; all new fields are optional with safe defaults.

Rollout plan: staged rollout: 1) feature gated off by default; 2) enable on Canary Dedicated clusters for 48h; 3) enable for opt-in customers with console toggle; 4) enable by default after 2 weeks of stable metrics.

Migration: API customers can ignore new fields; operators upgrading on-prem should ensure controllers and gateway are updated together.

- Load test harness reproduces 1-2 minute burst profiles and asserts scale-up events reduced by >=50% while 95th-p99 latency impact <5% in our baseline.

text:
Summary: Introduces a smoothing layer in the Dedicated Autoscaler and a lightweight backpressure signal propagated from the gateway to fleet controllers. This reduces autoscaler thrash on spiky traffic and gives gateways a controlled way to shed load gracefully against per-pool soft capacity and throughput guarantees.

Motivation: Customers on Dedicated have reported oscillations when short bursts cross autoscaler thresholds and then recede; this leads to unnecessary GPU spin-ups and billing variance, as well as transient error spikes. The gateway currently responds with 429s or fallback heuristics; we need a first-class, observable mechanism that cooperates with autoscaling and preserves headroom for SLA-critical requests.

Changes in this PR:
- Autoscaler: add Exponential Moving Average (EMA) smoothing of utilization and request rate with configurable half-life per-pool. New config fields: smoothing_window_ms, smoothing_alpha (derived).
- Soft capacity & grace tokens: each pool now tracks a small ephemeral token bucket (grace_tokens) to allow short bursts without triggering scale-up, decaying over time and replenished by underutilization.
- Gateway backpressure: add a new X-Redwood-Backpressure header and a compact gRPC/backchannel (gateway->controller) event to advertise desired ingress rate reductions per-route when pool smoothed_utilization > threshold.
- Controller/gateway handshake: gateway subscribes to per-pool backpressure_quota updates; controllers include backpressure_rate in scheduling decisions and expose metrics.
- Telemetry: new metrics smoothed_utilization, grace_tokens_available, backpressure_rate, backpressure_rejections. Add Prometheus labels for pool_id, model_id, and region.
- API schema: extend dedicated pool spec with smoothing and backpressure knobs; added validation and defaults.
- Console/docs: admin UI now surfaces smoothing window and current backpressure state with guidance.
- Tests: unit tests for smoothing math, integration e2e that injects burst traffic and asserts reduced scale-up churn and controlled 429 behavior.

- [x] Design review with SRE and Runtime teams (ENG-2147)
- [x] Unit tests for smoothing and token bucket
- [x] Integration e2e simulating bursty traffic
- [x] Telemetry dashboards updated
- [x] Migration notes and rollout plan
- [x] Backwards-compatible API schema changes (defaults safe)

source.title: Resumable streaming: disconnect quarantine, server timeouts, and function-call idempotency
source.author: Ava Chen
source.release_notes: Adds server-side reconnect quarantine window (default 20s) and configurable server-enforced soft-cancel timeouts; improves resume semantics for function/tool calls so partial tool results are preserved and retried idempotently; public SDKs updated to surface resume token behavior and retry guidance.
source.body:
Context and motivation: Production customers hit a class of errors where brief client disconnects (mobile network flaps, browser tab swaps) caused long-running streaming requests that invoked tools to either get duplicated or be lost after client reconnect. Existing resume tokens preserved raw streaming offsets but did not maintain function-call intent or partial outputs, making resuming tool invocations unsafe. This PR introduces a small set of changes to make streaming resumes predictable and safe for function/tool calls and structured output in the OpenAI compatibility layer.

What this change does (single continuous description): Adds a server-side "reconnect quarantine" (default 20s) during which a session can reconnect and claim in-flight stream state; introduces a server-enforced soft-cancel TTL for tool/function calls that allows the runtime to decide whether to allow the tool to finish or abort early when a client disconnects; augments resume tokens with a per-invocation idempotency key and partial-result checkpoint so a resumed session can continue or safely re-run a tool without duplicating side effects; updates the OpenAI-compat adapter to carry function_call metadata across resume and to map resumed partial payloads into the structured output contract; updates JS/Python SDKs to surface resume semantics, honor reconnect hints, and provide explicit opt-out flags for aggressive hard-cancel; adds end-to-end harness tests that simulate flaky disconnects and targeted regression coverage for previously observed duplicate-tool invocation failures.

Checklist: - [x] Design doc and linear: ENG-3881 - [x] Unit + integration tests - [x] E2E reconnect harness with simulated network flakiness - [x] Backwards-compatible adapter changes with feature flag (compat.resume_v2) - [x] Docs and SDK changelog entries - [x] Perf regression bench run (no regression in p50 latency observed)

Commits (high-level): - "session: introduce reconnect_quarantine manager with TTL and reclaim logic" - "runtime: soft-cancel policy and invocation idempotency keys" - "openai_adapter: preserve function_call and partial_args across resume" - "sdk-js: expose resumeToken behavior & reconnectHints to clients" - "sdk-py: resume client + test harness" - "tests: e2e flaky disconnect harness and regression cases" - "docs: streaming resume semantics and migration guidance"

Changed areas (high level): service/streaming/session_manager.go - new reconnect quarantine manager; runtime/invocation/soft_cancel.go - soft-cancel/safe-retry logic; adapters/openai/compat.go - carry function_call metadata and partial-result checkpointing; sdk/js/streaming.ts - resumeToken handling, reconnect hints; sdk/python/stream_client.py - same for python SDK; tests/e2e/stream_reconnect_spec.py - new harness; docs/api/streaming_resume.md - documentation and migration notes; console/observability/alerts.yaml - tuned alert thresholds for streaming disconnect spikes.

Review conversation (highlights): - Priya: "Can you clarify how the idempotency key is generated and where it is stored? If it's ephemeral only in memory we risk losing it on a node failover." 
Author (Ava): "Good call — idempotency key is now stored in the session metadata (persisted to our session store with eventual expiration aligned to the quarantine TTL). Updated commit adds migration to ensure older sessions get a generated key on first touch."
- Lucas: "The soft-cancel branch looks reasonable, but we need a clear operator knob to lower the quarantine in high-load scenarios to avoid resource hoarding." 
Author: "Added server config 'stream.reconnect_quarantine_ms' and admit policy to the Dedicated config; also threw in metrics (stream.quarantine_claims, stream.quarantine_evictions) for SLO tracing."
- Zoe: "SDK behaviour: when a client receives a resume token and then immediately reconnects, do we double-ack function calls? We should document recommended client-side backoff." 
Author: "Added SDK helpers and docs recommending an exponential jittered reconnect up to the quarantine window; SDK also exposes 'resumeMode' with explicit 'resume' | 'restart' choices."

CI & testing summary: - CI pipeline initially failed one flaky e2e test (tests/e2e/stream_reconnect_spec.py::test_multiple_disconnects) on first run; root cause was non-deterministic test harness timing. Adjusted harness timing and reran. - All unit tests: pass - Integration: pass - E2E (re-run): pass - Benchmarks: p50 latency unchanged, p95 +3% under pathological reconnect storm (acceptable; documented and configuration knob added).

Migration & backward compatibility: The OpenAI adapter changes are behind a feature flag (compat.resume_v2). Existing sessions continue to use the legacy resume behavior until customers opt-in or until we flip the default after the deprecation window. SDKs will default to presenting the new behavior but will not force servers to adopt it.

Operational notes: Added two new metrics and one alert: stream.quarantine_claims (counter), stream.quarantine_evictions (counter), alert "HighStreamingQuarantineUtilization" (fires when >80% of streaming pool has claimed quarantine slots for >5min). Documentation includes mitigation (reduce quarantine_ms, increase pool capacity, or enable fast-hard-cancel).

Merge notes: squash-merged after approvals; no migration scripts required for customer data beyond session metadata backfill which is handled lazily on first resume touch.

If you want to exercise the new flow locally: see docs/api/streaming_resume.md and the e2e harness under tests/e2e/ for guidance on toggling the feature flag and simulating reconnects.
commit_summaries: session: add reconnect_quarantine manager and reclaim API (ttl-based)
runtime: implement soft_cancel policy and invocation idempotency keys
openai_adapter: carry function_call/partial_result across resume tokens
sdk-js: expose resumeMode, reconnectHints, and resumeToken helpers
sdk-py: resume client, tests, and docs
tests: e2e flaky disconnect harness and focused regression tests
docs: streaming resume semantics + operator guidance
review_comments: Priya Nair: Can you clarify how the idempotency key is generated and where it is stored? If it's ephemeral only in memory we risk losing it on a node failover.
Ava Chen: Added persistence to session store with TTL aligned to quarantine window; updated migration and tests.
Lucas Meyer: Need operator knob to lower quarantine in high load to avoid resource hoarding.
Ava Chen: Added config 'stream.reconnect_quarantine_ms' and metrics: stream.quarantine_claims/evictions.
Zoe Patel: SDK behaviour edge case: potential double-ack on immediate reconnect; recommend client-side backoff.
Ava Chen: Added SDK helper for jittered reconnect and explicit resumeMode to avoid accidental double-runs.
ci_checks: bench: p50 unchanged, p95 +3% under storm
merge_details: Merged by Lucas Meyer via squash on 2026-03-02
Approvals: Priya Nair, Lucas Meyer, Zoe Patel
Merge commit message: "Resumable streaming: disconnect quarantine + soft-cancel and idempotent function-calls"

source.title: Introduce Toolcall Conductor with Async Signal Latching and OpenAI-compatible Result Hydration
source.author: Maya Patel
source.description: Context: Customers increasingly run multi-tool workflows where the model issues parallel function/tool calls and expects deterministic result composition, streaming stability, and graceful retries. Motivation: previous ad-hoc orchestration led to gibberish structured outputs, duplicate side-effects when retries happened, and brittle OpenAI-compat behavior for function-calling sequences. This PR introduces a new lightweight Toolcall Conductor that centralizes execution ergonomics: async signal latching to coordinate parallel tool runs, idempotent result hydration to prevent duplicate injections, and an OpenAI-compat shim for function-calling semantics in streamed responses. Summary of changes: 1) runtime: add redwood/pkg/runtime/toolcall/conductor.go implementing a conductor that tracks call IDs, latches result signals, and emits ordered hydration events to the streaming layer. 2) api: update redwood/pkg/api/openai_compat/handlers.go to translate OpenAI-style function_call choices into conductor jobs and to consume conductor hydration events for stable streamed structured output. 3) sdk: small ergonomics helpers in redwood-sdk-python/redwood/tooling.py and redwood-sdk-typescript/src/tooling.ts to expose a non-blocking call API that wires into the conductor. 4) client compat tests and e2e flows in benchmark-lab/test/toolcall_conductor_test.go and redwood-examples/multi-tool-orchestration/ to demonstrate retry-safe replay. 5) docs: new section in redwood-docs/reference/tool-calling.md describing the conductor lifecycle and recommended retry semantics. Key behavior & guarantees: - Async Signal Latching: parallel tool executions post a transient signal with a stable ID; the conductor latches the first successful result per signal ID and deduplicates subsequent completions. - Idempotent Result Hydration: hydration objects are stamped with stable request-call identifiers and replay-safe tokens so retries or replays will not cause duplicate injections or side-effects. - Streaming Stability: the OpenAI-compat shim buffers partial tool outputs and emits structured function_call + response blocks in the same ordering across initial stream and any reconnects. - Retry policy: default hedged-retry policy with configurable window; retries for non-idempotent tools are disabled by default and require opt-in via tool metadata. Migration notes: minor SDK change for tool registration signature (opt-in param "idempotent": true/false) and server-side default policy; existing tool integrations continue to work but will not get automatic retries until annotated. Backward compatibility: OpenAI-compat endpoints remain backwards-compatible for single-tool calls; multi-tool streaming semantics improved but explicit opt-in required in some edge cases. Checklist: - [x] Implement conductor core and unit tests - [x] Integrate with OpenAI-compat handler - [x] Add SDK ergonomics helpers and examples - [x] Add regression tests for retries and replay - [x] Update docs and migration guide Testing: unit tests for latch/dedup logic, integration tests covering streaming reconnection scenarios, manual QA on Dedicated and Hosted with synthetic multi-tool workloads, load tests in perf pipeline to validate latency impact. review_comments: Jin Park: Nice clear separation between latching and hydration. Can we add a short-circuit single-tool fast path in the conductor to avoid locking on the common case?
Maya Patel: Added fast-path in conductor.go; benchmark shows <2% p99 impact for single-tool flows.
Luis Gomez: Suggest exposing idempotent flag in TS SDK as named option rather than positional arg for ergonomics.
Maya Patel: Updated TS SDK surface and added migration note in docs.

Maya Patel: Added concurrent-identical-signal test and asserted single hydration event.

source.release_notes: Introduce Toolcall Conductor: centralized orchestration for multi-tool calls that provides async signal latching, idempotent result hydration, and improved OpenAI-compatible streaming for multi-tool workflows. SDKs add optional idempotent flag. Default policies disable retries for non-idempotent tools. See migration guide in docs.
source.testing: Unit tests cover latch/dedupe/retry policy. Integration tests simulate streaming reconnects and parallel tool invocations. Load tests validated latency impact under synthetic fan-out.
source.notes: This is intended as an incremental rollout; feature gated behind runtime flag and opt-in per-tenant configuration. Follow-up PRs will add console metrics and finer-grained routing by cost tier.

title: Introduce Toolcall Conductor with Async Signal Latching and OpenAI-compatible Result Hydration
preview: Context: Customers increasingly run multi-tool workflows where the model issues parallel function/tool calls and expects deterministic result composition, streaming stability, and graceful retries. Motivation: previous ad-hoc orchestration led to gibberish structured outputs, duplicate side-effects when retries happened, and brittle OpenAI-compat behavior for function-calling sequences. This PR introduces a new lightweight Toolcall Conductor that centralizes execution ergonomics: async signal latching to coordinate parallel tool runs, idempotent result hydration to prevent duplicate injections, and an OpenAI-compat shim for function-calling semantics in streamed responses. Summary of changes: 1) runtime: add redwood/pkg/runtime/toolcall/conductor.go implementing a conductor that tracks call IDs, latches result signals, and emits ordered hydration events to the streaming layer. 2) api: update redwood/pkg/api/openai_compat/handlers.go to translate OpenAI-style function_call choices into conductor jobs and to consume conductor hydration events for stable streamed structured output. 3) sdk: small ergonomics helpers in redwood-sdk-python/redwood/tooling.py and redwood-sdk-typescript/src/tooling.ts to expose a non-blocking call API that wires into the conductor. 4) client compat tests and e2e flows in benchmark-lab/test/toolcall_conductor_test.go and redwood-examples/multi-tool-orchestration/ to demonstrate retry-safe replay. 5) docs: new section in redwood-docs/reference/tool-calling.md describing the conductor lifecycle and recommended retry semantics. Key behavior & guarantees: - Async Signal Latching: parallel tool executions post a transient signal with a stable ID; the conductor latches the first successful result per signal ID and deduplicates subsequent completions. - Idempotent Result Hydration: hydration objects are stamped with stable request-call identifiers and replay-safe tokens so retries or replays will not cause duplicate injections or side-effects. - Streaming Stability: the OpenAI-compat shim buffers partial tool outputs and emits structured function_call + response blocks in the same ordering across initial stream and any reconnects. - Retry policy: default hedged-retry policy with configurable window; retries for non-idempotent tools are disabled by default and require opt-in via tool metadata. Migration notes: minor SDK change for tool registration signature (opt-in param "idempotent": true/false) and server-side default policy; existing tool integrations continue to work but will not get automatic retries until annotated. Backward compatibility: OpenAI-compat endpoints remain backwards-compatible for single-tool calls; multi-tool streaming semantics improved but explicit opt-in required in some edge cases. Checklist: - [x] Implement conductor core and unit tests - [x] Integrate with OpenAI-compat handler - [x] Add SDK ergonomics helpers and examples - [x] Add regression tests for retries and replay - [x] Update docs and migration guide Testing: unit tests for latch/dedup logic, integration tests covering streaming reconnection scenarios, manual QA on Dedicated and Hosted with synthetic multi-tool workloads, load tests in perf pipeline to validate latency impact. Risk & mitigations: potential perf overhead from conductor coordination under extreme fan-out; mitigated with batching windows and fast-path bypass for single-tool calls. Non-idempotent tools present risk of duplicate side-effects; default policy disables retries and docs + SDK require explicit idempotent flag. Related work: follow-ups expected to add observability hooks in Redwood Console for per-call idempotency rates and latency heatmaps. Commits summary: Commit a1b2c3 add conductor scaffolding and interface definitions; Commit d4e5f6 implement signal latch and dedupe; Commit f7g8h9 add openai_compat translations and streaming glue; Commit i0j1k2 SDK helpers and tests; Commit l3m4n5 docs and examples. Files changed (high level): ["redwood/pkg/runtime/toolcall/conductor.go","redwood/pkg/runtime/toolcall/conductor_test.go","redwood/pkg/api/openai_compat/handlers.go","redwood-sdk-python/redwood/tooling.py","redwood-sdk-typescript/src/tooling.ts","benchmark-lab/test/toolcall_conductor_test.go","redwood-examples/multi-tool-orchestration/demo.md","redwood-docs/reference/tool-calling.md","redwood-docs/migrations/2026-02-21-toolcall-conductor.md","redwood-console/observability/hooks_stub.go","redwood/ci/toolcall_conductor_e2e.yml","redwood/pkg/runtime/toolcall/retry_policy.go"].

source.title: Policy-driven gradient rollouts and shadow/A-B traffic orchestration
source.author: Ava Chen
source.description:
Motivation: provide a single policy layer that can express canary gradients, A/B splits, shadowing, and automated rollback triggers for model variant rollouts across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments. This PR implements a policy-driven rollout engine and runtime hooks to route traffic incrementally, evaluate health metrics, and trigger automated rollback or promotion when thresholds are met.

What this change contains: checklist: [x] rollout policy DSL and parser; [x] runtime router integration (routing-by-policy); [x] metrics adapter to evaluate success criteria (latency, error-rate, quality-regression hooks); [x] shadow mode implementation (non-impacting mirrored requests); [x] A/B split support with weighted sampling; [x] automated rollback circuit with hysteresis and cooldown; [x] console integration for visualizing rollout state and editing policies; [x] e2e and unit tests with synthetic traffic scenarios.

Design notes: the policy DSL is deliberately conservative: it expresses a percentage gradient (start->target), evaluation windows, primary metrics with tolerated deltas, and fallback actions. Policies are evaluated by a lightweight controller: the controller emits routing directives to the router via an atomic config push (no hot-restart required). To avoid cross-tenant leakage the controller enforces model variant isolation and integrates with existing RBAC checks.

Rollout modes supported: 1) gradient canary (0->N% over time), 2) A/B experiments with deterministic assignment keys, 3) shadow/mirror traffic where responses are recorded and compared asynchronously, 4) manual promote/abort via console.

Safety: automated rollback is conservative — thresholds must be met for three consecutive windows and the controller requires a minimum sample size before taking action. A circuit-breaker prevents rapid flapping and records reasons in audit logs. Emergency abort can be triggered via API or console and will immediately revert routing directives.

Testing and validation: included e2e scenarios that run synthetic traffic mixes (latency skew, quality regressions simulated via metric stubs). We also added perf microbenchmarks ensuring router decision path adds <0.5ms p50 on our standard config.

Migration notes: existing rollouts configured with the older mirror/canary flags are automatically translated to the new policy model on startup with a one-time migration job. Admins can opt-out via the feature flag rollout.policy_migration=false.

Operational notes: rollout controller exposes metrics: rollout_progress, rollout_failure_count, rollback_count, policy_eval_latency_ms. Alerts recommended: rollout_failure_count > 0 for 5m and policy_eval_latency_ms > 250ms.

Related: ENG-7432 implements the policy DSL and engine; ENG-7500 wires the console UI and migration job.
source.commits: feat(rollout): add policy DSL and parser | feat(router): integrate policy-driven directives | feat(shadow): add mirror pipeline and recording | test(rollout): e2e synthetic scenarios for gradient and AB | perf(router): microbenchmarks and path optimizations | docs: rollout policies and migration guide
review_comments: Diego Morales (2026-02-20): Can we make the evaluation window configurable per-policy? Also flagging that the default sample-size threshold needs to be conservative; otherwise small tenants may flapping. Ava: made window configurable and increased default sample size to 500.

Priya Nair (2026-02-23): UX looks good. Two requests: 1) add an explicit 'dry-run' mode for A/B so results show in console without affecting production metrics, 2) ensure audit log includes the pre/post routing directive snapshot. Ava: added dry-run and expanded audit payloads.

Liam O'Connor (2026-02-28): Integration tests are solid but e2e occasionally failed on noisy CI; root cause was a test race in the migration job. Fixed by adding a leader-election short lease during migration and reworked tests to use deterministic ports. Tests green on rerun.
source.merge_commit_summary: squash: policy-driven rollout engine, router integration, console UI, migration job, e2e tests
source.merge_outcome: merged to main after approvals from reviewers. no conflicts. backfilled release notes and updated migration docs.
source.release_notes:
Introduce policy-driven rollouts: canary gradients, A/B splits, and shadowing with automated rollback. Includes migration tool for older canary/mirror configs and console support.
Impact: new metrics and audit logs added; default rollout policy conservative to avoid traffic churn.
source.risk_and_mitigation: Risk: misconfigured policies could route too much traffic to unstable variants. Mitigations: conservative defaults, minimum sample size, cooldown, manual abort and feature-flag to disable auto-migration. Admins should stage policies in dev before promoting to production.

source.title: Multiplexer: parallel tool execution, idempotent retries, and ergonomic result injection
source.author: Diego Morales
source.description:
Motivation: production workloads increasingly issue multiple tool calls per response (lookups, enrichment, external actions) and clients expect predictable injection semantics, low-latency parallelism, and transparent retry behavior when external tools fail. This PR introduces a lightweight multiplexer for tool execution that: 1) executes independent tool calls in parallel when safe, 2) exposes an explicit idempotency token + retry middleware for transient tool failures, 3) provides an ergonomic result-injection API surface for handlers (deterministic injection points with ordering hints), and 4) keeps OpenAI-compatibility behaviors (function-calling fallback and structured outputs) intact.

Summary of changes: commits implement a runtime-level ToolMultiplexer that accepts a list of ToolCall requests and returns deterministic ToolResults with stable ids and insertion points. We added a RetryMiddleware that respects per-tool idempotency tokens and exponential backoff policies, and a backpressure-aware worker pool that bounds concurrency based on route config. The compatibility shim for openai->redwood function calling was updated to translate function return values into the new ToolResult shape. Documentation and SDK ergonomics were updated (Python and TS examples) and end-to-end tests were added for mixed success/failure scenarios.
commits: c1a9f4b: initial multiplexer runtime scaffolding and interface
a7d3b2e: add worker pool and backpressure accounting (route-configurable)
b6e4d11: idempotency token + retry middleware, integrate with multiplexer
f3c2a7e: openai compat shim: function->ToolCall translation and result mapping
d9b1f8a: SDK examples and docs for result injection API
e2a9b5c: e2e tests for parallel tool calls, flaky test harness adjustments
squash: small followups and lint fixes
changed_files: internal/api/tool_multiplexer.go — new multiplexer + workerpool + backpressure accounting
internal/api/tool_retry_middleware.go — idempotency token handling and retry policies
pkg/openai/compat_layer.go — translate function-calling payloads to ToolCall list and back
sdk/python/redwood/tools.py — convenience ToolCall builder and injection helpers
sdk/typescript/src/tools.ts — types and example helper (kept backward-compatible)
docs/api/tool-calling.md — documented new injection points, ordering hints, and examples
tests/e2e/tool_multiplexer_test.go — coverage for parallel success, partial failures, and retry logic
build/ci.yml — new job to run tool-multiplexer e2e in CI (flaky marker and rerun policy)
pkg/console/observability_tooling.go — emit metrics: tool.count, tool.latency.p95, tool.retry.count
review_comments: Ava Chen: High-level looks good. Two API questions: (1) should we make ordering_hint mandatory on ToolCall or optional? make it optional with clear docs. (2) How do we communicate partial failure to the caller (status + per-tool error) — ensure structured output maps to OpenAI function semantics.

Diego Morales: Reply: ordering_hint is optional; multiplexer will preserve provided order when present and otherwise use deterministic sort by tool_id. Partial failures are surfaced as per-tool result objects (status: success|error) and we map that to function-calling content for openai shim (see compat_layer translation).

Priya Natarajan: Is idempotency enforced only by user-provided token? What about non-idempotent tools invoked in parallel — risk of duplicate side effects. Recommend a warning in docs and a safe-mode flag at route level.
Diego Morales: Added route config 'tool_safe_mode' that forces serial execution for non-idempotent tools and emits warning log + telemetry. Updated docs and SDK helper to accept idempotency_token.
approvals: Samir Patel: Approved — tests and metrics look reasonable. One follow-up to reduce default workerpool from 32 to 16 for memory-constrained Dedicated nodes.
Ava Chen: Approved after doc clarifications.
source.e2e_notes: One flaky test (external-mock-timeout) failed on first run; added a short jitter and retry in test harness. CI uses rerun-on-flake policy for e2e jobs which allowed green merge after re-run.
source.release_notes:
- Add ToolMultiplexer to execute multiple tool calls in parallel when safe; supports ordering hints and deterministic injection points.
- Introduce per-tool idempotency tokens and RetryMiddleware with exponential backoff.
- OpenAI compatibility shim updated to translate function calling into multiplexer-friendly ToolCalls and map results into structured outputs.
- New observability metrics: tool.count, tool.latency.p95, tool.retry.count.
- Route config 'tool_safe_mode' to force serial execution for non-idempotent workflows.
source.notes_for_operators: When rolling out to Dedicated clusters, monitor tool.retry.count and tool.latency.p95. Default workerpool size reduced to 16 (was 32) to limit memory pressure on small nodes. If customers rely on side-effecting tools without idempotency tokens, recommend enabling tool_safe_mode on the route.
source.merge_outcome: Merged via squash after approvals and rerun of flaky e2e. No migrations required.

source.title: bake signed artifact index and provenance into private-installer release manifests
source.author: Maya Kline
source.description: Motivation: private deployments (VPC/on-prem) require a reproducible, signed index that bundles release artifacts (installer tarballs, Helm charts, Terraform modules), attestations (SBOM/prov.json), and signature metadata so customers can verify what they deploy. This PR introduces a baked 'signed-artifact-index' generation step in the private-installer release pipeline, integrates Notary v2-compatible signing (via GCP KMS in CI), surfaces provenance files alongside Helm and Terraform artifacts, and wires the installer/upgrade tooling to consume the index during installs and rollbacks. Implementation summary: 1) CI: add .github/workflows/release-private-installer-index.yml that builds artifacts in a hermetic container, generates SBOMs (syft), produces provenance JSON, and invokes notary-v2 client to produce signatures and an OCI index blob that contains pointers to signed artifacts; 2) release manifests: produce release-manifests/private/<version>/signed-artifact-index.json (index is content-addressed and references OCI descriptors for artifacts plus prov and sbom); 3) installer: private-installer/installer/manifest_fetcher.go updated to fetch and verify the signed index using a configurable verification key/cert bundle; 4) helm & terraform: helm-charts/values.yaml and terraform/modules/private_release/main.tf updated to prefer signed artifacts when index is present; 5) upgrade/rollback tooling: upgrade-planer now reads index history to plan deterministic rollbacks. Checklist: - [x] CI job builds artifacts and SBOMs - [x] Notary v2 signing step implemented with KMS key reference (kept configurable per org) - [x] Signed index JSON schema and example added - [x] Installer verification path implemented and tested locally - [x] Terraform module fallback added when index absent - [x] Documentation and upgrade notes drafted (docs/private-release-index.md) Notes on security: private key material is never stored in the repo; CI uses ephemeral signing via KMS grant. For air-gapped/private builds, the index generator supports an 'offline-sign' mode where prov + sbom are exported for customer-side signing. Rollout plan: land on main behind feature flag RELEASE_SIGNED_INDEX, enable on canary private release, collect verified installs, then flip default for new releases. Related: follows up on ENG-4920 and ties into SUP-3212 customer request for verifiable artifacts. Release notes: see release_notes field.
commits: a5e6d2d: ci(release): add release-private-installer-index workflow and container build image
c4b19f1: build(signing): integrate notary-v2 client wrapper and KMS signer helper

source.release_notes: Add signed-artifact-index to private releases: release manifest now includes a content-addressed index with SBOMs and provenance; CI produces Notary v2-compatible signatures via configured KMS. Installer and Terraform/Helm modules can verify signatures and prefer signed artifacts. Operators can use offline sign mode for air-gapped environments.
notes_for_followup: Automate rotation of KMS signing keys and add staged key promotion in the release pipeline (ENG-5120)
Consider support for TUF metadata layer on top of the index for higher resilience

source.acknowledgements: Thanks to SRE (Diego) for CI runner tuning, Security (Priya) for signing review, and Platform (Ethan) for terraform/helm validation.

title: bake signed artifact index and provenance into private-installer release manifests
preview: Motivation: private deployments (VPC/on-prem) require a reproducible, signed index that bundles release artifacts (installer tarballs, Helm charts, Terraform modules), attestations (SBOM/prov.json), and signature metadata so customers can verify what they deploy. This PR introduces a baked 'signed-artifact-index' generation step in the private-installer release pipeline, integrates Notary v2-compatible signing (via GCP KMS in CI), surfaces provenance files alongside Helm and Terraform artifacts, and wires the installer/upgrade tooling to consume the index during installs and rollbacks. Implementation summary: 1) CI: add .github/workflows/release-private-installer-index.yml that builds artifacts in a hermetic container, generates SBOMs (syft), produces provenance JSON, and invokes notary-v2 client to produce signatures and an OCI index blob that contains pointers to signed artifacts; 2) release manifests: produce release-manifests/private/<version>/signed-artifact-index.json (index is content-addressed and references OCI descriptors for artifacts plus prov and sbom); 3) installer: private-installer/installer/manifest_fetcher.go updated to fetch and verify the signed index using a configurable verification key/cert bundle; 4) helm & terraform: helm-charts/values.yaml and terraform/modules/private_release/main.tf updated to prefer signed artifacts when index is present; 5) upgrade/rollback tooling: upgrade-planer now reads index history to plan deterministic rollbacks. Checklist: - [x] CI job builds artifacts and SBOMs - [x] Notary v2 signing step implemented with KMS key reference (kept configurable per org) - [x] Signed index JSON schema and example added - [x] Installer verification path implemented and tested locally - [x] Terraform module fallback added when index absent - [x] Documentation and upgrade notes drafted (docs/private-release-index.md) Notes on security: private key material is never stored in the repo; CI uses ephemeral signing via KMS grant. For air-gapped/private builds, the index generator supports an 'offline-sign' mode where prov + sbom are exported for customer-side signing. Rollout plan: land on main behind feature flag RELEASE_SIGNED_INDEX, enable on canary private release, collect verified installs, then flip default for new releases. Related: follows up on ENG-4920 and ties into SUP-3212 customer request for verifiable artifacts. Release notes: see release_notes field.

source.title: v1/chat.completions: resilient stream-fragmenting + truncated-logprobs + toolcall backoff
source.author: Ava Chen
source.description:
Motivation: We've observed two classes of production failures in the OpenAI /v1/chat.completions compatibility adapter when tool/function calling and long-running model responses are involved: 1) clients receiving partial or duplicated streamed chunks after transient network blips or internal retries, and 2) malformed structured outputs (JSON) emitted incrementally by the model which break downstream tool parsing. Additionally, customers running toolcall heavy workloads saw elevated tail error rates when the function invocation subsystem experienced transient 5xxs or long latencies.

This PR implements a set of protocol and runtime changes to improve UX and parity with typical OpenAI semantics while keeping streaming stable:
- Stream fragment reconciliation: attach a "stream_fragment_id" UUID to every top-level event in the stream envelope and an optional "resume_token" when resuming. The client-side SDKs can re-assemble idempotent fragments and drop duplicates after reconnect. This reduces double-insert issues observed in clients that reconnect mid-response.
- Truncated logprobs envelope: instead of omitting logprobs for long token runs, we add a "logprobs_truncated": true flag and a "last_token_index" field so clients can reconcile partial probability data without mis-attributing tokens. This preserves parity with expected OpenAI logprobs semantics for downstream evaluators.
- Toolcall backoff and fallback policy: the toolcall manager now implements exponential backoff with jitter for transient tool failures (5xx/timeout). After 3 retries or 10s cumulative delay we emit a structured fallback event and mark the function call as "failed_retryable" in the response envelope. Admin-configurable policy allows immediate fallback to a non-tool resolution path (e.g., model-only response) for customers that prefer availability over tool fidelity.
- Structured output sanitizer: streaming JSON fragments are validated by a permissive streaming parser. We now buffer up to 4KB of partial JSON fragments per toolcall and attempt best-effort correction for common issues (trailing commas, unclosed braces) before sending final structured outputs. If sanitizer cannot repair, we emit a "structured_output_error" event with the raw fragment for downstream diagnostics.
- Client heartbeat: server emits a small "heartbeat" control event every 18s on long streams to keep HTTP/HTTP2 intermediaries alive and to surface stuck connections earlier to clients.

Changes included (summary):
- API/compat: redwood/api/compat/chatv1_adapter.py -- add fragment_id/resume_token header handling, map truncated logprobs into new envelope keys, and wire toolcall fallback flags.
- Runtime: redwood/runtime/streaming/fragment_coordinator.go -- fragment generation and dedupe table + resume token handling.
- Tooling: redwood/runtime/tooling/toolcall_manager.go -- retry/backoff policy, fallback event emission, metrics counters.
- Observability: redwood/observability/metrics/streaming_metrics.promcfg -- new metrics: stream.fragment.reconciled, toolcall.retry.count, structured_output.errors.
- SDKs: redwood-sdk-python/streaming_client.py -- resume token logic + heartbeat handling; redwood-sdk-typescript/streaming.ts -- same. Tests added to both SDKs.
- Tests: unit tests for fragment coordinator, integration test that simulates mid-stream disconnect and verifies idempotent reconstruction, e2e canary with perf-canary harness.

Checklist:
- [x] Protocol fields and server wiring
- [x] Backoff and fallback policy implementation
- [x] Streaming sanitizer + error event path
- [x] SDK resume/heartbeat logic (Python & TS)
- [x] Unit/integration/e2e tests
- [x] Benchmarks: perf-canary workload run and dashboard updated

Performance notes:
- Microbenchmark: median latency unchanged; 95th percentile latency increased ~1.8% at 2048 token workloads due to additional fragment metadata and sanitizer buffering.
- Error-rate experiments: toolcall transient failure recovery reduced end-user visible function-failure errors from 6.4% -> 1.2% in staging under injected 5xxs.

Operational notes:
- New metrics are in the streaming metrics group and exported to the dashboard. Alerting: added a throttled alert on structured_output.errors to detect unexpected sanitizer regressions.
- Defaults: backoff policy defaults are conservative (max_retries=3, base_delay=250ms, max_delay=5s) and can be overridden via tenant config in Dedicated/Private installs.

Related work and references: Linear ENG-9786 (stream resilience), ticket SUP-4321 (customer report), related PRs for chat-stream framing in history.

Merging: squash merge into main after green CI and a short canary in prod (rolled to 10% traffic) succeeded.

c4b7e9f: implement fragment coordinator with in-memory dedupe table and resume API
d9e2a46: toolcall_manager: add retry/backoff and structured fallback emission
f5b8c11: streaming sanitizer: permissive parser and repair heuristics
e2c3a99: sdk(python/ts): resume token handling + heartbeat event handling
b7f4aa2: tests: unit + integration + perf-canary config
c9d8f77: observability: add metrics and dashboard panels
review_comments: Miguel Torres (2026-02-21): Can you confirm the resume_token is stable across horizontal restarts? We should persist minimal token state in the fragment coordinator store.
Ava Chen (2026-02-21): Good catch. Coordinator now persists token -> last_sequence mapping to the runtime store (leveldb-backed) with TTL. Added unit tests.
Leah Patel (2026-02-24): Sanitizer heuristics look helpful but dangerous. Please ensure we log raw fragments to secure diagnostic bucket and gate repair behind a config flag.
Ava Chen (2026-02-24): Added secure logging and a tenant config flag 'streaming.sanitizer.enabled' (default true).
Connor O'Neill (2026-02-26): Can we add an opt-out for the heartbeat (for customers who rely on raw wire semantics)?
Ava Chen (2026-02-26): Added 'streaming.heartbeat.enabled' tenant config and documentation note.
source.release_notes: Added robust stream fragmenting with resume tokens and truncated-logprobs flag; improved toolcall reliability with retry/backoff and a configurable fallback path; streaming JSON sanitizer to reduce malformed structured outputs; new metrics and flags. Backwards compatible; SDKs updated to support resume and heartbeat behavior.
files_diff_snippet: + event := StreamEvent{FragmentID: fragmentID, ResumeToken: resumeToken, Type: 'delta', Data: payload}
+ // toolcall backoff: retry up to 3 times with jitter; emit structured fallback if exhausted

source.title: dynamic per-channel requant + KV-aware jitter-resilient continuous-batching
source.author: Leah Park
source.description: Background and motivation: Production workloads show variance in per-channel activation distributions across prompts and token positions which makes static requant scales suboptimal. We observed systematic p99 latency spikes when the runtime switched between hot and cold KV cache footprints under continuous-batching; these were exacerbated by small batching jitter when multiple streams aligned. This PR implements a dynamic per-channel requant stage combined with a KV-cache-aware scheduling hint and a jitter-resilient continuous-batching policy to smooth throughput while keeping low tail latency. Scope: 1) A lightweight per-channel scale estimator that runs on the microkernel hot path and updates requant multipliers at configurable amortization windows. 2) A cheap, lock-free KV cache sizing hint that the scheduler uses to avoid abrupt prefix evictions during batch coalescing. 3) A jitter-resilient continuous-batcher that introduces minimal, adaptive hold windows to align requests from high-variance sources without increasing median latency. 4) Microkernel changes to expose a fused transform that applies requant multipliers inline with dequant/attn input packing to eliminate an extra copy. Design highlights and tradeoffs: - Estimator runs every N tokens (configurable per model) and uses exponential moving moments to remain stable across bursts. The goal is to reduce per-token compute overhead to <0.5% while adapting scales within 5-10k tokens. - Scheduler hint is advisory: it tags requests with estimated KV working set delta. The placement layer still enforces hard SLOs; the hint avoids placing requests that would cause a working-set jump on the same GPU within a short window. - Batcher employs an adaptive jitter budget: it only delays the head of a queue by up to X ms if the expected throughput improvement outweighs the added latency; X decays if p99 latency rises. Benchmarks (representative): - OPT-13B, INT8 hybrid (smoothquant + per-channel dynamic re-quant): throughput +10.8% on steady-state mixed sized prompts, median latency +2% (due to micro-batching alignment), p99 latency -14% on tail-heavy traces. - Llama-2-7B FP8 fallback: end-to-end token cost reduced by ~7% due to fewer KV evictions and reduced memory copies. - End-to-end e2e test with 500 simulated concurrent streams: observed 8-12% lower GPU-side memcpy time and 6-9% better sustained throughput. Implementation notes: - Runtime changes: src/runtime/quant/dyn_requant.h/cpp, src/runtime/quant/microkernel_requant.s, src/runtime/scheduler/kv_hint_scheduler.cpp - Batcher: src/runtime/batching/jitter_batcher.cpp, with a new config block in configs/batching.yaml - Tests: tests/quant/test_dyn_requant.cc (unit), bench/latency/bench_kv_jitter.py (integration traces) - Docs: docs/perf/quantization.md updated with configuration knobs and suggested Linear tasks for tuning. Checklist: - [x] Unit tests for estimator accuracy and stability - [x] Integration benchmark harness with trace replay - [x] Backwards-compatibility: dynamic requant disabled by default; opt-in via runtime config - [x] Release notes draft included - [x] SRE smoke test run on dedicated cluster and passed Notes on safety and compatibility: This change touches kernel hot-paths; dynamic scaling is guarded behind a runtime flag and tuned conservatively out-of-the-box to avoid regression. We included an automatic rollback condition in the scheduler when p99 exceeds baseline by 20% during an experiment. Related work: ENG-2048 (per-channel requant research spike), PR #612345 (microkernel packing refactor) for reference. Next steps: 1) A follow-up PR to expose an admin dashboard for per-model scale drift metrics and recommended amortization windows. 2) Experiment with a learned estimator offline and compare to the EMA-based estimator used here.
commits: Initial dynamic estimator and scheduler hook (Leah Park) - implement EMA-based per-channel estimator and basic kv_hint tagging
Add fused microkernel path and inline requant multiplier application (Rohit Sinha) - assembly microkernel and unit tests
Jitter-resilient batcher with adaptive hold window (Mateo Ruiz) - integration bench harness
Docs and config knobs, release notes draft (Ava Chen)
review_threads:
Mateo: Can you explain why the EMA window is better than a sliding histogram here?
Leah: EMA keeps the hot path cheap and is resilient to heavy tails; we add a debug mode to collect histograms offline and compare. Added test to validate estimator vs small histogram sample.

Rohit: Assembly changes look good but please add comment on register usage for AArch64.
Leah: Added comments and fallback macro for older toolchains.

Ava: Any risk of oscillation when multiple concurrent streams adapt scales?
Leah: We added cross-request dampening and a per-GPU dampening register to avoid opposite-direction flips. Also added a safety rollback in the scheduler if oscillation detected.
ci_checks: bench-suite: passed (bench_kv_jitter: baseline vs patch collected)
source.release_notes: Introduces an opt-in dynamic per-channel requant estimator and a KV-aware jitter-resilient batching policy that together improve steady-state throughput and reduce tail latency for quantized models. Disabled by default; opt-in via runtime config. See docs/perf/quantization.md for tuning guidance.

Checklist: - [x] Unit tests for estimator accuracy and stability - [x] Integration benchmark harness with trace replay - [x] Backwards-compatibility: dynamic requant disabled by default; opt-in via runtime config - [x] Release notes draft included - [x] SRE smoke test run on dedicated cluster and passed Notes on safety and compatibility: This change touches kernel hot-paths; dynamic scaling is guarded behind a runtime flag and tuned conservatively out-of-the-box to avoid regression. We included an automatic rollback condition in the scheduler when p99 exceeds baseline by 20% during an experiment. Related work: ENG-2048 (per-channel requant research spike), PR #612345 (microkernel packing refactor) for reference. Next steps: 1) A follow-up PR to expose an admin dashboard for per-model scale drift metrics and recommended amortization windows. 2) Experiment with a learned estimator offline and compare to the EMA-based estimator used here.
text: Background and motivation: Production workloads show variance in per-channel activation distributions across prompts and token positions which makes static requant scales suboptimal. We observed systematic p99 latency spikes when the runtime switched between hot and cold KV cache footprints under continuous-batching; these were exacerbated by small batching jitter when multiple streams aligned. This PR implements a dynamic per-channel requant stage combined with a KV-cache-aware scheduling hint and a jitter-resilient continuous-batching policy to smooth throughput while keeping low tail latency. Scope: 1) A lightweight per-channel scale estimator that runs on the microkernel hot path and updates requant multipliers at configurable amortization windows. 2) A cheap, lock-free KV cache sizing hint that the scheduler uses to avoid abrupt prefix evictions during batch coalescing. 3) A jitter-resilient continuous-batcher that introduces minimal, adaptive hold windows to align requests from high-variance sources without increasing median latency. 4) Microkernel changes to expose a fused transform that applies requant multipliers inline with dequant/attn input packing to eliminate an extra copy. Design highlights and tradeoffs: - Estimator runs every N tokens (configurable per model) and uses exponential moving moments to remain stable across bursts. The goal is to reduce per-token compute overhead to <0.5% while adapting scales within 5-10k tokens. - Scheduler hint is advisory: it tags requests with estimated KV working set delta. The placement layer still enforces hard SLOs; the hint avoids placing requests that would cause a working-set jump on the same GPU within a short window. - Batcher employs an adaptive jitter budget: it only delays the head of a queue by up to X ms if the expected throughput improvement outweighs the added latency; X decays if p99 latency rises. Benchmarks (representative): - OPT-13B, INT8 hybrid (smoothquant + per-channel dynamic re-quant): throughput +10.8% on steady-state mixed sized prompts, median latency +2% (due to micro-batching alignment), p99 latency -14% on tail-heavy traces. - Llama-2-7B FP8 fallback: end-to-end token cost reduced by ~7% due to fewer KV evictions and reduced memory copies. - End-to-end e2e test with 500 simulated concurrent streams: observed 8-12% lower GPU-side memcpy time and 6-9% better sustained throughput. Implementation notes: - Runtime changes: src/runtime/quant/dyn_requant.h/cpp, src/runtime/quant/microkernel_requant.s, src/runtime/scheduler/kv_hint_scheduler.cpp - Batcher: src/runtime/batching/jitter_batcher.cpp, with a new config block in configs/batching.yaml - Tests: tests/quant/test_dyn_requant.cc (unit), bench/latency/bench_kv_jitter.py (integration traces) - Docs: docs/perf/quantization.md updated with configuration knobs and suggested Linear tasks for tuning. Checklist: - [x] Unit tests for estimator accuracy and stability - [x] Integration benchmark harness with trace replay - [x] Backwards-compatibility: dynamic requant disabled by default; opt-in via runtime config - [x] Release notes draft included - [x] SRE smoke test run on dedicated cluster and passed Notes on safety and compatibility: This change touches kernel hot-paths; dynamic scaling is guarded behind a runtime flag and tuned conservatively out-of-the-box to avoid regression. We included an automatic rollback condition in the scheduler when p99 exceeds baseline by 20% during an experiment. Related work: ENG-2048 (per-channel requant research spike), PR #612345 (microkernel packing refactor) for reference. Next steps: 1) A follow-up PR to expose an admin dashboard for per-model scale drift metrics and recommended amortization windows. 2) Experiment with a learned estimator offline and compare to the EMA-based estimator used here.

source.title: hybrid flashattn + lazy-kv-eviction + operator-fusion for long-seq workloads
source.author: Ava Chen
source.description:
Motivation: Production workloads with mixed short- and long-context requests show uneven tail latency and high aggregate token cost due to conservative KV cache retention and generic attention kernel selection. This PR introduces a hybrid attention path that (1) dispatches FlashAttention when safe, (2) uses a lightweight fused matmul-add-dropout kernel for mid-sized heads, and (3) falls back to the existing fallback kernel for pathological shapes. In parallel, we implement a lazy KV eviction policy that decouples KV entry lifetime from request completion and enables opportunistic eviction under memory pressure. Together these changes reduce long-sequence p99 latency and improve throughput for streaming workloads while keeping memory bounded.

What changed (high level): implemented a Flash-aware scheduler that picks kernel variant at invocation time based on sequence length, head size, and kv-cache residency; added a fused QKV->matmul+add kernel for medium shapes (mp size 16/32) to reduce kernel launch overhead; integrated a lazy-kv-eviction manager that uses LRU+age heuristics and signals the scheduler to prefer compact kernels when eviction is active; CI/build: updated CUDA kernels, operator registration, and added unit/integration tests; docs: runtime tuning doc and rollout checklist.

Benchmarks (representative): evaluated on 8xA100-80GB and local dev GPU (A5000). Long-seq streaming (seq=16384, batch=1) shows p50 latency -12%, p95 -23%, p99 -31%, tokens/sec +18% vs main with previous FlashAttention dispatch. Medium mixed workload (seq distribution 128-2048) throughput improved +12% and reduced kernel launch counts by ~28%. Memory: average KV memory footprint reduced ~9% due to lazy eviction behavior under synthetic tail traffic.

Testing & CI: unit tests for kernel selection logic, KV eviction invariants, and end-to-end streaming harness. Added synthetic replay tests for real production traces. CI initially failed on quantization edge-case in fused kernel (nvcc error for sm_80 path), fixed by tightening alignments and adding memcpy fallback for odd strides. Full CI now green.

Rollout plan: feature-gated behind runtime.experimental.hybrid_attention. Staged rollout: Canary on Dedicated pool -> 10% Hosted -> 50% -> full. Canary will enable additional tracing hooks for per-request kernel decisions and kv-eviction events. Rollback path: flip feature flag or restart runtime to main kernel selection. The PR does not change public API, no breaking change.

Checklist: - [x] Kernel selection heuristics implemented and unit-tested - [x] Fused kernel validated on sm_70/75/80 - [x] Lazy KV eviction has safety invariants and metrics - [x] End-to-end perf harness and regression alerts - [x] Rollout feature flag and tracing - [x] Docs & tuning guide updated
commits: a1f3d7c: add hybrid scheduler and kernel registry (initial)
b2c4e11: implement fused qkv->matmul-add kernel and registration
c3d55f9: lazy kv eviction manager and metrics
d4e6a21: tests + perf harness updates
e5f77aa: fix nvcc alignment for sm_80 and add fallback memcpy
review_comments: Diego: Can we get a microbenchmark showing kernel selection cost? We saw an extra 60us in the first iteration in your local run.
Ava: Added kernel-launch amortization in the scheduler and microbench results (see benchmarks/launcher_latency.csv) showing <8us avg after warmup.
Priya: Lazy eviction seems dangerous for regulatory customers who want strict retention; how do we guarantee zero-data-loss semantics?
Ava: Lazy eviction only evicts cached KV used for inference (stateless) and leaves audit logs intact; added feature flag and explicit opt-out for Private deployments in docs.
Ethan: NVCC failure on CI for sm_80 initially — good catch. Please add the fallback path to handle unaligned strides.
Ava: Added memcpy fallback and expanded CI matrix to include sm_80 and sm_86 machines.
source.release_notes: runtime: improved attention dispatch and KV cache eviction for long-sequence streaming workloads; optional feature gated (runtime.experimental.hybrid_attention) that can boost throughput and reduce tail latency for mixed workloads. See docs/runtime/tuning/hybrid-attention.md for tuning.
testing: regression: nightly perf alert added for p95 on long_seq_stream
source.risk_and_mitigation: Risk: kernel selection may misclassify pathological shapes and cause CPU fallback thrashing. Mitigation: conservative initial thresholds, feature flag, and auto-fallback to previous scheduler on sustained regressions. Added tracing to detect misclassifications.
source.notes: Follow-up work: integrate operator fusion with compilation pipeline to avoid runtime registration overhead; consider a memory-aware scheduler that factors in cross-request memory peaks.

source.title: Introduce token-lifecycle burnup histogram, cross-service latency fingerprint, and alert coalescer
source.author: Anjali Rao
source.release_notes: Add token_lifecycle_burnup histogram, latency_fingerprint rollup, and server-side alert coalescer. Dashboard panels and runbook updated. No customer-facing API changes.
source.description:
Context: token-level SLO noise and long-tail cost/latency alerts are creating alert fatigue. Existing token-cost rollups capture per-token aggregates but struggle to: (1) show how cost accumulates across pipeline stages (ingest, model, postprocess), (2) identify cross-service latency shape changes (same total latency but different phase breakdown), and (3) suppress repeated similar alerts that indicate the same incident.

What this PR does: introduces three coordinated pieces:
1) token_lifecycle_burnup histogram metric (metric/token_lifecycle_burnup): records cumulative token cost/latency at stage boundaries per invocation (labels: tenant_id, model, route, stage). Supports configurable buckets and exported rollups.
2) latency_fingerprint: a small vectorized signature (string) derived from percentile deltas across stages for an invocation; stored as a tag on traces and emitted as a low-cardinality rollup for fast grouping.
3) alert_coalescer: a server-side component in observability-alerts that groups token-level alerts using latency_fingerprint similarity and recent-window dedupe rules (window default 5m, jittered). Coalescer emits a single incident with aggregated counts and exemplar trace links.

Design notes:
- token_lifecycle_burnup is implemented as a histogram with predefined exponential buckets plus explicit tail buckets to capture heavy-tailed token cost. The SDK helper is backwards-compatible (adds a single call at stage boundaries).
- latency_fingerprint is intentionally low-cardinality (bucketized percentiles -> base64-encoded 64-bit signature) to avoid index/explosion. We ensure privacy by hashing tenant_id before attaching higher-cardinality metadata.
- Alert coalescer runs as an idempotent stateless podset backed by a short-lived Redis lease for leader selection; it consumes alert events, computes fingerprint similarity via Hamming distance on the signature and groups alerts meeting threshold (0.8 similarity).

Rollout plan:
- Feature flag: observability.token_burnup.enabled=false by default.
- Canary: enable for 2 enterprise tenants for 48 hours, sampling rate 5% invocations.
- Ramp: 5% -> 25% -> 100% over 2 weeks with monitoring on incident changes and index size.

Checklist:
- [x] Metric schema and SDK helper
- [x] Tracing enrichment for fingerprint
- [x] Alert coalescer service + leader election
- [x] Dashboards: token-burnup panel, fingerprint cohort explorer
- [x] Runbook: how to un-coalesce and escalate
- [x] Unit + integration tests

Why this is safe: no breaking API changes. All new signals are behind a feature flag and low-sample default. The coalescer is additive — it suppresses alert noise but can be disabled quickly.

Related: ENG-4821 (alert noise reduction), ENG-4899 (token cost attribution).
commits: a1f3d91  Add histogram type and SDK helper for token_lifecycle_burnup
4b22c4f  Emit latency_fingerprint from invocation tracer step (hash and encode)
7c8a3f2  Implement alert_coalescer service and Redis lease for leader election
d9f4b60  Add dashboard JSON: token-burnup-panel and fingerprint-explorer
e2b11a3  Add runbook and migration notes
f7c9d45  Tests: unit + e2e for sampling, coalescing logic
review_comments: Marcelo Vega: Nice design — small notes: (1) can we reduce default fingerprint length to 48 bits to cut index pressure? (2) please add an opt-out env for redis in the coalescer podset.
Author (Anjali Rao): Reply: lowered to 48 bits in latest commit and added COALESCER_DISABLE_REDIS env check; see commit 7c8a3f2.
Priya Natarajan: Request changes: make sure token_lifecycle_burnup buckets are configurable via feature-flag/tenant-config; also add a metric for coalesced_alert_count.
Author: Reply: added tenant-config hook and exported observability.alerts.coalesced_count metric (commit d9f4b60).
Liam O'Connor: CI flake on integration test (redis connection race) — can we add retry/backoff?
Author: Reply: added exponential backoff to leader election and retried the build; CI passed on rerun.
ci_checks: integration-tests: pass (rerun required for redis flake)
source.notes_on_rollout: Start with feature flag off. Enable for two enterprise tenants (canary) using tenant config and 5% sampling. Monitor: observability.alerts.coalesced_count, token_lifecycle_burnup.bucket_growth, index.bulk_upload_rate. If aggregated index growth > 2x expected, pause ramp and reduce sampling. Full runbook in docs/runbooks/token-burnup-coalescer.md.
