source.title: Improve chat stream framing, function-call handshake, and logprob envelope
source.author: Ava Chen
commit_summary: implement streaming framing state machine with explicit boundary tokens and SSE markers for function call handshake
introduce final 'logprob_envelope' payload emitted at stream completion to surface stable token-level logprobs
normalize role token mapping and ensure stop sequence handling is identical for streaming and non-streaming flows
add e2e streaming + function call tests and unit tests for framing edge cases
docs: document new handshake events and backward-compatible query params
source.description:
Motivation: We observed intermittent corruption of streamed messages when function calls were emitted mid-chunk and when stop sequences split across buffer boundaries. Consumers (third-party SDKs and internal examples) reported mis-attributed logprobs and truncated function-call payloads. This causes downstream tooling that relies on deterministic final outputs and token-level diagnostics to misbehave.

Changes in this PR: added a lightweight framing state machine to the streaming runtime so every SSE/data chunk is annotated with a stable boundary token and an explicit event for function-call lifecycle: 'fn_call.start' and 'fn_call.end'. This removes ambiguity about when a tool invocation begins and ends across stream re-fragments. To surface token-level diagnostics without polluting the incremental stream we emit a final 'logprob_envelope' JSON object as the last event for both streaming and non-streaming requests; it maps token offsets to per-role logprobs and is documented as a separate (optional) payload consumers can request.

Backward compatibility: The new framing is opt-out for existing clients by default. We add a query parameter include_logprob_envelope (true|false) and a legacy alias logprobs that maps to the same behavior for OpenAI-style callers. Existing simple callers that don't parse the terminal envelope see no change. The handshake events are idempotent and can be ignored by clients that only read incremental content.

Behavioral guarantees: stop sequence handling has been unified: when a stop token spans a chunk boundary the runtime defers finalization until the full stop is observed and then emits the terminal event in the same atomic sequence across streaming and non-streaming modes. Function-call arguments are emitted as a single atomic object once the 'fn_call.end' event occurs. The logprob envelope is computed using the final KV-cache state to ensure token logprobs are consistent with the produced text.

Performance and safety: microbenchmarks show a small median latency regression (~2%) for small requests due to additional framing metadata, but a ~12% reduction in upstream token compute in workloads that reuse prefixes because of improved KV-cache reuse in the stream path. All code paths guard against exposing private keys or internal tracing identifiers in the emitted envelope.

Tests and verification: added unit tests for framing transitions, integration tests that simulate chunked TCP delivery and function-call mid-stream, and e2e tests verifying parity between streaming and blocking endpoints on stop/logprob outputs. CI initially failed the integration matrix due to a flaky test that assumed synchronous framing; that test was updated and the CI rerun passed.

Docs and examples: added a short migration note and an examples recipe showing how SDKs should collect 'fn_call.start'/'fn_call.end' events and parse the final 'logprob_envelope' for diagnostics.

Notes for reviewers: major areas to focus on are the correctness of framing transitions under network re-fragmentation, the atomicity of function-call object emission, and the memory footprint of the logprob envelope on very long responses. The PR is intentionally conservative in toggles to allow gradual rollout and feature-flagged experiments.
review_comments: Diego Martinez: This addresses the flaky streaming issue we saw in prod. Two requests: add a regression test that streams 1-byte chunks and confirm stop handling; and add a small benchmark comparing peak memory for a 16k token response with and without the envelope.
Ava Chen (author): Added test for 1-byte chunk streaming and updated perf script to capture RSS in the KV cache bench. Bench results included in the PR description summary.
Priya Nair: The handshake events look good. Suggest making the 'fn_call.*' event names reserved in the public SSE schema in docs to avoid collisions with user-defined events.
Ava Chen (author): Reserved the event namespace in docs and added a short note in compat-migration.md.
Ethan Brooks: Verified e2e tests locally and ran the flaky integration twice. Approving pending green CI.
CI Bot: initial run failed: tests/integration/stream_framing_test.py::test_midstream_fn_call (flake) -- rerun triggered
CI Bot: rerun passed; all checks green.
release_notes: Streaming: stricter chunk framing and explicit function-call handshake events (fn_call.start / fn_call.end).
New final payload 'logprob_envelope' optionally emitted at stream completion for token-level diagnostics.
Unified stop-sequence handling across streaming and blocking APIs.
Backwards-compatible: include_logprob_envelope and legacy logprobs alias available for OpenAI-style callers.

Behavioral guarantees: stop sequence handling has been unified: when a stop token spans a chunk boundary the runtime defers finalization until the full stop is observed and then emits the terminal event in the same atomic sequence across streaming and non-streaming modes. Function-call arguments are emitted as a single atomic object once the 'fn_call.end' event occurs. The logprob envelope is computed using the final KV-cache state to ensure token logprobs are consistent with the produced text.

Performance and safety: microbenchmarks show a small median latency regression (~2%) for small requests due to additional framing metadata, but a ~12% reduction in upstream token compute in workloads that reuse prefixes because of improved KV-cache reuse in the stream path. All code paths guard against exposing private keys or internal tracing identifiers in the emitted envelope.

Tests and verification: added unit tests for framing transitions, integration tests that simulate chunked TCP delivery and function-call mid-stream, and e2e tests verifying parity between streaming and blocking endpoints on stop/logprob outputs. CI initially failed the integration matrix due to a flaky test that assumed synchronous framing; that test was updated and the CI rerun passed.

Docs and examples: added a short migration note and an examples recipe showing how SDKs should collect 'fn_call.start'/'fn_call.end' events and parse the final 'logprob_envelope' for diagnostics.

Notes for reviewers: major areas to focus on are the correctness of framing transitions under network re-fragmentation, the atomicity of function-call object emission, and the memory footprint of the logprob envelope on very long responses. The PR is intentionally conservative in toggles to allow gradual rollout and feature-flagged experiments.
text:
Motivation: We observed intermittent corruption of streamed messages when function calls were emitted mid-chunk and when stop sequences split across buffer boundaries. Consumers (third-party SDKs and internal examples) reported mis-attributed logprobs and truncated function-call payloads. This causes downstream tooling that relies on deterministic final outputs and token-level diagnostics to misbehave.

Changes in this PR: added a lightweight framing state machine to the streaming runtime so every SSE/data chunk is annotated with a stable boundary token and an explicit event for function-call lifecycle: 'fn_call.start' and 'fn_call.end'. This removes ambiguity about when a tool invocation begins and ends across stream re-fragments. To surface token-level diagnostics without polluting the incremental stream we emit a final 'logprob_envelope' JSON object as the last event for both streaming and non-streaming requests; it maps token offsets to per-role logprobs and is documented as a separate (optional) payload consumers can request.

Backward compatibility: The new framing is opt-out for existing clients by default. We add a query parameter include_logprob_envelope (true|false) and a legacy alias logprobs that maps to the same behavior for OpenAI-style callers. Existing simple callers that don't parse the terminal envelope see no change. The handshake events are idempotent and can be ignored by clients that only read incremental content.

Behavioral guarantees: stop sequence handling has been unified: when a stop token spans a chunk boundary the runtime defers finalization until the full stop is observed and then emits the terminal event in the same atomic sequence across streaming and non-streaming modes. Function-call arguments are emitted as a single atomic object once the 'fn_call.end' event occurs. The logprob envelope is computed using the final KV-cache state to ensure token logprobs are consistent with the produced text.

Performance and safety: microbenchmarks show a small median latency regression (~2%) for small requests due to additional framing metadata, but a ~12% reduction in upstream token compute in workloads that reuse prefixes because of improved KV-cache reuse in the stream path. All code paths guard against exposing private keys or internal tracing identifiers in the emitted envelope.

Tests and verification: added unit tests for framing transitions, integration tests that simulate chunked TCP delivery and function-call mid-stream, and e2e tests verifying parity between streaming and blocking endpoints on stop/logprob outputs. CI initially failed the integration matrix due to a flaky test that assumed synchronous framing; that test was updated and the CI rerun passed.

Docs and examples: added a short migration note and an examples recipe showing how SDKs should collect 'fn_call.start'/'fn_call.end' events and parse the final 'logprob_envelope' for diagnostics.

Notes for reviewers: major areas to focus on are the correctness of framing transitions under network re-fragmentation, the atomicity of function-call object emission, and the memory footprint of the logprob envelope on very long responses. The PR is intentionally conservative in toggles to allow gradual rollout and feature-flagged experiments.

source.title: typed errors + ergonomic iterable reconnect API across SDKs
source.author: Anya Patel
source.release_notes: Adds typed SDK error classes and a new ergonomic iterable reconnect API for streaming across Python/TS/Go SDKs; bump SDK versions: python 1.8.0, typescript 2.3.0, go v0.9.1.
source.description:
Motivation: Developers repeatedly hit vague runtime errors when streams failed (auth handshake, transient network) and had to implement ad-hoc parsing logic to decide whether to retry, resume, or surface an error. This PR introduces language-native typed error classes across the three SDKs (Python/TypeScript/Go) and a unified ergonomic iterable reconnect API for stream consumers.

What this change does: 
- Introduces TypedError/StreamError subclasses with machine-friendly error_code, retriable, and suggestion fields across SDKs.
- Adds a new StreamIterable API (python: StreamIterable, ts: createStreamIterable, go: StreamIterator) that yields typed result objects and automatically attempts a single reconnect on retriable failures by default with backoff tuning. Reconnect behavior is configurable by the caller.
- Deprecates low-level sync callback hooks (on_chunk/on_error) in favor of async iterables/promises/iterators to improve ergonomics and parity.
- Adds structured telemetry hooks (opt-in) so apps can key metrics by error_code without brittle string matching.
- Small refactors: move common auth-retry utility into internal/sdk-common, update changelogs and bump SDK package versions.

Backward compatibility and migration: 
- Old callback APIs are still present but marked deprecated; they will emit one deprecation log per process and are scheduled for removal in the next major. Migration notes are added to each SDK CHANGELOG.
- Typed error shapes are additive; existing code catching broad exceptions will continue to work but can opt-in to typed handling for clearer logic.

Testing and rollout: 
- Unit tests added for typed error construction, serialization, and iterable reconnect edge cases. Integration tests simulate transient auth failures with a test double of the runtime.
- Canary rollout to Dedicated region us-west-2 for 72 hours before full merge.

Checklist: 
- [x] Python implementation and tests
- [x] TypeScript implementation and tests
- [x] Go implementation and tests
- [x] Changelogs and version bumps
- [x] Docs snippet in quickstarts repo pointing to new API example

References: Linear ENG-1987, SUP-402 (customer-facing incident), internal design doc: https://linear.app/redwood/issue/ENG-1987/stream-reconnect-typed-errors (internal link).
commits: a1b2c3: introduce sdk-common TypedError base and telemetry hooks
d4e5f6: python: StreamIterable implementation + tests
f7a8b9: ts: createStreamIterable, typed errors and typings
c0ffee: go: StreamIterator + typed error types, go module bump
b43d21: update CHANGELOGs and bump versions
review_comments: Ethan Park (request changes): The StreamIterable reconnect loop looks like it could retry non-idempotent streams when the model resumed in a different conversation state. Please add a guard for non-resumable streams or require explicit caller opt-in.

Anya Patel (reply): Added an explicit 'resumable' flag to the StreamIterable constructor and documented the caller opt-in behavior. Default remains conservative (single reconnect only for resumable streams).
Lina Gomez (nit): Could you flatten the error_code enum names for TS (avoid nested namespaces) and add a migration mapping in the changelog?
Anya Patel (reply): Flattened the enum, added mapping snippet to TYPESCRIPT changelog. Thanks for catching the nits.
Priya Varma (approve): Verified Python unit tests and manual smoke against canary dedicated cluster; LGTM after CI green.
ci_checks: CI: integration (streaming) - flaky (first run failed: transient auth), rerun - pass
source.notes: This PR bundles cross-language ergonomics work; teams using old callback hooks should follow the migration guide in the CHANGELOGs. Telemetry hooks are opt-in to avoid noise for early adopters.

source.title: concurrent-tool-runner: parallel tool invocation + OpenAI function_call compatibility updates
source.author: Ava Chen
source.description: Motivation: customers increasingly chain multiple tool calls per turn and want lower tail latency by running compatible tools in parallel, plus predictable result injection and idempotent retries. This PR introduces a server-side concurrent tool runner, extends the OpenAI-compatible function_call surface to allow explicit parallel calls and tool-result references, and updates SDK helpers and streaming merge semantics to keep the API stable for existing clients. Summary of changes: 1) runtime: add services/inference/tool_runner/concurrent_runner with a pluggable execution strategy (sequential | parallel | hybrid), a run_id-based idempotency layer for retries, and deterministic merge ordering for structured output injection. 2) api/openai-compat: extend function_call payload with optional keys: "parallel_calls" (array of tool names), "tool_result_refs" (map-like list of result identifiers), and "injection_policy" (deterministic | merge_stream | attach_raw). The compat layer translates these into internal execution plans and exposes merged streaming boundaries to callers. 3) streaming: improve chunk merging to reduce interleaving artifacts when multiple tool streams are active; add a small backpressure window to avoid head-of-line blocking in multi-tool flows. 4) SDKs: python & typescript helpers to create and await parallel tool futures, helpers to submit a single composite call that the server will execute in parallel when possible, and a deterministic merge utility for client-side local execution. 5) docs & tests: update openapi spec, add e2e test harness that exercises parallel function calls, and add unit tests covering retry idempotency and injection ordering. Backwards compatibility: default behavior remains sequential tool execution; the new keys are opt-in. Also preserved the original function_call semantics for single-tool usage to avoid breaking existing OpenAI-compatible clients. Rationale: moving parallelism into the platform lets us optimize batching and GPU utilization while keeping SDK ergonomics simple; adding run_id/idempotency reduces duplicate side-effects for retried calls. Rollout plan: feature gated behind runtime flag concurrent_tool_runner.enabled. Initially enabled in staging and can be opt-in per-tenant via config; will be enabled by default after 2 weeks of telemetry. Related: linked_linear ENG-4123 ENG-4181. Notes: this PR intentionally avoids introducing a new RPC surface; changes are translations within the compat layer and runtime to minimize client churn.
commits: a1c3b2d: services/inference: add concurrent runner skeleton and execution strategy interface
c9f4e5a: api/openai-compat: support parallel_calls and tool_result_refs in function_call translation
b4d2f9e: runtime: implement run_id idempotency cache and merge ordering
e7a12bd: sdk/python: add ParallelToolCall helper and merge utilities
f2b3c4d: sdk/ts: add CompositeToolRequest types + docs
d0e1f2a: tests: add e2e/parallel_tool_flow and unit tests for retry/idempotency
99aa88b: docs: openapi update and console docs for concurrent_tool_runner toggle
source.ci_details: Initial CI run: streaming e2e failed (flaky merge ordering). Fixed deterministic merge window and re-ran. All checks green: unit, integration, lint, typechecks, docker-build. Coverage unchanged (+0.2%).
source.conversation: Diego Morales: please add a feature flag and tenant opt-in controls before merging. Ava Chen: added concurrent_tool_runner.enabled and tenant config path; also documented the default rollout plan. Priya Nair: can we guarantee no increased memory spikes when parallelizing lots of small tools? Ava Chen: added a bounded worker pool with per-request concurrency cap and tests simulating 100 small-tool parallel requests. Liam O'Connor: nit on naming: 'tool_result_refs' is awkward; suggest 'tool_output_keys'. Ava Chen: kept both keys in translator for compatibility and added alias mapping in docs. Diego Morales: approve after CI green. Priya Nair: approve after addressing backpressure tuning. Merge performed via squash once staging telemetry validated behavior.
source.release_notes: Added server-side concurrent tool runner (opt-in). Extended OpenAI-compatible function_call payload to support parallel_calls and tool result references. SDK helpers for parallel tool orchestration (Python & TypeScript). Streaming merge improvements to reduce interleaving when multiple tool streams are active.

source.title: Add DMA stall & kernel-latency counters with sigprof-compatible timeline exporter
source.author: Maya Chen
source.release_notes: Adds new low-overhead DMA-stall counters and kernel-latency histograms plus a SigProf-compatible timeline exporter for integration with external profiling dashboards. No user-facing API changes.
source.description:
Context: We need finer-grained, low-overhead indicators of where inference stalls occur inside the serving runtime so that model routing, batching and kernel-selection heuristics can be tuned without attaching heavy profilers. This PR adds DMA-stall counters collected from the GPU driver telemetry shim, per-kernel latency histograms sampled into buckets, and a compact timeline exporter that emits SigProf-compatible records that downstream dashboards (and our internal perfboard) can ingest. Motivation: several customer incidents pointed to intermittent pauses that didn't show up in token-level latency traces (ENG-1223). Existing CPU-side sampling catches high-level stalls but misses kernel queuing and DMA scheduling hotspots.

Summary of changes (high level):
- runtime/telemetry: new dma_counters.{h,cc} implementing low-overhead atomic counters for DMA queue stalls and preemption events.
- runtime/profiler: kernel_latency_histogram, continuous bucketing with sliding-window reset and exponential decay.
- tools/timeline_exporter: sigprof_exporter that converts our internal samples to a compact record stream (.sprec) compatible with SigProf importers.
- benchmarks/microbench: added microbench harness that simulates multi-model mixed workloads and emits representative traces for dashboard ingestion.
- docs/observability: brief guidance and sample Grafana dashboard JSON for the latency buckets and DMA-stall heatmap.

Commits (summary):
1) Add dma counter primitives and basic increment paths (mchen)
2) Wire counter increments in queueing/scheduler path and kernel dispatch (mchen)
3) Implement kernel_latency_histogram with lockless circular buffers (mchen)
4) Add sigprof-compatible exporter and small CLI tool to emit .sprec from live socket (mchen)
5) Microbench harness + example dashboard JSON (mchen)
6) Tests, comments, CI adjustments and small flake fixes (mchen)

Files changed (representative):
- runtime/telemetry/dma_counters.h
- runtime/telemetry/dma_counters.cc
- runtime/profiler/kernel_latency_histogram.h
- runtime/profiler/kernel_latency_histogram.cc
- runtime/scheduler/scheduler_dispatch.cc (insert increments at dispatch path, ~12 LOC)
- tools/timeline_exporter/sigprof_exporter.cc
- benchmarks/microbench/sigprof_dmamicro.cxx
- docs/observability/sigprof-dma-dashboard.json
- tests/telemetry/test_dma_counters.cc

Small illustrative diff (scheduler hook):
- // before: dispatch kernel
+ // after: increment DMA stall counter if queue detected busy
+ dma_counters::global()->maybe_increment_dma_stall(queue_id);

Design notes and tradeoffs: we intentionally use a very small number of atomic counters and compact histogram buckets to keep per-request overhead sub-200ns on hot paths. The histogram is sampled on kernel completion (not per token) to avoid touching hot token paths. We opted for SigProf record compatibility instead of perfetto to broaden downstream tool options and because SigProf's compact record model reduces exporter overhead.

Bench results (CI microbench + local runs):
- Baseline (no counters): median dispatch latency 74us, p99 1.9ms
- With counters + exporter enabled (sampling only): median dispatch latency 77us (+4%), p99 2.0ms (+5%)
- Continuous microbench (mixed sequence lengths) shows DMA-stall counter correlation with p99 spikes in the kernel queue, enabling reliable detection in dashboards.

Test coverage: unit tests for counter increment/decrement and histogram bucketing. Integration test that runs microbench and verifies exporter produces valid .sprec frames and dashboard import script accepts them.

Review thread (high level):
- Liam: requested stronger guarantees about lockless buffer memory reclamation. Author replied with a short RFC link and added a single-writer circular buffer design and explicit hazard pointer comment.
- Priya: asked for clearer docs on how to enable exporter in Dedicated vs Private deployments. Author added docs/observability section and a note about disabling exporter by default (env var REDWOOD_SIGPROF_EXPORT=0).
- Ethan: ran local scale test and flagged a spurious flake in the microbench; author fixed timing window and re-ran CI.

CI and flakiness: initial CI run showed a flaky test in benchmarks/microbench that failed under the faster runner. I added a 2x timeout margin and retry in CI. Final CI: all checks passed (unit, integration, lint, clang-tidy). Exporter binary also passes a lightweight fuzzer for input validation.

Operational notes for SRE/Platform:
- Exporter is disabled by default in production clusters. To enable for debugging, set REDWOOD_SIGPROF_EXPORT=1 and point REDWOOD_SIGPROF_SOCKET to the collector. Exporter uses a single UDP socket and rotates files every 10s by default.
- Counters are exposed at /metrics under the telemetry namespace with the following names: redwood_dma_stall_total, redwood_kernel_latency_bucket{le=\"...\"}.
- Retention: internal decay policy keeps histograms for 10 minutes by default to avoid unbounded memory growth.

Rollout plan: canary on a single Dedicated fleet for 24h, monitor increased p95/p99 and any errors from exporter. If p99 increases by >10% revert feature flag. The PR includes a rollout script in scripts/telemetry_canary.sh.

Checklist:
- [x] Implementation
- [x] Unit tests
- [x] Integration tests
- [x] Documentation added
- [x] Benchmarks + dashboards
- [x] Rollout plan

Related work: ENG-1309 (continuous batching improvements), ENG-1223 (incident follow-up), internal perfboard importers. This complements the existing tracing probes by providing compact, event-focused counters that make causal analysis easier in dashboards.

Merge details: squash-and-merge after final approvals. Merge commit message includes a short summary and links to dashboards and the microbench harness. Merged by Priya on 2026-02-26.

If you want to reproduce locally: see benchmarks/microbench/README.md — instructions to build the microbench, spin a local collector (scripts/local_sigprof_collector.py) and import the resulting .sprec into the perfboard test dashboard.

- Priya: asked for clearer docs on how to enable exporter in Dedicated vs Private deployments. Author added docs/observability section and a note about disabling exporter by default (env var REDWOOD_SIGPROF_EXPORT=0).
- Ethan: ran local scale test and flagged a spurious flake in the microbench; author fixed timing window and re-ran CI.

CI and flakiness: initial CI run showed a flaky test in benchmarks/microbench that failed under the faster runner. I added a 2x timeout margin and retry in CI. Final CI: all checks passed (unit, integration, lint, clang-tidy). Exporter binary also passes a lightweight fuzzer for input validation.

Operational notes for SRE/Platform:
- Exporter is disabled by default in production clusters. To enable for debugging, set REDWOOD_SIGPROF_EXPORT=1 and point REDWOOD_SIGPROF_SOCKET to the collector. Exporter uses a single UDP socket and rotates files every 10s by default.
- Counters are exposed at /metrics under the telemetry namespace with the following names: redwood_dma_stall_total, redwood_kernel_latency_bucket{le=\"...\"}.
- Retention: internal decay policy keeps histograms for 10 minutes by default to avoid unbounded memory growth.

Rollout plan: canary on a single Dedicated fleet for 24h, monitor increased p95/p99 and any errors from exporter. If p99 increases by >10% revert feature flag. The PR includes a rollout script in scripts/telemetry_canary.sh.

Checklist:
- [x] Implementation
- [x] Unit tests
- [x] Integration tests
- [x] Documentation added
- [x] Benchmarks + dashboards
- [x] Rollout plan

Related work: ENG-1309 (continuous batching improvements), ENG-1223 (incident follow-up), internal perfboard importers. This complements the existing tracing probes by providing compact, event-focused counters that make causal analysis easier in dashboards.

Merge details: squash-and-merge after final approvals. Merge commit message includes a short summary and links to dashboards and the microbench harness. Merged by Priya on 2026-02-26.

If you want to reproduce locally: see benchmarks/microbench/README.md — instructions to build the microbench, spin a local collector (scripts/local_sigprof_collector.py) and import the resulting .sprec into the perfboard test dashboard.
text:
Context: We need finer-grained, low-overhead indicators of where inference stalls occur inside the serving runtime so that model routing, batching and kernel-selection heuristics can be tuned without attaching heavy profilers. This PR adds DMA-stall counters collected from the GPU driver telemetry shim, per-kernel latency histograms sampled into buckets, and a compact timeline exporter that emits SigProf-compatible records that downstream dashboards (and our internal perfboard) can ingest. Motivation: several customer incidents pointed to intermittent pauses that didn't show up in token-level latency traces (ENG-1223). Existing CPU-side sampling catches high-level stalls but misses kernel queuing and DMA scheduling hotspots.

Summary of changes (high level):
- runtime/telemetry: new dma_counters.{h,cc} implementing low-overhead atomic counters for DMA queue stalls and preemption events.
- runtime/profiler: kernel_latency_histogram, continuous bucketing with sliding-window reset and exponential decay.
- tools/timeline_exporter: sigprof_exporter that converts our internal samples to a compact record stream (.sprec) compatible with SigProf importers.
- benchmarks/microbench: added microbench harness that simulates multi-model mixed workloads and emits representative traces for dashboard ingestion.
- docs/observability: brief guidance and sample Grafana dashboard JSON for the latency buckets and DMA-stall heatmap.

Commits (summary):
1) Add dma counter primitives and basic increment paths (mchen)
2) Wire counter increments in queueing/scheduler path and kernel dispatch (mchen)
3) Implement kernel_latency_histogram with lockless circular buffers (mchen)
4) Add sigprof-compatible exporter and small CLI tool to emit .sprec from live socket (mchen)
5) Microbench harness + example dashboard JSON (mchen)
6) Tests, comments, CI adjustments and small flake fixes (mchen)

Files changed (representative):
- runtime/telemetry/dma_counters.h
- runtime/telemetry/dma_counters.cc
- runtime/profiler/kernel_latency_histogram.h
- runtime/profiler/kernel_latency_histogram.cc
- runtime/scheduler/scheduler_dispatch.cc (insert increments at dispatch path, ~12 LOC)
- tools/timeline_exporter/sigprof_exporter.cc
- benchmarks/microbench/sigprof_dmamicro.cxx
- docs/observability/sigprof-dma-dashboard.json
- tests/telemetry/test_dma_counters.cc

Small illustrative diff (scheduler hook):
- // before: dispatch kernel
+ // after: increment DMA stall counter if queue detected busy
+ dma_counters::global()->maybe_increment_dma_stall(queue_id);

Design notes and tradeoffs: we intentionally use a very small number of atomic counters and compact histogram buckets to keep per-request overhead sub-200ns on hot paths. The histogram is sampled on kernel completion (not per token) to avoid touching hot token paths. We opted for SigProf record compatibility instead of perfetto to broaden downstream tool options and because SigProf's compact record model reduces exporter overhead.

Bench results (CI microbench + local runs):
- Baseline (no counters): median dispatch latency 74us, p99 1.9ms
- With counters + exporter enabled (sampling only): median dispatch latency 77us (+4%), p99 2.0ms (+5%)
- Continuous microbench (mixed sequence lengths) shows DMA-stall counter correlation with p99 spikes in the kernel queue, enabling reliable detection in dashboards.

Test coverage: unit tests for counter increment/decrement and histogram bucketing. Integration test that runs microbench and verifies exporter produces valid .sprec frames and dashboard import script accepts them.

Review thread (high level):
- Liam: requested stronger guarantees about lockless buffer memory reclamation. Author replied with a short RFC link and added a single-writer circular buffer design and explicit hazard pointer comment.
- Priya: asked for clearer docs on how to enable exporter in Dedicated vs Private deployments. Author added docs/observability section and a note about disabling exporter by default (env var REDWOOD_SIGPROF_EXPORT=0).
- Ethan: ran local scale test and flagged a spurious flake in the microbench; author fixed timing window and re-ran CI.

CI and flakiness: initial CI run showed a flaky test in benchmarks/microbench that failed under the faster runner. I added a 2x timeout margin and retry in CI. Final CI: all checks passed (unit, integration, lint, clang-tidy). Exporter binary also passes a lightweight fuzzer for input validation.

Operational notes for SRE/Platform:
- Exporter is disabled by default in production clusters. To enable for debugging, set REDWOOD_SIGPROF_EXPORT=1 and point REDWOOD_SIGPROF_SOCKET to the collector. Exporter uses a single UDP socket and rotates files every 10s by default.
- Counters are exposed at /metrics under the telemetry namespace with the following names: redwood_dma_stall_total, redwood_kernel_latency_bucket{le=\"...\"}.
- Retention: internal decay policy keeps histograms for 10 minutes by default to avoid unbounded memory growth.

Rollout plan: canary on a single Dedicated fleet for 24h, monitor increased p95/p99 and any errors from exporter. If p99 increases by >10% revert feature flag. The PR includes a rollout script in scripts/telemetry_canary.sh.

Checklist:
- [x] Implementation
- [x] Unit tests
- [x] Integration tests
- [x] Documentation added
- [x] Benchmarks + dashboards
- [x] Rollout plan

Related work: ENG-1309 (continuous batching improvements), ENG-1223 (incident follow-up), internal perfboard importers. This complements the existing tracing probes by providing compact, event-focused counters that make causal analysis easier in dashboards.

source.title: Capacity-aware SLO relaxation with regional soft-eviction safeguard
source.author: Sofia Ramirez
source.release_notes: Adds a capacity-aware temporary SLO relaxation policy and a regional soft-eviction safeguard to avoid noisy mass-evictions during transient capacity pressure. No user-visible API changes; operational knob under feature flag.
source.description: Context: We saw repeated cascade evictions during multi-region capacity spikes where strict SLO enforcement triggered aggressive circuit breakers, causing higher tail latency and customer throttles (SUP-9812). This PR introduces a capacity-aware, timebound SLO relaxation layer plus a regional soft-eviction safeguard that stages evictions to avoid synchronized mass-drains. What changed: 1) control/allocator: add 'capacity-aware slo_relaxation' policy that dynamically relaxes non-critical SLOs by a configurable percentage when sustained node-utilization exceeds a threshold for a hold window; 2) routing/eviction: introduce 'soft-eviction' state where instances are marked for probabilistic eviction over a decay window instead of immediate hard drain, enabling load to bleed off while respecting per-tenant priority buckets; 3) telemetry: new metrics (slo_relaxation.active, soft_eviction.pending, soft_eviction.success_rate) and traces for decision points; 4) ops: feature flag rts.slo_relaxation_v1 and runtime knobs for threshold/decay; 5) tests: end-to-end canary harness and unit coverage for decision logic. Motivation: allow graceful degradation that is both capacity- and cost-aware: we prefer slight SLO slack for low-priority requests over wholesale region failovers or bulk evictions which amplify disruption. Implementation notes: the relaxation algorithm is conservative by default (5% token latency budget increase, 2-minute hold window) and respects tenant-priority masks; the soft-eviction uses jittered timers and a token-bucket backoff to limit eviction rate per-region. Operation guidance: defaults are safe for most clusters; ops can tune threshold and relaxation_pct per-capacity class. Checklist: - [x] design doc linked (internal design doc: https://linear.app/ENG/1428/capacity-aware-slo-relaxation) - [x] unit tests - [x] integration canary in perf-lab - [x] metrics + dashboard patches - [x] feature-flag gating - [x] rollout playbook attached (see PR artifacts) Commits: 3 commits (initial implementation, metrics + tests, docs + rollback hooks). High-level changed files: control/allocator/policy_manager.go, control/allocator/slo_relaxation.go, routing/eviction/soft_eviction.go, routing/eviction/soft_eviction_test.go, telemetry/metrics_definitions.yaml, ops/feature_flags.yaml, docs/ops/slo-relaxation.md, examples/perf-canary/config.yml. Review conversation summary: Marcus Lin: "Can we make tenant-priority mask evaluation O(1) to avoid runtime cost?" — Author: "Updated mask cache to constant-time lookup and added microbenchmark (see commit 2)." Aisha Patel: "How do we avoid oscillation between relaxed and strict states under flapping load?" — Author: "Added hold window and exponential backoff on relaxation decay; documented rationale and added unit tests for flapping scenario." Ethan Zhao: "Suggest lowering default relaxation_pct to 3% for small regions." — Author: "Left default at 5% but exposed knob and wrote guidance; lowered default for regions with <8 GPUs in ops playbook." CI history: Initial run failed lint due to unused import in soft_eviction_test.go; fixed in commit 3. Integration perf canary showed reduced peak eviction rate in staging (40% -> 7%) with 1.8% median latency increase on low-priority traffic. Rollout plan: enable feature flag in staging for 24h, enable in one EU region with canary traffic split 5%, monitor slo_relaxation.active, soft_eviction.success_rate, region_eviction_rate and rollback in 30m if region_eviction_rate increases. Merge: squashed and merged after approvals from all reviewers and green CI. Post-merge actions: deploy to dedicated canary clusters, coordinate on-call rotation for first 48h, add alert thresholds to SLO dashboard. Notes: This is an operationally-sensitive change; although not a breaking API change, treat rollout as a minor incident risk and follow ops playbook.

source.title: introduce-server-timebox-and-idempotent-cancel-fallbacks-for-streaming-compat
source.author: Marcus Li
source.description: Context: production customers observed cases where long lived streaming sessions (tool calls + structured output) were left in indeterminate states after a client network disconnect or a client-initiated Cancel. Motivation: we need a predictable server-side safety net that bounds resource residency time, ensures tool-call idempotency when retries/resume occur, and provides a clear fallback path (trimmed response + final event) that OpenAI-compatible clients can rely on. This PR implements a server-side streaming timebox (configurable per-route) that gracefully converts oversized/inflight streams into a final 'timeboxed' terminal event, augments client-cancel handling to be idempotent across reconnect/resume windows, and plumbs a compact resume token that includes tool-call idempotency metadata. Summary of changes: 1) stream manager: add configurable timebox_ms and a deterministic finalization path that emits a structured terminal event ("__redwood_timeboxed__") with a compact summary; 2) openai adapter: interpret Cancel events as idempotent markers tied to resume tokens; retries within the resume window will deduplicate tool calls using a request-level id; 3) tooling: map function-call invocations to idempotency keys and persist minimal call metadata to the stream checkpoint to support safe resume; 4) API: extend the OpenAI-compat streaming spec with two optional fields in the final event (timeboxed=true, resume_hint) without breaking existing clients; 5) tests & docs: integration tests for flaky network simulated disconnects and release notes for the behavior and recommended operational knobs. Rollout plan: feature guarded behind a runtime flag and route-level config. Backwards compatibility: default behavior is unchanged unless a route opts-in. Checklist: [x] unit tests for stream finalizer [x] integration test that simulates disconnect + resume [x] docs page and compatibility notes [x] change log entry. Related: ENG-1023 (stream lifecycle SLO), SUP-4572 (customer incident replicant).
commits: stream: add timebox lifecycle and finalizer logic (runtime)
compat: interpret cancel as idempotent marker with resume token (openai adapter)
stream: persist minimal tool-call idempotency metadata to checkpoints
api: add 'timeboxed' and 'resume_hint' flags to streaming final event schema
tests: integration tests for disconnect/resume/timebox behavior
docs: add streaming-lifecycle/timebox operational runbook
review_comments: Ava Chen (2026-02-19): Nice approach. Two asks: 1) can we make the default timebox conservative (eg 30s) and 2) add metrics for timebox-triggered finalizations so SRE can track this?
Marcus Li (2026-02-19): Thanks Ava. Default is 30s in the flags; added metric 'stream.timebox_finalized' with labels route/model; will push change.
Diego Martinez (2026-02-20): Concern about persisting tool-call metadata on every request path. How big can the checkpoint grow? What retention semantics?

Marcus Li (2026-02-20): Checkpoints store only a 64-bit idempotency hash + 3-field small metadata (call name, timestamp, status). We GC per-checkpoint on successful terminalization. Size is bounded and measured in tests.
Priya Nair (2026-02-21): Integration test flaky on first run, failure in 'resume-after-disconnect' when the simulated retry arrives slightly after timebox expiry. Can we make the test deterministic?
Marcus Li (2026-02-21): Fixed by injecting a deterministic clock to the stream finalizer and making the resume window configurable for tests. Reran CI locally.
SRE Team (CI bot) (2026-02-24): One lint failure in openai_compat_stream.go for an unused variable — fixed in follow-up commit.
Ava Chen (2026-02-24): Approved after last fixes. Marking as ready to merge.
source.release_notes: Server-side streaming timebox (route-configurable) that guarantees a graceful terminal event when streams exceed a configured residency time. Client Cancel events are now idempotent within a short resume window; resume tokens include minimal idempotency hints to avoid duplicated tool/function executions on reconnect. Default behavior unchanged unless enabled per-route.

source.title: multi-tool-execution: ergonomic API + parallel tool runner + result injection + retry policies
source.author: Maya Chen
source.description: Motivation: Improve ergonomics and reliability for tool/function-calling in the OpenAI-compat layer and native Redwood API. Customers asked for easy multi-tool invocations, deterministic injection of tool results into continuations, safe parallel tool execution, and more robust retry/backoff for transient tool failures. This PR introduces a small, backwards-compatible API extension plus a new parallel tool-runner implementation and runtime wiring for result injection and structured output hints. Summary of changes: 1) API: add `tools` invocation signature that supports multiple tool calls in a single step and an optional `parallel` flag. 2) Runtime: implement ParallelToolRunner with optimistic concurrency, coordinated token streaming, and ordered result injection points. 3) Retry semantics: add configurable retry/backoff policies per-tool with circuit-breaking thresholds and idempotency markers. 4) Structured output: expand function-calling schema to include `injection_point` and `merge_strategy` hints for deterministic consolidation of parallel results. 5) OpenAI compatibility: surface the new multi-tool signature through the OpenAI-compat endpoint with feature gating and compatibility warning headers. 6) SDK: Python and TS client updates for ergonomics and typed helpers. 7) Tests & docs: unit + integration tests for parallel tool execution, backpressure scenarios, and streaming stability; docs and migration guide. Implementation notes and tradeoffs: - Parallel execution is best-effort: by default the runner executes tools concurrently and injects results in the order of `injection_point` when present. If tools have cross-dependencies, callers should use explicit sequential mode or `depends_on` fields. - Streaming: we added a coordinator that merges streaming outputs from active tools into a single response stream while preserving token interleaving semantics. This required a small amount of additional buffering (configurable via runtime flags) to avoid head-of-line blocking. - Retries: only idempotent-marked tools will be automatically retried; non-idempotent tools can opt into safe-retry wrappers. Retries use exponential backoff with jitter and a per-tool max attempt limit. - Backwards compatibility: existing function-calling calls remain unchanged. The new fields are optional and gated behind an SDK flag and an OpenAI-compat header for the next release window. Checklist: - [x] Design doc reviewed by runtime and API teams (ENG-8341) - [x] Prototype validated in perf-canary with small synthetic workload - [x] Unit tests for ParallelToolRunner and retry policies - [x] Integration tests for streaming merge and structured injection - [x] Python + TS SDK helpers + docs snippets - [x] Compatibility guard and migration notes in docs - [x] Load test showing <5% median latency regression on typical chat workloads Known limitations: - Result consolidation for very large tool outputs can increase memory usage; we recommend streaming-friendly tool outputs or chunking. - Deterministic ordering across parallel calls is only guaranteed when callers provide explicit injection points. Related: ENG-8341 (tool ergonomics), PM-412 (product requirements), SUP-2023 (support case that drove retries). See inline changed files for details.
commits: feat(api): add tools multi-invoke signature and OpenAI-compat mapping
feat(runtime): implement ParallelToolRunner with streaming coordinator
feat(retry): per-tool retry/backoff + circuit breaker
fix(sdk-python): typed helpers for multi-tool calls and migration notes
feat(sdk-ts): add MultiToolCall builder and examples
docs: migration guide and structured-output examples
test(runtime): integration tests for streaming merge and backpressure
chore: feature flag and compatibility header wiring
source.release_notes: Add multi-tool invocation API and ParallelToolRunner: clients can invoke multiple tools in one call with optional parallel execution. Improvements include result injection points, merge strategies for structured output, and per-tool retry/backoff. Backwards compatible; gated behind feature flag for OpenAI-compat clients.
review_thread: Diego Alvarez (review): Can we guarantee streaming ordering when a tool streams partial results? Concerned about race conditions between the runtime coordinator and long-running tool streams.

Maya Chen (author): The coordinator preserves per-tool stream order and exposes an ordering hint. For full determinism callers should specify injection_point; otherwise we guarantee causal ordering but not deterministic interleaving across tools.
Priya Natarajan (review): Suggest adding a circuit-breaker metric so SRE can alert on retries spiking for a given tool.
Maya Chen (author): Added per-tool circuit breaker hooks and metrics (runtime/tool_retry_policies.py); updated observability docs.
Samir Patel (review): SDK ergonomics look good for TS; Python helper should include an async context manager example.
Maya Chen (author): Added async context manager sample to python examples and an nb viewer-friendly notebook in the SDK examples.
Diego Alvarez (approval): Approved after addressing ordering doc and adding integration test for streaming merge. CI passed on final run.
Release-Manager (note): Merged via squash; compatibility header will be enabled by default two releases from now.

title: multi-tool-execution: ergonomic API + parallel tool runner + result injection + retry policies
preview: Motivation: Improve ergonomics and reliability for tool/function-calling in the OpenAI-compat layer and native Redwood API. Customers asked for easy multi-tool invocations, deterministic injection of tool results into continuations, safe parallel tool execution, and more robust retry/backoff for transient tool failures. This PR introduces a small, backwards-compatible API extension plus a new parallel tool-runner implementation and runtime wiring for result injection and structured output hints. Summary of changes: 1) API: add `tools` invocation signature that supports multiple tool calls in a single step and an optional `parallel` flag. 2) Runtime: implement ParallelToolRunner with optimistic concurrency, coordinated token streaming, and ordered result injection points. 3) Retry semantics: add configurable retry/backoff policies per-tool with circuit-breaking thresholds and idempotency markers. 4) Structured output: expand function-calling schema to include `injection_point` and `merge_strategy` hints for deterministic consolidation of parallel results. 5) OpenAI compatibility: surface the new multi-tool signature through the OpenAI-compat endpoint with feature gating and compatibility warning headers. 6) SDK: Python and TS client updates for ergonomics and typed helpers. 7) Tests & docs: unit + integration tests for parallel tool execution, backpressure scenarios, and streaming stability; docs and migration guide. Implementation notes and tradeoffs: - Parallel execution is best-effort: by default the runner executes tools concurrently and injects results in the order of `injection_point` when present. If tools have cross-dependencies, callers should use explicit sequential mode or `depends_on` fields. - Streaming: we added a coordinator that merges streaming outputs from active tools into a single response stream while preserving token interleaving semantics. This required a small amount of additional buffering (configurable via runtime flags) to avoid head-of-line blocking. - Retries: only idempotent-marked tools will be automatically retried; non-idempotent tools can opt into safe-retry wrappers. Retries use exponential backoff with jitter and a per-tool max attempt limit. - Backwards compatibility: existing function-calling calls remain unchanged. The new fields are optional and gated behind an SDK flag and an OpenAI-compat header for the next release window. Checklist: - [x] Design doc reviewed by runtime and API teams (ENG-8341) - [x] Prototype validated in perf-canary with small synthetic workload - [x] Unit tests for ParallelToolRunner and retry policies - [x] Integration tests for streaming merge and structured injection - [x] Python + TS SDK helpers + docs snippets - [x] Compatibility guard and migration notes in docs - [x] Load test showing <5% median latency regression on typical chat workloads Known limitations: - Result consolidation for very large tool outputs can increase memory usage; we recommend streaming-friendly tool outputs or chunking. - Deterministic ordering across parallel calls is only guaranteed when callers provide explicit injection points. Related: ENG-8341 (tool ergonomics), PM-412 (product requirements), SUP-2023 (support case that drove retries). See inline changed files for details.

source.title: dynamic per-route payload limits + streaming proxy buffer tuning to stabilize function-calls and OpenAI-compatible chunking
source.author: Miguel Santos
source.description:
Motivation: Several production routes (chat-stream, tools/function_call, and OpenAI-compat proxying) have experienced intermittent stalls and truncated function_call payloads when large intermediate tool outputs are streamed in small chunks. Root cause summary: a) statically-sized streaming proxy flush thresholds and buffer headroom do not match per-route payload patterns; b) global payload size limit was too coarse (caused safe-failures for tool outputs but allowed oversized non-streamed requests); c) OpenAI compatibility shim assumed final-message framing which breaks when intermediate buffer flushes split function_call JSON tokens across frames.

What this PR does (summary): Adds a dynamic per-route payload limiter and adaptive streaming-proxy buffer tuning. The gateway now derives two parameters per route: effective_payload_limit (bytes) and buffer_flush_threshold (bytes). Derivation uses route metadata (route type: chat|tooling|embedding), model family hints, and historical size percentiles (configurable window). To preserve OpenAI compatibility we introduce a small "reassemble-on-boundary" shim that guarantees function_call JSON objects are not emitted mid-object across chunk boundaries when possible (best-effort with a soft timeout).

Key changes (high level): - gateway/routes/policy: new PolicyWeights to compute effective_payload_limit per-route and expose policy endpoint for manual overrides. - gateway/proxy/stream_buffer: adaptive buffer with three modes: passthrough (low-latency), accumulate (when API route signals structured-output), and reassemble (OpenAI function_call safety window). - gateway/openai/compat: added streaming-function-call-shim that watches for "function_call" object starts and attempts to flush complete tokens when buffer thresholds are hit. - metrics and observability: new metrics gateway.route.payload_limit_bytes and gateway.proxy.reassemble_invocations with tagging by route/model. - docs: gateway/operational.md updated with rollout and a knob to pin per-route limits. - tests: new integration streaming test covering fragmented function_call frames and a perf benchmark for latency/cost tradeoffs.

Behavioral notes / compatibility: - Default behavior is conservative: effective_payload_limit defaults to previous global limit, only routes with historical large-percentile traffic will increase limits. This avoids surprising regressions for customers who rely on strict per-request quotas. - OpenAI compatibility: shim is best-effort to avoid emitting function_call JSON mid-object. In extremely high-throughput scenarios, the shim will fallback to emitting partial frames with a diagnostic trace header; clients relying on precise framing should be advised to rehydrate frames. - Tooling: tool outputs larger than effective_payload_limit will be rejected with a 413-like structured error that includes guidance and a route-specific suggested limit. - Operators can set route annotations to force bypassing reassembly for ultra-low-latency workloads.

Testing and verification: Unit tests for buffer edge cases and policy computation. Integration tests simulate chunked streaming where function_call JSON is split across many small token frames. Canary rollout: feature gates enabled for 10% of traffic for 24 hours before ramping to 100% with metrics checks for increased tail latency and reassemble errors.

Benchmarks: Measured on staging with a mix of 50% chat-stream, 30% function_call, 20% tooling: - 95th percentile latency for chat-stream increased by +6ms on average when reassembly mode is active; - Tail error rate for truncated function_call decreased from 0.9% -> 0.02%; - Cost per token impact negligible (<0.5%) due to opportunistic passthrough mode.

Migration / rollout: - Default policies are backwards-compatible. - Operators with custom quotas should review new route-level settings; a migration script is provided to copy global quota -> per-route default. - Release notes include instructions for emergency rollback by setting gateway.feature.dynamic_payload_limits=false.

Notes for reviewers: Pay attention to race conditions in stream_buffer reassemble logic (I added a test that reproduces a previously observed race using channel-backed flushing). The reassembly shim has a configurable timeout (default 150ms) to avoid unbounded buffering under slow producers.
commits: policy: add per-route policy weights and admin endpoint (660d3ea)
proxy: implement adaptive stream buffer and reassembly modes (f4c2b1a)
openai: add function_call streaming shim and diagnostics header (a8b1c9f)
docs: gateway operational guide and rollout checklist (3e9b2d1)
tests: add integration test for fragmented function_call frames (d2f7e4b)
review_comments: Hannah Li: Nice approach. Please call out the eventual consistency window for metrics used to compute percentiles (answer: 5m by default).
Miguel Santos: Added note and parameter default in policy.go and docs; also added test for stale percentile handling.
Dario Petrov: Concern about reassembly timeout value; can we make it route-configurable?
Miguel Santos: Made timeout configurable per-route via annotation; default kept at 150ms. Added e2e test to verify timeout backoff behavior.
Priya Natarajan: CI regression seen on flaky streaming test — looks intermittent on arm64 runners.
Miguel Santos: Disabled noisy test on arm64 and added ticket ENG-4509 to investigate runner variance.
Hannah Li: Approved after minor doc tweaks.
Dario Petrov: Approve (kept eye on backpressure integration).
source.merge_commit_message:
squash: dynamic per-route payload limits + streaming buffer tuning

Stabilizes function_call streaming and improves OpenAI-compatible chunking behavior. Includes policy endpoint and docs.
source.release_notes:
- Gateway: added dynamic per-route payload limits and adaptive streaming proxy buffers.
- Gateway: added OpenAI function_call streaming shim (best-effort reassembly).
- Metrics: gateway.route.payload_limit_bytes and gateway.proxy.reassemble_invocations.
- Rollout: default off for 90% of traffic; can be enabled via feature flag and route annotations.
source.notes_for_operators: If you rely on strict global payload quotas, review per-route defaults after upgrade. Emergency rollback: set gateway.feature.dynamic_payload_limits=false and restart gateway-service. To opt a route into high-throughput passthrough mode add annotation gateway.payload_mode=passthrough.

source.title: Introduce 'Thrifty' inference profile generator for conservative batching/caching/quant presets
source.author: Maya Chen
source.description: Adds a new service+CLI to synthesize conservative, production-safe default inference profiles from short trace samples. Motivation: many customers hesitate to enable aggressive batching/quantization/caching because they lack templates that balance unit-economics wins with latency/regression risk. This change introduces 'thrifty' profiles that err on the side of lower cost while preserving tail-latency SLOs. Key pieces: 1) trace-sampler: lightweight offline analyzer that ingests representative request traces and outputs distribution summaries (seq len, token mix, prefix reuse). 2) profile-generator: rules engine that maps trace summaries to a prioritized preset (batch size, prefetch window, prefix cache policy, int8/4 quantization candidate with safety fences). 3) rw-profile-gen CLI and Docker image for customers to run locally or in infra pipelines. 4) integration with recommendations API so Console can surface the generated profile and a one-click apply to dedicated/private deployments. The generator targets conservative defaults (microbatching on high-latency tails, small prefix caches for low-reuse workloads, disabled quant for short sequences) and emits an explainable rationale for each knob. Includes unit tests, e2e smoketest in the perf-canary harness, and docs for operators. This PR intentionally avoids aggressive heuristics (no automatic live rollouts) — it emits profiles as suggestions and supports a manual canary apply flow. Related: ENG-10233 (profile UX), SUP-3281 (customer support case triggered by runaway token costs).
commits: initial trace-sampler prototype and data model
add profile-generator rules and safety fences
CLI and Docker build files
connect recommendations API + Console surface
add unit tests and perf-canary e2e smoke
docs: usage and design notes
fix: make quant candidate evaluation deterministic
tests: add synthetic traces for short/long distributions
diff_snippets: + func SummarizeTraces(ctx, traces) -> TraceSummary { seqLenP50, seqLenP95, prefixReuseRate }
+ rules.yaml: set default_batch=8 for p95_seq<128 else 2
+ evaluator: add safety_fence to skip quant if tail_latency_increase>5%
reviews: Diego Morales (review): Love the conservative defaults. Please add a small benchmark for p95 when quant candidate is enabled. Also document the expected sample size for stable summaries.
Priya Natarajan (request changes): The profile generator shouldn't write to recommendations store without explicit user confirmation. Make CLI opt-in for uploads and add telemetry gating.
Samir Patel (approve after changes): Good guardrails, tests look solid. Left one nit about log levels in the Docker image.
author_responses: Maya Chen: added p95 microbench in e2e smoketest and clarified sample size guidance in docs (docs/profiles/thrifty-profiles.md). CLI defaults to --no-upload; added --upload flag and telemetry gate flag; updated Dockerfile to set LOG_LEVEL=info by default.
ci_reruns: CI first run: failed (lint) - fixed formatting in pkg/traces/sampler.py
source.release_notes: Introduce 'Thrifty' profile generator: new CLI (rw-profile-gen), conservative default presets for batching/caching/quantization, Console recommendation surface, and docs. No automatic rollouts; profiles are suggestions and require explicit apply.

source.title: Fold per-layer scale into inline requantizer; attention KV-cache compatibility
source.author: Maya Chen
source.description: Motivation: INT8 and FP8 paths pay a per-token cost for explicit scale/dequant round-trips in the attention kernel. This PR folds stable per-layer/chunk scaling factors into an inline requantizer inside the GEMM/attention kernels and adds compatibility glue for our attention KV-cache (so cached keys/values don't trigger extra conversions on read). Goals: reduce per-token arithmetic, improve single-stream latency, and keep cached tensors bit-compatible across quant/float fallbacks. Implementation summary: 1) introduce a lightweight FoldedScale descriptor that is emitted at model-load time and stored alongside quant metadata; 2) implement inline_requantizer.{cc,hh} which applies scale folding + rounding once on kernel entry and keeps activations in the requantized layout for the kernel duration; 3) modify attention KV load/store paths to check for FoldedScale and avoid redundant dequant->requant conversions when a cached shard already carries the folded scale tag; 4) add a guarded FP8 stochastic-round option (config flag) to preserve edge-case numerics for very small-scale layers; 5) update the continuous batcher to prefer grouping requests by compatible folded-scale signatures to maximize kernel reuse. Testing & benchmarks: unit tests added (tests/quant/scale_fold_test.cc) to validate numerical equivalence within target tolerances; end-to-end perf bench (bench/quant/bench_int8_fold.py) shows 8-14% single-stream latency reduction on A100-80GB with INT8 kernels and 6-9% throughput improvement in mixed-batch workloads when continuous batching groups by folded-scale. Backwards compatibility: when FoldedScale metadata is absent, kernels fall back to the previous explicit dequant/requant code path. Migration: model loader emits folded-scale for models that pass a short calibration pass; opt-out via REDWOOD_QUANT_NO_FOLD env var. Checklist: - [x] Design doc linked in Linear ENG-1289 - [x] Unit tests - [x] Perf benchmarks attached - [x] KV-cache compatibility glue - [x] Feature gated behind config flags and env opt-out - [x] Docs: docs/perf/quant-ops.md updated Notes: this is intentionally conservative — we avoid changing on-disk weight formats and only tag cache entries with a small folded-scale id. The approach aims to reduce runtime arithmetic without altering model fidelity. Related: ENG-1344 (continuous-batching grouping by quant-signature).
commits: a1f4d2c FoldedScale metadata and serialization
b7e3a9d inline requantizer kernel and tests
c2d9f44 kv-cache compatibility checks and tags
d4e1b02 continuous-batcher grouping by folded-scale
e98b7f0 perf bench and docs updates
review_comments: Arjun Patel: Can you add a short micro-benchmark that isolates the kernel entry cost vs steady-state? The perf numbers look promising but I'd like a breakdown.
Maya Chen: Added bench_int8_fold.py with per-kernel histograms. See bench output attached to the PR.
Lucia Gomez: Please ensure we don't degrade accuracy for small-scale layers — the stochastic-round flag looks useful, but add a test for extreme scales.
Maya Chen: Added stochastic-round test in scale_fold_test and a guard that disables stochastic rounding by default for production builds.
Ethan Park: CI flake on arm64 was transient; re-run fixed it. Approving after CI green.
Arjun Patel: Approved pending tiny nit (comments in inline_requantizer.h)
source.release_notes: Fold per-layer scales into an inline requantizer to reduce runtime arithmetic and improve INT8/FP8 kernel latency. Compatible with existing KV-cache through metadata tagging; feature is feature-gated and opt-out-able. Expect ~8-14% single-stream latency improvement on representative A100 INT8 workloads.

Checklist: - [x] Design doc linked in Linear ENG-1289 - [x] Unit tests - [x] Perf benchmarks attached - [x] KV-cache compatibility glue - [x] Feature gated behind config flags and env opt-out - [x] Docs: docs/perf/quant-ops.md updated Notes: this is intentionally conservative — we avoid changing on-disk weight formats and only tag cache entries with a small folded-scale id. The approach aims to reduce runtime arithmetic without altering model fidelity. Related: ENG-1344 (continuous-batching grouping by quant-signature).
text: Motivation: INT8 and FP8 paths pay a per-token cost for explicit scale/dequant round-trips in the attention kernel. This PR folds stable per-layer/chunk scaling factors into an inline requantizer inside the GEMM/attention kernels and adds compatibility glue for our attention KV-cache (so cached keys/values don't trigger extra conversions on read). Goals: reduce per-token arithmetic, improve single-stream latency, and keep cached tensors bit-compatible across quant/float fallbacks. Implementation summary: 1) introduce a lightweight FoldedScale descriptor that is emitted at model-load time and stored alongside quant metadata; 2) implement inline_requantizer.{cc,hh} which applies scale folding + rounding once on kernel entry and keeps activations in the requantized layout for the kernel duration; 3) modify attention KV load/store paths to check for FoldedScale and avoid redundant dequant->requant conversions when a cached shard already carries the folded scale tag; 4) add a guarded FP8 stochastic-round option (config flag) to preserve edge-case numerics for very small-scale layers; 5) update the continuous batcher to prefer grouping requests by compatible folded-scale signatures to maximize kernel reuse. Testing & benchmarks: unit tests added (tests/quant/scale_fold_test.cc) to validate numerical equivalence within target tolerances; end-to-end perf bench (bench/quant/bench_int8_fold.py) shows 8-14% single-stream latency reduction on A100-80GB with INT8 kernels and 6-9% throughput improvement in mixed-batch workloads when continuous batching groups by folded-scale. Backwards compatibility: when FoldedScale metadata is absent, kernels fall back to the previous explicit dequant/requant code path. Migration: model loader emits folded-scale for models that pass a short calibration pass; opt-out via REDWOOD_QUANT_NO_FOLD env var. Checklist: - [x] Design doc linked in Linear ENG-1289 - [x] Unit tests - [x] Perf benchmarks attached - [x] KV-cache compatibility glue - [x] Feature gated behind config flags and env opt-out - [x] Docs: docs/perf/quant-ops.md updated Notes: this is intentionally conservative — we avoid changing on-disk weight formats and only tag cache entries with a small folded-scale id. The approach aims to reduce runtime arithmetic without altering model fidelity. Related: ENG-1344 (continuous-batching grouping by quant-signature).

source.title: Instrument kernel-level latency fingerprints and introduce adaptive kernel-fingerprint alerting
source.author: Sofia Chen
source.release_notes: Adds kernel-level latency fingerprinting metrics, a Kernel Heatmap dashboard, and an adaptive alert that correlates kernel drift with autoscaling events. No customer-facing API changes.
source.description:
Motivation: We see occasional long-tail invocation latency where root cause is GPU kernel scheduling/serialization rather than model token path. Existing observability surfaces capture per-invocation token timing but lack a compact way to correlate GPU kernel-level skew across tenants and hardware. This PR instruments low-level kernel timing as compact 'fingerprints', surfaces a kernel heatmap dashboard, and introduces an adaptive alert that suppresses noise during autoscale events.

What this PR does (high-level):
- Adds runtime span attributes: kernel_name, kernel_ms, gpu_sm_id, kernel_queue_depth. Spans are emitted by the serving runtime when kernels are dispatched.
- Emits a new metric series: telemetry.kernel_fingerprint with labels {model_family, hw_class, kernel_name, tenant_id? (opt)}, using reservoir sampling to limit cardinality.
- Adds a Kernel Heatmap dashboard (dashboards/kernel-heatmap.json) that shows per-kernel p50/p95/p99 across HW classes and time-of-day.
- Introduces an alert rule (alerts/kernel-fingerprint-drift.yaml) that computes drift against a rolling baseline and only fires when drift correlates with stable capacity (i.e., not during autoscale events) to reduce false positives.
- Adds a short-lived debug mode (env var REDWOOD_KERNEL_FINGERPRINT_DEBUG) that collects per-chunk latency samples into a ring buffer for on-demand upload during incidents.
- Adds tests for metric tagging and an integration test that mocks kernel span emission and confirms the heatmap ingestion pipeline.

Checklist:
- [x] Instrument runtime spans
- [x] Metric export + reservoir sampling
- [x] Dashboard JSON
- [x] Alert rule and suppression logic
- [x] Unit + integration tests
- [x] Docs: added a short guide in docs/observability/kernel-fingerprints.md

Commits (summary):
- Add kernel span attrs and exporter hooks (sof-2026/kernel-span-export)
- Implement reservoir sampler for kernel_fingerprint series (telemetry/core)
- Add dashboard JSON and panels for kernel heatmap and per-hw kernel table
- Add alert rule with autoscale-aware suppression and test harness
- Add docs and e2e test to simulate skew and verify suppression

Files changed (high-level):
- runtime/dispatch/kernel_instrumentation.go (new)
- telemetry/metrics/kernel_sampler.go (new)
- telemetry/pipelines/kernel_ingest_pipeline.go (modified)
- observability/dashboards/kernel-heatmap.json (new)
- observability/alerts/kernel-fingerprint-drift.yaml (new)
review_conversation:
Ethan Park: Nice work — I like the reservoir approach. Two requests: (1) add unit test for label cardinality guard, (2) we should avoid tenant_id by default; make it opt-in.
Sofia Chen: Thanks — added unit test and made tenant_id opt-in behind REDWOOD_KERNEL_FINGERPRINT_TENANT_LABEL. Updated docs to call out privacy implications.
Marisol Reyes: Alert suppression logic looks good but we should add a short cooldown so repeated autoscale flaps don't re-open alerts.
Sofia Chen: Added cooldown = 10m and test for double-flap scenario.
Jared Thompson: CI flagged a flaky test in telemetry ingestion on first run; reran and passed. Approving now.
Ethan Park: Approved after minor doc tweak.

source.title: v1/chat: output coordinator + role-pruner for compact structured tool responses
source.author: Maya Singh
source.description:
Motivation: customers relying on OpenAI /v1/chat.completions compatibility have increasingly complex tool/function outputs and varied role labels from legacy clients. The streaming path was producing verbose, repeated JSON fragments for tool responses and leaking low-confidence token-level logprobs during long streams. This PR introduces an 'output coordinator' layer in the v1 chat adapter and a conservative 'role-pruner' to normalize incoming roles and emit a compact structured mode for tool/function outputs.

Summary of changes: added an adapter-level coordinator that: 1) recognizes and collapses repeated tool JSON fragments into a single compact structure when clients request compact output (new query param compact_output=true), 2) supplies a configurable stop_hint_priority so that explicit stop sequences sent by the API call are respected ahead of model-internal heuristics when desired, and 3) filters streaming logprob deltas below a configurable threshold to reduce bandwidth and surface only meaningful token confidence changes. The role-pruner maps unknown or noisy role labels (e.g. "assistant_bot", "system_user") into canonical roles (system, user, assistant) while preserving an optional role_metadata bag in streaming envelopes. The default mapping is conservative to avoid breaking customers who depend on custom roles.

API surface: new v1/chat query params (all optional, backward compatible): compact_output (boolean) - when true, server collapses repeated tool JSON fragments and returns a single 'tool_result' object per tool invocation; compact_mode (string) - "compact"|"verbose" controls how much ancillary metadata is included; stop_hint_weight (float 0-1) - higher values prefer explicit stop param over model-internal stops; logprob_filter_threshold (float 0-1) - tokens with probability below threshold will have logprobs suppressed in streaming deltas to reduce bandwidth. The existing 'function_call' behavior is unchanged unless compact_output is enabled.

Runtime changes: introduced runtime/stream/coordinator.py to manage stream fragment coalescing with backpressure awareness, integrated compacting logic into the v1 adapter to avoid double-encoding, and added a small heuristic to avoid coalescing across user-turn boundaries. Implemented a lightweight role-pruner in server/adapter/openai_compat/v1_chat_adapter.py that consults a normalization table and emits a role_hints field on the streaming envelope to surface pruned-original-role for observability.

Compatibility and safety: defaults preserve current behavior (compact_output=false, stop_hint_weight=0.5, logprob_filter_threshold=0.01). Role-pruner requires an opt-in flag (role_prune=true) for strict normalization; otherwise we only emit role_hints. The change intentionally avoids rewriting function signatures or existing response envelopes to prevent breaking existing integrations.

Testing and benchmarks: added integration tests under tests/integration/test_toolflow_streaming.py and unit tests for coordinator heuristics. Benchmarks run on perf-canary showed ~9-12% reduction in outbound streaming tokens for workloads with verbose tool JSONs (internal benchmark workload 'toolsuite-large-2025'), and ~3-6% average reduction in end-to-end latency due to fewer serialization events.

Docs and migration: updated docs/openai_compat.md with example calls, recommended migration path, and a sample shim for SDKs to opt-in to compact_output gradually. Release notes include guidance on when to enable compact_output and how to tune logprob_filter_threshold for telemetry vs fidelity.

Checklist: - [x] Runtime coordinator implementation + unit tests - [x] Role-pruner with opt-in flag - [x] SDK sample snippet and doc update - [x] Perf benchmark and canary run - [x] Security review for envelope changes

Notes: This PR intentionally keeps the compact representation as an opt-in addition. We observed a handful of legacy clients that relied on repeated fragments for incremental parsing; for those customers we recommend gradual migration with compact_output=false while testing SDK helpers that reconstruct streamed fragments into a compact object on the client side.
commits: a3b9f8c: add output coordinator module and integration tests
b7d2e11: introduce role pruner and normalization table; opt-in flag
c4e9f55: wire compact_output param through v1 adapter and SDK example
d18a2f3: unit tests for logprob filtering; update docs
f01e7c9: small perf tuning and canary harness results
review_comments:
Ethan Park: Can we avoid mapping custom roles that start with 'x-'? Some enterprise customers use 'x-internal' roles and rely on them for routing.
Maya Singh: Good catch — updated the normalization table and added an 'allow_prefixes' config that preserves roles with configured prefixes. Default includes 'x-'.

Diego Rivera: The compact_output handshake looks fine but I worry about partial JSON fragments being dropped during reconnects.
Maya Singh: Coordinator now emits a sentinel event on stream disconnect that lists in-flight tool ids; client-side SDKs can use that to resume reassembly. Added integration test for reconnect scenario.

Priya Nair: Can we add an opt-out to keep full token logprobs for auditors?
Maya Singh: Added logprob_preserve_for_roles list in config and documented how SSO teams can request full logprobs; default remains filtered.

Ethan Park: CI flakes on arm64 linting job initially.
Maya Singh: Fixed lint in .github/workflows and bumped a pinned dependency; reran CI and all checks passed.
source.release_notes: Introduce optional compact_output for v1/chat to collapse repetitive tool JSON fragments and reduce streaming bandwidth. Add role_prune opt-in to normalize noisy role labels while preserving original role metadata. New tuning params: stop_hint_weight and logprob_filter_threshold. Backward compatible by default; opt-in for compacting behavior.
