source.title: generate delta upgrade index and SBOM stamping for private-installer CI
source.author: Ethan Park
source.description: This PR adds a CI-first pipeline to produce deterministic delta upgrade packages and stamp SBOM/provenance metadata into the private-installer release artifacts. Motivation: customers running Private (VPC/on-prem) need compact upgrade bundles and a verifiable upgrade graph so operators can perform safe roll-forwards and rollbacks without re-pulling full images. The output of the pipeline is a signed delta-index (delta-index.json) that expresses available upgrade paths between installer versions and references per-artifact SBOMs, Helm chart lockfiles, and Terraform module plan snapshots. Changes include: 1) new pipeline job (private-installer:delta-pack) that builds compact tarballs containing only changed manifests and image digests; 2) delta-indexer tool (tools/release/delta-indexer.go) which computes graph edges and emits SBOM references; 3) SBOM stamping step using syft and a lightweight metadata envelope; 4) cosign/cosign-wrapper integration to attach OCI signatures and timestamped provenance to each delta bundle; 5) Helm chart lockfile generator (charts/lockfiles/) and Terraform module plan snapshotter (terraform/modules/plan-snapshots/); 6) makefile and CI matrix additions to validate deltas with a staging smoke deploy and terraform plan dry-run. Checklist: - [x] implement delta-index format and generator - [x] wire up CI job and pipeline config - [x] add SBOM generation and envelope signing - [x] add unit tests for indexer and SBOM parsing - [x] add e2e smoke test in staging workflow - [x] document format and CLI usage in docs/release/delta-index.md Backwards compatibility: existing full-installer artifacts and release flow are unchanged; the delta-index is additive and consumed by the private-installer CLI/upgrade controller. Note on security: SBOMs are generated with syft v0.83.0 and emitted alongside artifacts; cosign keys are stored in the CI KMS and rotated per release cadence. See linked Linear tickets for rollout plan and gating criteria.
commits: a1b2c3: add delta-indexer tool and unit tests
d4e5f6: CI job private-installer:delta-pack and matrix entries
b7c8d9: helm chart lockfile generator and lockfiles for v1.12
e0f1a2: terraform plan snapshotter and example snapshots
c3d4e5: sbom stamping step and cosign wrapper integration
f6a7b8: docs: add delta-index spec and examples
9a0b1c: tests: add staging smoke deploy and terraform dry-run check
conversation: Alice Nguyen (reviewer): Left a few nits on error messages in delta-indexer.go; please check multi-arch image digest handling.
Ethan Park (author): addressed nits and added explicit tests for amd64/arm64 digest selection in commit b7c8d9.
Marco Ruiz (reviewer): requested CI job to publish unsigned staging artifacts for manual validation before cosign step -- added a conditional publish step.
Priya Shah (security): asked for SBOM tool version pin and signature envelope format verifier. Added syft v0.83.0 pin and envelope verification unit test.
Ben Cohen (approver): Verified staging smoke deploy on us-west-2; approved after 2 successful CI runs.
Zoe Kim (approver): Approved — suggested follow-up to add a delta retention policy for old bundle GC (tracked in ENG-5923).
source.release_notes: Introduce delta-index (delta-index.json) for private-installer releases: smaller upgrade bundles, SBOMs per delta, signed provenance, Helm/Terraform lockfiles. Operators can use the index to compute safe upgrade paths and perform rollbacks more quickly.
source.notes_for_ops: To consume deltas, upgrade private-installer CLI >= v0.23 which understands delta-index.json. CI publishes both full and delta artifacts; by default the installer will prefer delta bundles when ancestry is available. For emergency rollback, use --force-apply with a specific delta bundle digest from the index.
source.testing_summary: Unit tests added for delta graph computation and SBOM envelope parsing. CI runs: 2x matrix builds (linux/amd64 linux/arm64), staging smoke deploy succeeded, terraform dry-run validated. Flaky test (e2e network teardown) was re-run and passed on the second attempt.
source.risks_and_mitigation: Risk: consumers may accidentally apply a partial delta for mismatched base versions. Mitigation: delta-index includes base-version constraint checks and the installer will refuse mismatched deltas. CI includes a gating smoke deploy and signature verification to prevent unsigned deltas from reaching stable. Added audit log events for delta apply/rollback.
follow_up_tasks: ENG-5923: implement delta bundle GC and retention policy
ENG-6001: integrate delta-index consumption into private-installer operator
PM-1234: add delta visualization to Console release page
source.notes: Merged after CI green and approvals. Deployment: no immediate rollout to production; release manager will tag the first delta-enabled release after coordinated customer communications.

text: This PR adds a CI-first pipeline to produce deterministic delta upgrade packages and stamp SBOM/provenance metadata into the private-installer release artifacts. Motivation: customers running Private (VPC/on-prem) need compact upgrade bundles and a verifiable upgrade graph so operators can perform safe roll-forwards and rollbacks without re-pulling full images. The output of the pipeline is a signed delta-index (delta-index.json) that expresses available upgrade paths between installer versions and references per-artifact SBOMs, Helm chart lockfiles, and Terraform module plan snapshots. Changes include: 1) new pipeline job (private-installer:delta-pack) that builds compact tarballs containing only changed manifests and image digests; 2) delta-indexer tool (tools/release/delta-indexer.go) which computes graph edges and emits SBOM references; 3) SBOM stamping step using syft and a lightweight metadata envelope; 4) cosign/cosign-wrapper integration to attach OCI signatures and timestamped provenance to each delta bundle; 5) Helm chart lockfile generator (charts/lockfiles/) and Terraform module plan snapshotter (terraform/modules/plan-snapshots/); 6) makefile and CI matrix additions to validate deltas with a staging smoke deploy and terraform plan dry-run. Checklist: - [x] implement delta-index format and generator - [x] wire up CI job and pipeline config - [x] add SBOM generation and envelope signing - [x] add unit tests for indexer and SBOM parsing - [x] add e2e smoke test in staging workflow - [x] document format and CLI usage in docs/release/delta-index.md Backwards compatibility: existing full-installer artifacts and release flow are unchanged; the delta-index is additive and consumed by the private-installer CLI/upgrade controller. Note on security: SBOMs are generated with syft v0.83.0 and emitted alongside artifacts; cosign keys are stored in the CI KMS and rotated per release cadence. See linked Linear tickets for rollout plan and gating criteria.

source.title: weighted-queue-and-kv-pin-timeouts-to-mitigate-starvation
source.author: Maya Chen
source.release_notes: Improves continuous-batcher fairness and prevents long-tail starvation by introducing a weighted queue throttler and KV-cache pin expiry tuning. No API changes; rollout controlled by feature flag.
source.description: Context: Production traces and perf canaries exposed recurring tail-latency regressions under mixed workloads with hot and cold tenants. Root cause analysis showed that the continuous batching scheduler's FIFO-dominant queue allowed small high-priority requests to be repeatedly overtaken when the runtime favored large batched prefixes; simultaneously the KV cache had a long pin expiry that amplified hot-key monopolization. This PR introduces two coordinated changes: 1) a lightweight weighted-queue throttler in the continuous batcher that enforces service-share weights at queue admission time (soft scheduling), and 2) adjustable KV-cache pin timeouts and a small-metadata eviction hint to reduce long-running pin monopolies. Design: - Admissions now record a per-queue weight token and a sliding-window deficit counter; long-running peers lose admission weight over configurable windows to prevent starvation. - The scheduler still batches by model/seq-length compatibility, but before combining candidates we apply a fast O(1) fairness filter that reorders a small prefix of the queue based on deficit and age. This preserves batching efficiency for the common case while bounding unfairness. - KV cache: introduce per-tenant pin_ttl (default lowered from 30s -> 8s) and a 'soft-evict' hint that allows cold-key compaction during sustained high memory pressure. - Feature gate: both features are behind runtime flags (batcher.weighted_queue=true, kv.pin_adaptive=true) and can be tuned via config map in Dedicated/Private. Implementation notes: - runtime/scheduler/continuous_batcher.rs: added WeightedQueue struct, deficit accounting, admission helpers, unit tests for corner cases. - runtime/scheduler/queue.rs: added peek-and-reorder primitive with micro-optimizations to avoid allocations. - runtime/kv_cache/pinning.rs: introduced pin_ttl, soft_evict hints, metrics for pin_count and pin_ttl_hist. - tests/scheduler_integration.rs: new deterministic multi-tenant load harness and long-tail assertion checks. - docs/ops/continuous-batcher-tuning.md: operator-facing notes and suggested knob values for common workloads. Performance summary: - Canary synthetic workload (10 tenants; mix heavy/light): P95 request latency reduced by 22% for light tenants and P99 reduced by 14% overall when feature gate enabled; end-to-end throughput unchanged within 3%. - Microbench: weighted admission adds ~150ns median overhead per admission check; observed <1% CPU cost at 100k qps. Testing and rollout: - Unit tests added for deficit accounting, corner cases for zero-weight tenants, and KV pin expiry behaviour. - Integration canary run in staging for 48h before merge. - Rollout plan: enable behind feature gate for 10% of Dedicated clusters, observe tail metrics for 72h, then ramp to 100% with monitor playbook. Backwards compatibility: defaults preserve existing behavior (feature gates off). Related work: builds on ENG-7120 (continuous-batcher refactor) and pairs with upcoming kernel-level scheduling hints (ENG-8350).

7f9b1d0: add peek-and-reorder primitive to queue module; micro-optimizations (queue.rs)
e3d9a8f: introduce KV pin ttl and soft-evict hint with metrics (kv_cache/pinning.rs)
4b2e0c1: add integration harness and deterministic multi-tenant tests (tests/scheduler_integration.rs)
a6c5f7b: docs: ops tuning guide and rollout playbook (docs/ops/continuous-batcher-tuning.md)
review_comments: Diego Alvarez (2026-02-19): Comment: The deficit window constant seems conservative; did you try a 2s window instead of 5s? Also please add unit tests for zero-weight tenants.

Maya Chen (2026-02-19): Reply: I tried 2s in the microbench but saw slightly more reordering churn causing marginal batch inefficiency; 5s is a good middle ground but made configurable. I added tests for zero-weight tenants (see tests commit).
Priya Natarajan (2026-02-20): Comment: KV pin ttl default reduction looks large. Any regression risk for hot-key throughput? Please include metrics and an optional override per-tenant.
Maya Chen (2026-02-20): Reply: We included per-tenant override; microbench shows hot-key throughput <2% change at 8s default. Added metrics pin_ttl_hist and pin_count for monitoring (prometheus).
Liam O'Reilly (2026-02-21): Request changes: please add a long-running canary result snippet for mixed seq-length workloads and document the feature flags in the helm chart values.yaml.
Maya Chen (2026-02-22): Update: added canary dashboard screenshots and values.yaml example in docs. Re-ran integration canary for 48h; appended results to PR description.
Diego Alvarez (2026-02-23): Approval: LGTM after nits addressed. Priya approved after verifying canary graphs. Merging once CI passes.
ci_checks: perf-e2e: pass (P95 regression < -20% for light tenants)
source.merge_commit_message: squash: weighted queue & kv-pin: mitigate batcher starvation (#31247)
source.rollout_notes: Feature gated. Default off. Suggested default knobs: batcher.weighted_queue.window=5000ms, batcher.weighted_queue.prefix_size=8, kv.pin_ttl.default=8000ms. Start with 10% Dedicated, monitor P95/P99 per-tenant for 72h.

source.title: fp8-autotune-dequant-rewrite-and-kernel-router
source.author: Ethan Park
source.description:
Motivation: We observed cache-sensitive and sequence-length-dependent regressions when serving FP8/INT8 model variants on the high-throughput path. Two root causes surfaced in profiling: (1) redundant dequantization stubs inserted by the compiler leading to extra memory traffic across the KV cache boundary, and (2) suboptimal kernel selection for mixed FP8/INT8 attention matmuls at mid-sequence lengths, which increases both latency tail and per-token GPU utilization. This PR introduces an autotuned FP8 dequant rewrite pass (dequant-rewriter) plus a scheduler-aware kernel router that picks kernels based on runtime signals (sequence position, KV cache hit pattern, and batching profile).

Summary of changes:
- Implement dequant-rewriter: a graph-level pass that coalesces adjacent dequant ops, hoists dequantization out of hot KV cache paths when safe, and emits a fused "dequant+transpose" kernel where applicable. The pass supports conservative aliasing checks and a feature-flagged faster mode that assumes stable KV shapes in Dedicated deployments.
- FP8 autotuner: offline calibration tool and lightweight runtime probe that chooses between FP8-fused kernels, hybrid FP8/INT8 kernels, or fallback FP16 kernels per model and per-device. Tuner's decisions are persisted in model-serving metadata and can be overridden by rollout flags.
- Kernel router: scheduler integration that consumes continuous-batching statistics (batch size distribution, arrival jitter) and KV cache telemetry to choose kernel variants at request-time. Router minimizes kernel switches within a batch and prefers kernels that maintain low memory traffic across KV boundaries.
- Scheduler hints: expose a small set of hints (seq_pos_bucket, kv_hot_ratio, batch_shape_key) from the serving runtime to the kernel selector. These are lightweight scalar tags attached to batch descriptors.
- Tests and regressions: microbenchmarks for gbench and internal canary; unit tests for the dequant-rewriter; end-to-end perf test in the perf-canary workflow.
- Documentation: notes in docs/serving/quantization.md and a new troubleshooting section describing common FP8 failure modes and mitigation.

Design notes and rationale:
- We avoided aggressive whole-graph FP8 lowering because (a) some models use dynamic dispatch patterns incompatible with a single lowering, and (b) dequantization hoisting across KV cache write-back needs conservative checks to avoid stale keys. The rewriter therefore applies targeted fusion and hoisting, with a safe fallback to existing dequant ops when aliasing cannot be proven.
- Autotuner workflow: an offline pass analyzes representative prompts to build a candidate set. At runtime, a short probe (10s of warmup tokens) validates latency/throughput and writes a per-model best-variant tag. For private and dedicated tenants, the faster autotune mode is enabled by default; hosted tenants require opt-in.

Benchmarks (NVIDIA A100s, sequence mix representative of chat workloads):
- Baseline: mixed FP16 path (pre-change)
  - P50 latency: 86ms
  - P95 latency: 220ms
  - Tokens/sec: 35k
- Post-change (autotuned FP8-fused kernels + dequant-rewriter):
  - P50 latency: 72ms (16% reduction)
  - P95 latency: 165ms (25% reduction)
  - Tokens/sec: 45k (28% increase)
- For small-batch/high-jitter scenarios the kernel router avoided pathological kernel switches and reduced tail variance by ~30%.

Cost impact: in Dedicated benchmarks the improvements correspond to ~22% lower token-GPU-hours for the measured workload mix.

Rollout plan and safety:
- Feature-flagged under runtime.feature.fp8_autotune; default off for hosted mainline for 48h while we collect telemetry.
- Canary deployment to perf-canary and one Dedicated customer (internal) for 72h.
- Automatic rollback policy: if P99 latency increases by >10% or error rate increases by >0.5% over baseline within a 10m window, the rollout controller reverts to the previous kernel tag.

CI and validation:
- Unit and integration tests added under runtime/quant and serving/scheduler.
- Perf canary runs showed initial flakiness due to an aliasing edge-case; fixed by strengthening the alias analysis in the rewriter.
- CI: initial run failed one flaky test (serve_kv_integration_test) and was rerun: final CI passed.

Migration and compatibility notes:
- Models that embed custom kernels may require a one-line model metadata opt-in: "quant.auto_tune: true". We added a compatibility probe that logs recommended kernels without changing runtime behavior until opt-in.
- No API surface change to the Redwood public API.

Observability:
- New metrics: quant_kernel.variant_chosen, quant_autotune.probe_latency, quant_dequant.fused_ops.
- Traces: added tags for seq_pos_bucket and kernel_variant to GPU traces to make regressions easier to root-cause.

Related work: closes ENG-4231 (autotune baseline), progresses ENG-4278 (KV-aware scheduling).

Commits summary: see commits field below.

Files changed (high level): see changed_files field below.

Follow-ups:
- Add FP8 support to the GPU profiler export (ENG-4305).
- Expand autotune dataset to include longer-form decoding patterns.
- Evaluate similar rewriter for INT8-only models where dequantization churn is observed.

b4c2e1f: add FP8 autotuner scaffolding and offline analyzer

Ethan Park: Added conservative alias checks and a fast-path flag for Dedicated where we can assume stable shapes. See dequant_rewriter.h for the check and the feature flag backing.
Marco Ruiz: The autotuner persistence format needs a version tag so future changes don't silently change selected kernels. Please add a version field.
Ethan Park: Added metadata versioning (quant_autotune_v1) and a migration check at load time.
Priya Shah: Approve after the perf-canary numbers are reproducible and CI passes the integration test.
CI Bot: serve_kv_integration_test failed on first run (flaky timing). Rerunning...
CI Bot: All checks passed. Perf-canary run shows P95 improvement and stable error rate.
Ava Chen: Approved and merging. Keep an eye on the canary dashboard for the next 48h.
source.release_notes: Performance: Add FP8 autotune + dequant fusion to reduce dequantization overhead and improve kernel selection for mixed-precision workloads. Improves P50/P95 latency and tokens/sec for chat-like workloads. Ops: Feature-flagged rollout with automatic rollback conditions. Observability: new metrics and tracing tags for kernel variants and autotune probes.
source.notes: This PR is part of the broader quantization initiative; it's intentionally conservative with hoisting to avoid KV correctness issues. Follow-ups listed in description.
source.changelog: Added dequant-rewriter pass, FP8 autotuner, scheduler-integrated kernel router, tests, and docs.

source.title: Dependency-aware rollout orchestrator with safety chains and cohort quarantine
source.author: Ethan Park
source.description:
Motivation: We frequently run multi-model experiments (A/B tests, shadow+canary hybrids) where some models are dependent on upstream feature transformations or other model variants. Current rollouts treat each variant independently which can lead to invalid traffic splits and unsafe rollbacks when dependent cohorts are promoted or quarantined. This PR introduces a dependency-aware rollout orchestrator that models 'safety chains' between rollout nodes so that promotion/rollback decisions are propagated deterministically across related cohorts.

Summary of changes: Adds a new orchestration component (dependency orchestrator) to the servegraph pipeline, implements safety-chain semantics, and extends the rollout policy language with two new directives: "requires" (declare dependencies) and "quarantine_on_failure" (automatic cohort isolation). The orchestrator supports: 1) transactional commit semantics for multi-cohort promotions, 2) automatic quarantine propagation (if a dependent cohort fails SLOs, its dependents are quarantined), 3) staged A/B tie-breaking rules when cohorts share traffic windows, and 4) integration points for Redwood Console to visualize dependency graphs and quarantine status.

Compatibility/migration: Existing rollout specs remain valid; new behaviors are opt-in via the 'dependency_mode: enabled' flag on rollout configs. For teams that want automatic dependency inference, we provide a best-effort heuristic that can be toggled off. No breaking API changes; CLI/SDK support for the new directives added in separate SDK PRs.

Testing: Unit coverage for orchestration logic, integration harness that runs multi-cohort simulations (synthetic latency/error distributions), and a canary e2e with a shadowed dependent model running in the perf-canary lab. A flaky quota test was retried in CI; final green run attached.

Operational notes: When dependency_mode is enabled, redeploys create a transient 'planning' phase for up to 30s where traffic is frozen while the orchestrator evaluates commit plans. This avoids partial promotions. We also emit new metrics: rollout.dependency.plan_duration, rollout.dependency.quarantine_count, and rollout.dependency.propagation_errors. Documented in the rollout runbook and console changelog.

Checklist:
- [x] design doc (linked in ENG-1782)
- [x] unit + integration tests
- [x] console visualization hooks
- [x] migration path and docs

Notes: This PR intentionally keeps the orchestration core separate from the scheduling/batching runtime to preserve low-latency paths for token serving. Follow-up PRs will add autoscale-aware ordering for promotions to minimize capacity churn.
commits: feat(orchestrator): add dependency-aware rollout core (planning + commit engine)
feat(api): rollout spec support for 'requires' and 'quarantine_on_failure' directives
chore(console): expose dependency graph and quarantine UI hooks
test: add simulation harness for multi-cohort experiments
fix: edge-case handling for circular dependency declarations (reject with validation error)
review_conversation: Marisol Vega: Overall approach looks good, but we need to ensure plan_duration doesn't blow up for large graphs. Can we add a worst-case timeout?

Ethan Park: Added a default 30s planning timeout with a configurable env var REDWOOD_DEP_PLAN_TIMEOUT. If planning times out we fall back to safe serial promotion and emit rollout.dependency.plan_timeout metric.
Jonas Klein: Suggest rejecting circular dependency declarations rather than attempting to auto-break them. Auditing cycles is better than implicit behavior.
Ethan Park: Agreed. Validator added to proto layer; cycles now return 422 with a descriptive message. Added unit tests.
Priya Anand: We should ensure quota/limits for transactional promotions to avoid deadlock with Dedicated capacity reservations. Has the autoscaler team reviewed?
Ethan Park: Noted. Linked ENG-1799 for autoscaler follow-up; promotion holds are bounded and will auto-fallback after 2 minutes to prevent deadlocks; logging added to help SRE triage.

title: Dependency-aware rollout orchestrator with safety chains and cohort quarantine
preview:
Motivation: We frequently run multi-model experiments (A/B tests, shadow+canary hybrids) where some models are dependent on upstream feature transformations or other model variants. Current rollouts treat each variant independently which can lead to invalid traffic splits and unsafe rollbacks when dependent cohorts are promoted or quarantined. This PR introduces a dependency-aware rollout orchestrator that models 'safety chains' between rollout nodes so that promotion/rollback decisions are propagated deterministically across related cohorts.

Summary of changes: Adds a new orchestration component (dependency orchestrator) to the servegraph pipeline, implements safety-chain semantics, and extends the rollout policy language with two new directives: "requires" (declare dependencies) and "quarantine_on_failure" (automatic cohort isolation). The orchestrator supports: 1) transactional commit semantics for multi-cohort promotions, 2) automatic quarantine propagation (if a dependent cohort fails SLOs, its dependents are quarantined), 3) staged A/B tie-breaking rules when cohorts share traffic windows, and 4) integration points for Redwood Console to visualize dependency graphs and quarantine status.

Compatibility/migration: Existing rollout specs remain valid; new behaviors are opt-in via the 'dependency_mode: enabled' flag on rollout configs. For teams that want automatic dependency inference, we provide a best-effort heuristic that can be toggled off. No breaking API changes; CLI/SDK support for the new directives added in separate SDK PRs.

Testing: Unit coverage for orchestration logic, integration harness that runs multi-cohort simulations (synthetic latency/error distributions), and a canary e2e with a shadowed dependent model running in the perf-canary lab. A flaky quota test was retried in CI; final green run attached.

Operational notes: When dependency_mode is enabled, redeploys create a transient 'planning' phase for up to 30s where traffic is frozen while the orchestrator evaluates commit plans. This avoids partial promotions. We also emit new metrics: rollout.dependency.plan_duration, rollout.dependency.quarantine_count, and rollout.dependency.propagation_errors. Documented in the rollout runbook and console changelog.

Checklist:
- [x] design doc (linked in ENG-1782)
- [x] unit + integration tests
- [x] console visualization hooks
- [x] migration path and docs

Notes: This PR intentionally keeps the orchestration core separate from the scheduling/batching runtime to preserve low-latency paths for token serving. Follow-up PRs will add autoscale-aware ordering for promotions to minimize capacity churn.

source.title: openai-v1-chat-params-clarity-and-toolcall-debounce-for-stream-stability
source.author: Maya Singh
source.release_notes: Clarify v1 chat param mapping; introduce toolcall debounce to reduce duplicated function-call bursts in streaming scenarios; add stream heartbeat to improve client parity and recoverability.
source.description:
Motivation: We continue to close the gap between Redwood's /v1/chat compatibility surface and the OpenAI chat/completions expectations. Customers reported two classes of issues: (1) noisy rapid fncall footprints during network retries and websocket reconnects that caused multiple tool invocations, and (2) ambiguous behavior when multiple stop signals, role aliases, or logprob fallbacks co-occurred in streaming output. This PR implements two coordinated changes: (A) a parameter clarity layer that normalizes and documents v1 chat params (role aliases, stop token semantics, logprob backoff) so downstream stages get a deterministic shape; (B) a lightweight toolcall debounce and stream heartbeat handshake that prevents duplicated function/tool invocations on transient reconnects and provides a clearer stream parity signal to clients. 

What changed (high level):
- Added a v1 param canonicalizer that: maps role aliases (e.g. assistant/assistant_bot -> assistant), coercively flattens stop lists into prioritized sequences, and exposes an explicit logprob fallback strategy field so the runtime can decide deterministic fallback ordering.
- Introduced a ToolCallDebouncer component (in the streaming runtime) that groups function call events within a short, configurable window and emits a single canonical tool invocation when the payloads are equivalent or supersets. Debounce window defaults to 150ms; policy is configurable per-route.
- Implemented a StreamHeartbeat handshake token inserted into the framed streaming envelope to help clients reason about stream continuity and replay semantics. This is backward-compatible: older clients will ignore the heartbeat key.
- Small changes to the OpenAI compatibility adapter so that v1/chat params are translated into a stable internal config object consumed by the streamer and tool dispatcher.

Why this design:
- Debounce (vs dedupe) preserves intent for near-simultaneous distinct calls but prevents accidental duplicate tool effect when identical payloads are retried.
- Canonical param shape reduces branching across the stack (routing, batching, evals) and simplifies rollout of future stop/logprob policies.
- Heartbeat token makes it feasible to implement at-most-once tool semantics in the face of websocket reconnects without a heavy transactional system.

Checklist: 
- [x] param canonicalizer implemented and unit-tested
- [x] toolcall debounce with config and integration tests
- [x] stream heartbeat handshake integrated into framed streaming envelope
- [x] updated docs with migration notes for customers using advanced stop/logprob behavior
- [x] load/perf smoke tests run on staging (no regression in p50 latency; slight p99 increase for streams with debounce enabled, measured and documented)

Testing and results: 
- Unit tests: src/compat/test_param_mapper.py covers role mapping and stop prioritization permutations.
- Integration: tests simulate websocket reconnects with rapid fncall retries; prior to debounce we observed 2–4 duplicate tool invocations in 12% of reconnection scenarios; after changes duplicates drop to <1%.
- Performance: enabling debounce adds ~0.8ms median overhead to streaming path when active; default off for customers unless route config enables it.

Backwards compatibility: default behavior preserves existing semantics unless a route explicitly opts into the debounce policy. The canonicalizer will not mutate user-visible strings except for role alias normalization, which is documented as a safe mapping.

Related work: ENG-1604 (stream-framing maturity), ENG-1712 (fncall reliability post-incident), SUP-4211 (customer report leading to this PR).

Commits summary: 
- initial: param canonicalizer and tests
- add: toolcall debouncer core logic + integration tests
- add: heartbeat envelope field and streamer wiring
- docs: openai_compat.md migration guidance and default config notes
- fix: handle edge-case when stop list is empty and logprob fallback is present

Changed files (high level):
- src/compat/param_mapper.py (new)
- src/streaming/heartbeat_manager.py (new)
- src/streaming/toolcall_debouncer.go (new)
- src/adapter/openai_v1/adapter.py (updates to use canonicalizer)
- src/runtime/tool_dispatcher.py (integrate debounce hook)
- tests/integration/test_fncall_retries.py (added)
- tests/unit/test_param_mapper.py (added)
- docs/openai_compat.md (update)
- examples/chat/parity_demo.py (example showing heartbeat)
- scripts/perf/smoke_stream.sh (updated)
commits: c1a3f9a - add param canonicalizer and base tests
b7e2c4d - implement toolcall debounce core and unit tests
f2d5b9a - add stream heartbeat and adapter wiring
a9c7e11 - docs and migration notes
e4d2f00 - fix stop->logprob edge case
review_comments: Ethan Park: Overall approach looks good. Two asks: (1) can we surface the debounce window in the per-route dashboard so SREs can see if it's firing frequently, (2) add a fuzz test for slightly different payloads to prove we don't conflate distinct calls.

Maya Singh: Responding - added telemetry counter (route.debounce.fired) and dashboard note; wrote fuzz integration test that compares payload hashes and ensures distinct payloads bypass merge. See tests/integration/test_fncall_retries.py:line ~120.

Ravi Patel: CI failure on first run was the flakey streaming test; after rebase and test ordering fix CI passed. Left a nit about naming (ToolCallDebouncer -> DebouncedToolInvoker) but ok to address in follow-up.
Sofia Nguyen: Approved after reading perf numbers and doc updates. Recommended we include a short migration note for enterprise customers that pin older client SDKs.

source.title: Invoke compatibility proto mapper and decision telemetry
source.author: Maya Chen
source.release_notes: Adds an OpenAI-compatibility proto mapper that normalizes function/tool invocation envelopes to Redwood internal shapes. Introduces an explicit decision telemetry event (invoke.decision) that records tool selection candidates, normalized scores, and final chosen tool to aid post-deployment debugging. Default rollout: gradual on Hosted, opt-in for Dedicated/Private until config flag 'openai_compat_proto_mapper' enabled.
commits: core: add proto mapper and rule engine (proto_mapper.py)
runtime: implement choice scoring and confidence enrichment (choice_scorer.py)
telemetry: emit invoke.decision events with candidate list (telemetry/invoke_events.go)
tests: unit + integration harness for fragmented streams and backpressure cases
docs: compatibility notes and migration guide for customers
changed_files: runtime/openai_adapter/proto_mapper.py - new module: canonicalizes function/tool invocation payloads, maps OpenAI 'name'/'arguments' to redwood tool descriptor fields and attaches 'redwood_tool_id' and 'compat_hint' metadata
runtime/openai_adapter/choice_scorer.py - new: heuristic ensemble to score candidate tools using schema affinity, stream-priming tokens, and historical latency/cost priors
runtime/openai_adapter/stream_coordinator.py - update: align fragment boundaries to tool message frames and emit 'fragment_resync' when partial payloads seen
console/telemetry/invoke_events.go - update: decision telemetry emitter, schema v1 for invoke.decision events, includes candidates[], scores[], chosen_index, confidence, and request_id
tests/unit/test_proto_mapper.py - new: mapping unit tests for edge cases (nested JSON args, missing schema, legacy alias fields)
tests/integration/test_invoke_end_to_end.py - update: harness that simulates streaming toolcall fragments and verifies deterministic reassembly
docs/openai_compatibility.md - update: migration guide, behavior matrix for tool_choice ambiguity and recommended flags
examples/openai_adapter/sample_tool_descriptor.json - new: canonical examples demonstrating canonicalization and compat_hint usage
tools/rbac/allowed_tools_policy.yaml - update: ensure new redwood_tool_id is accepted in policy checks
pkg/metrics/invoke_metrics.go - update: add histograms for choice_count and decision_time_ms
ci/workflows/compat-integration.yaml - update: new integration workflow that runs streaming fragment scenarios
scripts/migrate_tool_aliases.py - new: helper to generate alias->redwood_tool_id mapping for customers
source.description: Motivation: Customers expect OpenAI-style function/tool calling to 'just work' when routed through Redwood's serving runtime. However, in practice we see a set of recurring issues: heterogeneous tool descriptors from third-party models, ambiguous tool_choice semantics when multiple tools match, and stream fragmentation that splits the tool invocation payload across SSE boundaries. This PR introduces a compatibility-focused proto mapper and decision telemetry that together make tool invocation more deterministic and observable.Design summary: 1) proto_mapper: a lightweight rule engine that canonicalizes incoming OpenAI-compatible invocation envelopes into a single Redwood internal representation. It normalizes fields (name/description/schema/arguments), expands legacy aliases into canonical redwood_tool_id, and attaches a compat_hint when the mapping required heuristics. Mappings are deterministic and extensible via a small YAML ruleset shipped with the package. 2) choice_scorer: when multiple tools match a requested function name/schema, the scorer computes an ordered candidate list using schema affinity (how well the provided arguments conform to each tool schema), stream-priming context (tokens already generated that imply certain tool semantics), and operational priors (recent latency/cost observed for the customer's tenant). The winner receives an explicit confidence score and a short rationale encoded in metadata. 3) stream_coordinator: improves streaming stability by aligning fragments to logical tool frames: partial JSON fragments are buffered and resynchronized rather than forwarded to downstream tool adapters; we emit a fragment_resync event for observability. 4) telemetry: emit an invoke.decision event for every tool invocation that contains candidate list, scores, chosen_index, confidence, request_id, and quick pointers to the proto_mapper rule that fired. This event is low-cardinality and intended for debugging regressions and production investigations.Changes and files: See changed_files for a compact list. Key behavioral changes are limited and guarded behind a feature flag 'openai_compat_proto_mapper' that defaults to on for Hosted only and off for Dedicated/Private until customers opt in. Tests: added unit tests covering nested JSON arguments, missing schema resolution, alias expansion, and integration tests that simulate streaming SSE fragmentation across multiple boundaries to verify deterministic reassembly and correct candidate scoring. Performance: choice_scorer is designed to be cheap (fast schema affinity checks and cached token-based signals). Benchmarks on a 4k sequence workload show median decision latency ~= 0.9ms additional per-invoke at p50 and 2.4ms at p95 under load (detailed numbers in benchmark artifact attached to CI). Rollout and risk mitigation: rollout is staged. Hosted -> 10%/50%/100% over two weeks with monitoring dashboards. Dedicated/Private customers will see release notes and can enable via console or config. We included a migration script to generate alias mappings for large customers that rely on legacy tool names.Open questions and future work: - Consider surfacing the decision rationale in debug traces for customers with private deployments. - Expand rule engine to accept safe machine-suggested mappings (requires guardrails). - Add optional delta compression for repeated identical invocation envelopes to reduce egress cost.Review conversation snapshot: See review_thread field for representative comments. Merge strategy: squash; small logical commits squashed to a single logical change and merged to main.Approvals: Approved by core runtime reviewers and SRE, CI passed and manual integration harness validated in staging.
review_thread: Ethan Park: Nice separation of concerns. Two asks before merge: tightened unit tests for alias collisions and a short doc paragraph on security implications of compat_hint.
Maya Chen (author): Added tests for alias collision and documented compat_hint semantics in docs/openai_compatibility.md. compat_hint is a low-entropy string and does not include sensitive args.

Liam O'Connor: Optional nit: can we avoid constructing full JSON blobs when a fragment is small? Suggest a fast-path in stream_coordinator. Response: added fast-path to return on single-fragment invocations without buffering.
Priya Desai: Telemetry schema looks good. Confirmed low-cardinality fields only. Approved +1.

QA Lead (Ravi Patel): Ran integration harness against staging with 50k synthetic invokes. Observed 0.02% additional failures attributable to edge-case mapping; mitigated by fallback to legacy mapper. Added a hotfix backout flag.
Ops (Sara Nguyen): Please ensure the feature-flag default is off for Private customers. Confirmed and updated deployment manifest.
Maya Chen: addressed all comments and pushed final CI fixes.
ci_logs: ci/build: pass - compiled runtime and adapter modules
ci/lint: pass - formatting and type checks

We included a migration script to generate alias mappings for large customers that rely on legacy tool names.Open questions and future work: - Consider surfacing the decision rationale in debug traces for customers with private deployments. - Expand rule engine to accept safe machine-suggested mappings (requires guardrails). - Add optional delta compression for repeated identical invocation envelopes to reduce egress cost.Review conversation snapshot: See review_thread field for representative comments. Merge strategy: squash; small logical commits squashed to a single logical change and merged to main.Approvals: Approved by core runtime reviewers and SRE, CI passed and manual integration harness validated in staging.
text: Motivation: Customers expect OpenAI-style function/tool calling to 'just work' when routed through Redwood's serving runtime. However, in practice we see a set of recurring issues: heterogeneous tool descriptors from third-party models, ambiguous tool_choice semantics when multiple tools match, and stream fragmentation that splits the tool invocation payload across SSE boundaries. This PR introduces a compatibility-focused proto mapper and decision telemetry that together make tool invocation more deterministic and observable.Design summary: 1) proto_mapper: a lightweight rule engine that canonicalizes incoming OpenAI-compatible invocation envelopes into a single Redwood internal representation. It normalizes fields (name/description/schema/arguments), expands legacy aliases into canonical redwood_tool_id, and attaches a compat_hint when the mapping required heuristics. Mappings are deterministic and extensible via a small YAML ruleset shipped with the package. 2) choice_scorer: when multiple tools match a requested function name/schema, the scorer computes an ordered candidate list using schema affinity (how well the provided arguments conform to each tool schema), stream-priming context (tokens already generated that imply certain tool semantics), and operational priors (recent latency/cost observed for the customer's tenant). The winner receives an explicit confidence score and a short rationale encoded in metadata. 3) stream_coordinator: improves streaming stability by aligning fragments to logical tool frames: partial JSON fragments are buffered and resynchronized rather than forwarded to downstream tool adapters; we emit a fragment_resync event for observability. 4) telemetry: emit an invoke.decision event for every tool invocation that contains candidate list, scores, chosen_index, confidence, request_id, and quick pointers to the proto_mapper rule that fired. This event is low-cardinality and intended for debugging regressions and production investigations.Changes and files: See changed_files for a compact list. Key behavioral changes are limited and guarded behind a feature flag 'openai_compat_proto_mapper' that defaults to on for Hosted only and off for Dedicated/Private until customers opt in. Tests: added unit tests covering nested JSON arguments, missing schema resolution, alias expansion, and integration tests that simulate streaming SSE fragmentation across multiple boundaries to verify deterministic reassembly and correct candidate scoring. Performance: choice_scorer is designed to be cheap (fast schema affinity checks and cached token-based signals). Benchmarks on a 4k sequence workload show median decision latency ~= 0.9ms additional per-invoke at p50 and 2.4ms at p95 under load (detailed numbers in benchmark artifact attached to CI). Rollout and risk mitigation: rollout is staged. Hosted -> 10%/50%/100% over two weeks with monitoring dashboards. Dedicated/Private customers will see release notes and can enable via console or config. We included a migration script to generate alias mappings for large customers that rely on legacy tool names.Open questions and future work: - Consider surfacing the decision rationale in debug traces for customers with private deployments. - Expand rule engine to accept safe machine-suggested mappings (requires guardrails). - Add optional delta compression for repeated identical invocation envelopes to reduce egress cost.Review conversation snapshot: See review_thread field for representative comments. Merge strategy: squash; small logical commits squashed to a single logical change and merged to main.Approvals: Approved by core runtime reviewers and SRE, CI passed and manual integration harness validated in staging.

source.title: tooling/execution: concurrency safeguards and tool-result-injection ergonomics
source.author: Alyssa Chen
source.release_notes: Improves safety and ergonomics for multi-tool and parallel tool calls: deterministic injection order, configurable parallelism limits, idempotent retry markers, and OpenAI-compat layer behavior tweaks.
source.description: Motivation: Customers integrating complex tool chains reported flaky outputs when multiple tools ran in parallel and tool results were injected back into the model stream. This PR standardizes execution semantics for multi-tool calls, adds runtime safeguards for parallelism, and harmonizes behavior with our OpenAI-compat surface. Summary of changes: 1) Executor: introduce a per-request "tool-parallelism" cap (configurable at API/route level) that bounds concurrent tool invocations and provides backpressure hooks to the scheduler; 2) Injection protocol: formalize a deterministic injection ordering when multiple tool results arrive within the same model turn and attach an idempotency token to each injected result so retries do not duplicate content; 3) Retry semantics: new retry policy enum (none, best-effort, strict-idempotent) exposed via the API and respected by the runtime and OpenAI-compat shim; 4) Streaming stability: when streaming responses include tool-calls, we now atomically flush tool-invocation markers to the stream and gracefully resume generation once injected results are committed to the KV cache; 5) OpenAI-compat updates: function-calling payloads now accept an optional "execution_hints" map with parallelism and idempotency hints that the compat layer translates into our internal routing and executor options; 6) Observability: emit new metrics (tool.invocations.active, tool.invocations.queued, tool.injection.latency) and traces annotated with execution_hints for easier debugging. Implementation notes: - Executor changes are confined to runtime/executor/* and api/handlers/tool_call_handler.go; the executor preserves the previous scheduling algorithm but applies per-request parallelism caps before dispatching tool workers. - Injection order uses a deterministic comparator: (request_tool_sequence, tool_priority, start_timestamp). This avoids nondeterministic interleaving in model-visible outputs. - Idempotency tokens are short-lived UUIDs persisted in the request context and kept in the per-request token-cache; replayed tool results with matching token are ignored by the injection layer. - The OpenAI compatibility shim accepts additional fields but falls back to previous behavior when execution_hints are omitted to preserve backward compatibility. Checklist: - [x] Design doc updated with sequence diagrams and failure modes (ENG-1523) - [x] Unit tests for ordering and idempotency - [x] Integration tests simulating multi-tool fan-out and parallel retries - [x] Metrics/tracing added and tested in canary - [x] Backwards compatibility verified for existing function-calling clients - [x] Docs snippet for API surface added Notes on tradeoffs: We intentionally chose a conservative default parallelism cap of 4 to reduce burst load on downstream tool services; customers can raise this per-route with a warning in the console. The idempotency token approach requires minimal state but does not protect against cross-request duplicate side-effects (we call this out in the design doc). Migration: No action required from existing users; new execution_hints fields are additive. For customers wanting deterministic injection ordering across retries, they should include explicit tool priority in their tool manifest. Rollout plan: Canary on a small percentage of hosted traffic for one week with annotated traces and enhanced logging; force-enable flag included to accelerate rollouts for enterprise tenants. Backwards compatibility: tests included to ensure older SDKs (OpenAI-compat) do not break. Risk and mitigations: The biggest risk is increased latency when a tool result arrives slightly after generation — mitigated by configurable timeouts and an async-fallback policy that allows the generator to continue with a best-effort placeholder if strict-idempotent is not set. Local testing: see integration test harness in tests/integration/tool_invoke_fanout_test.go. CI: all unit/integration checks passed after two reruns (flaky test fix in metrics). Merge outcome: squash-merged to main with commit 0a9f3d2 (executor: add parallelism cap + injection protocol).
commits: feat(executor): add per-request parallelism cap and queueing
fix(api): OpenAI-compat map passthrough for execution_hints
test: simulate parallel retry and idempotency handling
docs: API example for execution_hints
review_conversation: Jordan Blake: +1 on the design, request: add trace tags that surface the execution_hints (done in follow-up)
Priya Kapoor: please confirm backpressure behavior when downstream is saturated — implemented queue with max-wait and documented in design
Lars Müller: small nit on naming (strict-idempotent -> idempotent_strict) applied in second commit
Alyssa Chen (author): responded — kept public API as 'strict-idempotent' for readability, normalized internal name to 'idempotent_strict' to satisfy both
Jordan Blake: approved after CI green
Priya Kapoor: approved with one suggestion around logging verbosity (implemented)

source.title: introduce-budget-mapper-mini-batcher-defaults-and-lightweight-quant-profiles
source.author: Ava Chen
source.description:
Motivation: many tenants operate with low to moderate QPS where full tenant-wide prefill and large batch policies add cost without improving tail latency. This PR adds a lightweight 'budget mapper' that emits per-tenant mini-batcher defaults and an opinionated set of small quantization profiles so teams can get cost-down defaults that are safe for production.

Summary of changes: adds a BudgetMapper component that infers conservative mini-batching and cache window defaults from historical unit-econ signals (p95 latency, tokens-per-request, cold-start rate). Introduces a tiny, validated quant_profiles.yaml with three compact profiles (tiny-int8, lean-int4, compat-fp16) and a CLI subcommand 'redwood econ inspect' to preview tenant defaults. Defaults are intentionally conservative and include automatic safe fallbacks (no quantization for models flagged as unsafe). The PR wires telemetry (econ.unit_price_estimate, batching.shape_hint, quant.profile_applied) and a lightweight e2e smoke test that validates latency and token-cost regressions against a golden workload.

Design notes: BudgetMapper is implemented as a single-file controller (pkg/econ/budget_mapper.go) that consumes aggregated profile snapshots (already emitted by existing telemetry) and runs a deterministic rule set to produce config suggestions. The decision graph is intentionally small so it can be audited (no ML training). Mini-batcher defaults focus on microbatch_size, per-tenant prefix_cache_seconds, and warmup_window_ms. Quant suggestion heuristics prefer lean-int4 for non-sensitive models with median request length < 256 and token-efficiency > 0.8.

Testing & safety: unit tests for decision branches, CI smoke (ci/econ-smoke.yml) against a local mock runtime, and an e2e harness that replays a sampled tenant workload to assert no >5% regression in p95 latency and at least 8% reduction in measured token cost for qualifying tenants. We also added a feature flag (feature.econ.budget_mapper.enabled) and rollout steps in the console so operators can opt-in per-tenant.

Migration / rollout: feature gated behind console flag and an opt-in CLI. Default remains off for existing tenants. Operators can run 'redwood econ inspect --tenant TID --dry-run' to preview suggested defaults without applying them. If applied, changes are stored as tenant overrides and can be rolled back with 'redwood econ rollback'.

Checklist:
- [x] BudgetMapper core implementation
- [x] quant_profiles.yaml (three profiles) and validation schema
- [x] CLI preview and apply/rollback helpers
- [x] telemetry hooks and unit-econ metrics
- [x] unit tests and e2e smoke
- [x] docs (observability/econ-telemetry.md)

Related: ENG-3124 contains the decision criteria; PM-198 is the product brief for conservative defaults. SUP-411 was consulted for tenant opt-in UX during an earlier support escalation.
commits: a1b2c3d: initial budget mapper skeleton and rulegraph + basic unit tests
b2c3d4e: add quant_profiles.yaml and validation; include compat-fp16, lean-int4, tiny-int8
c3d4e5f: CLI econ inspect/apply/rollback and integration hooks to tenant overrides
d4e5f6a: telemetry: emit econ.unit_price_estimate and batching.shape_hint metrics
e5f6a7b: e2e smoke test harness and ci job (ci/econ-smoke.yml)
f6a7b8c: docs and changelog for console rollout; add feature flag check
review_conversation: Mateo Ruiz (reviewer): Can we make sure the lean-int4 profile has an explicit fallback to compat-fp16 for these model families? Some of our older LLaMA forks fail int4 at generation start.
Ava Chen (author): Good catch — added a model_family whitelist/blacklist check and explicit fallback in quant_profiles.yaml. Comments in budget_mapper.go updated to document the fallback reasoning.

Priya Kapoor (reviewer): Tests look solid. Suggest adding a small synthetic workload where median token length ~ 512 to verify we don't incorrectly pick lean-int4. Also can we gate telemetry keys behind a flag to avoid noise until rollout?

Ava Chen (author): Added the 512-length synthetic test and gated econ.unit_price_estimate behind feature.econ.telemetry.enabled. Updated CI job to run only when the feature flag is present in test env.
Luca Bianchi (approver): I verified e2e smoke locally and in the staging cluster; metrics look reasonable. Approve pending one doc nit (clarify rollback steps).
Mateo Ruiz (approver): Approved — loaded staging tenants, applied suggestions to three low-QPS tenants; saw ~11% token cost reduction and <2% p95 latency increase (within SLO).
Ava Chen (author): Addressed doc nit; clarified rollback and console steps. Ready to merge.
source.merge_commit_message:
squash: budget-mapper for conservative mini-batcher defaults + lightweight quant profiles

Adds BudgetMapper, quant_profiles.yaml, econ CLI, telemetry and e2e smoke tests. Feature gated behind feature.econ.budget_mapper.enabled.
source.release_notes: Add BudgetMapper: opt-in tenant defaults for mini-batching and conservative quant profiles (tiny-int8, lean-int4, compat-fp16). Feature gated; operators should preview suggestions with 'redwood econ inspect' before applying. Includes telemetry hooks to measure unit-econ impact.
source.notes_for_ops: Rollout plan: enable feature flag for a small set of dev tenants, run 'redwood econ inspect --tenant <TID>' and review suggested overrides. Staged apply to 5% of eligible tenants, monitor econ.unit_price_estimate and p95 latency for 48h. Full rollout once token-cost decreases >= 5% and p95 within SLO.

source.title: Reduce CPU serialization overhead and introduce request-affine thread pinning
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.description:
Motivation: production canaries showed CPU-side stalls in the request path under medium tail-load (concurrent short requests). Root cause analysis indicated three correlated issues: a global serialization lock in the request marshalling path, KV-cache read contention on a small number of shards, and excessive cross-thread wakeups from the executor when continuous batching is active. This PR addresses those CPU-side bottlenecks with low-risk, togglable changes that reduce serialization overhead and make scheduling request-affine so hot requests avoid context migration.

Summary of changes: I) Replace global request-serialization mutex with per-worker small-buffer fast-path using FlatBuffers for marshalling and a fallback to proto when necessary. II) Introduce a request-affine thread-pool (request-affine executors) that pins a request's lifecycle to a CPU core group to reduce cache churn during multi-stage request handling. III) Add a lock-free fast read path for KV-cache using a seqlock-style version stamp per shard plus per-shard read counters, and shard rebalancer to spread hot keys. IV) Reduce continuous-batching wakeups by batching wake signals and using a wait-free work-stealing hint for low-latency tails.

Performance impact (canary + micro-bench): - End-to-end P50: -6% latency, P95: -14%, P99: -18% for short inference (<128 tokens) workloads. - CPU sys time on sandboxed 8-vCPU nodes: -35% for the runtime process when under 500rps of small requests. - Throughput improved by ~9% in multi-tenant density tests due to reduced serialization stalls.

Rollout plan: flags added to runtime config and console: runtime.cpu_optimizations.enabled (default: false), runtime.request_affinity.policy (off|pinned|affine). Default rollout: enable on Dedicated clusters for canary group A (5% traffic) for 72 hours, compare P99 and host CPU; if stable, increase to 25% then 100%. Quick rollback: set runtime.cpu_optimizations.enabled=false -> instant drain-of-new-requests (no binary change).

Compatibility and safety: changes are behind config flags. No model artifacts changed. Unit and integration tests added. This is not a breaking API change.

Checklist: - [x] Unit tests for serialization fast-path and fallback - [x] KV-cache microbench and canary harness - [x] End-to-end canary config + dashboard widgets - [x] Documentation for new runtime flags and helm values - [x] Security review (no secrets touched)

Commits: detailed below.

Notes for reviewers: focus review on correctness of the seqlock implementation (KV-cache) and on potential live-migration interactions with request affinity. I added a conservative fairness timer on the affine pool (200ms) to avoid starvation of low-priority background tasks.

Additional context: This work was driven by ENG-9123 (investigate CPU stalls) and is intended to be low-risk stepping-stone; the next PR will expose adaptive shard sizing and per-tenant affinity policies.
commits: a3f9b7c core: add flatbuffer marshalling fast-path with fallback to proto
b7d21a2 scheduler: introduce request-affine thread pool and pinning hooks
c4e6f1b kv-cache: add shard seqlock and hot-key sharder
d9f2aae batching: reduce wakeups and add work-stealing hint
f1bb4ee tests: add microbench and end-to-end canary config
diff_snippets: src/runtime/request_marshaller.cpp: replaced global std::mutex g_serialize_lock with per-worker SmallBufferSerializer fast-path; fallback uses existing ProtoSerializer::serialize(req)
src/runtime/scheduler/affine_pool.cpp: new AffinePool::submit(request) pins thread via cpu_set and stores affinity token on RequestContext; added fairness timer to rotate affinity slots
src/runtime/kv_cache/shard_seqlock.cpp: implemented seqlock read pattern: snapshot version -> read values -> fence -> check version even; added per-shard hot_count atomic counter for rebalancer
review_comments: Ethan Cole: Can you explain why FlatBuffers was chosen over msgpack/Cap'n Proto here? Concerned about copying semantics on large metadata blobs.

Priya Natarajan (reply): FlatBuffers allows zero-copy read after the initial marshal and a compact encoding for our thin request envelope; large metadata still falls back to proto path to avoid regressions. Measured copy reduction ~60% on the marshalling hot path.
María Alvarez: The seqlock read path looks OK but need to make sure we don't observe a torn pointer on 32-bit hosts. Add static_assert on pointer-size or ensure 64-bit-only compilation for that file.
Priya Natarajan (reply): Added BUILD guard; seqlock implementation compiled only on 64-bit targets and fallback locking path retained for 32-bit. Updated tests to assert build-time guard.
Jasper Liu: Bench results are impressive. Please confirm this does not degrade long-running batch workloads ( >1024 tokens ).

Priya Natarajan (reply): Verified in perf suite: large-batch throughput unchanged (+1% variance). Continuous batching timers are untouched for large requests; affinity only affects short-request lanes by default.
Ethan Cole: small nit, add a comment in affine_pool about the fairness timer and why value is 200ms
Priya Natarajan (reply): Added explanatory comment and a unit test that verifies fairness timer rotates affinity after 200ms under synthetic load.
source.release_notes: runtime: reduce CPU serialization and add request-affine thread pinning behind a flag; P99 latency improved for short requests; rollout behind canary flag. See docs/runtime/flags.md for config and helm/redwood/values-runtime.yaml for helm values.

title: Reduce CPU serialization overhead and introduce request-affine thread pinning
preview:
Motivation: production canaries showed CPU-side stalls in the request path under medium tail-load (concurrent short requests). Root cause analysis indicated three correlated issues: a global serialization lock in the request marshalling path, KV-cache read contention on a small number of shards, and excessive cross-thread wakeups from the executor when continuous batching is active. This PR addresses those CPU-side bottlenecks with low-risk, togglable changes that reduce serialization overhead and make scheduling request-affine so hot requests avoid context migration.

Summary of changes: I) Replace global request-serialization mutex with per-worker small-buffer fast-path using FlatBuffers for marshalling and a fallback to proto when necessary. II) Introduce a request-affine thread-pool (request-affine executors) that pins a request's lifecycle to a CPU core group to reduce cache churn during multi-stage request handling. III) Add a lock-free fast read path for KV-cache using a seqlock-style version stamp per shard plus per-shard read counters, and shard rebalancer to spread hot keys. IV) Reduce continuous-batching wakeups by batching wake signals and using a wait-free work-stealing hint for low-latency tails.

Performance impact (canary + micro-bench): - End-to-end P50: -6% latency, P95: -14%, P99: -18% for short inference (<128 tokens) workloads. - CPU sys time on sandboxed 8-vCPU nodes: -35% for the runtime process when under 500rps of small requests. - Throughput improved by ~9% in multi-tenant density tests due to reduced serialization stalls.

Rollout plan: flags added to runtime config and console: runtime.cpu_optimizations.enabled (default: false), runtime.request_affinity.policy (off|pinned|affine). Default rollout: enable on Dedicated clusters for canary group A (5% traffic) for 72 hours, compare P99 and host CPU; if stable, increase to 25% then 100%. Quick rollback: set runtime.cpu_optimizations.enabled=false -> instant drain-of-new-requests (no binary change).

Compatibility and safety: changes are behind config flags. No model artifacts changed. Unit and integration tests added. This is not a breaking API change.

Checklist: - [x] Unit tests for serialization fast-path and fallback - [x] KV-cache microbench and canary harness - [x] End-to-end canary config + dashboard widgets - [x] Documentation for new runtime flags and helm values - [x] Security review (no secrets touched)

Commits: detailed below.

Notes for reviewers: focus review on correctness of the seqlock implementation (KV-cache) and on potential live-migration interactions with request affinity. I added a conservative fairness timer on the affine pool (200ms) to avoid starvation of low-priority background tasks.

Additional context: This work was driven by ENG-9123 (investigate CPU stalls) and is intended to be low-risk stepping-stone; the next PR will expose adaptive shard sizing and per-tenant affinity policies.

source.title: introduce-lightweight-local-dev-runner-and-toolchain-stub
source.author: Lina Chen
source.release_notes: Adds a lightweight local dev runner (fastdev) and a minimal toolchain stub to make contributor onboarding and iterative CI iteration faster. No behavioral change to production CI. Opt-in for developers.
source.body:
Context and motivation: onboarding and fast feedback for local CI iterations has regressed as our CI matrix grew. This PR introduces a lightweight local dev runner (tools/fastdev) and a minimal toolchain stub (dev/Toolchain.toml) plus a small workflow that developers can run locally or in ephemeral self-hosted runners to get the same sanity checks as CI without the full matrix. This is an opt-in developer convenience feature that reduces time-to-first-green and iteration time on lint/test changes. Summary of changes: - add tools/fastdev/runner.go: small Go binary that runs a subset of checks in-process with conservative timeouts and filesystem caching - add tools/fastdev/README.md: usage, examples, and how to wire into VS Code tasks - add dev/Toolchain.toml: lightweight list of pinned tool versions for local dev (go, node, python, prettier, husky minimal config) - add .github/workflows/dev-local.yaml: workflow targeted at self-hosted/dev-runner label and usable as reference for local runs - add .pre-commit-config.yaml entries and Makefile target fastdev - add scripts/ci/sanity-check.sh to share common checks between CI and fastdev Why not full devcontainer? Full devcontainers are heavier and we want a quick path for contributors who already have the environment. The Toolchain.toml is intentionally minimal and human-editable; it is not authoritative for production. Checklist: [x] design doc linked (ENG-4172) [x] unit tests for runner logic [x] small e2e smoke used locally by author [x] docs and README for opt-in usage Implementation notes: the runner currently shells out to the language toolchain where appropriate but uses a caching layer (tools/fastdev/.cache) to avoid repeated installs during rapid iterations. It ships a dry-run mode and a --ci-compat flag which performs the same commands as the CI sanity lane (lint, go vet, pytest -k sanity). Small illustrative snippet of the workflow (from .github/workflows/dev-local.yaml):
name: Dev local referenced
on: [workflow_dispatch]
jobs:
  fastdev:
    runs-on: self-hosted
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - run: ./tools/fastdev/runner --ci-compat

Changes are conservative: no production CI lane behaviors removed; existing CI still the source of truth. Rollout plan: merge to main and advertise opt-in in the contributor guide; monitor adoption and iterate. Performance expectations: local fastdev runs should be ~30-60s for small changes on modern dev machines, substantially faster than full CI matrix. Risks and mitigations: - risk: divergence between fastdev and CI -> mitigation: include --ci-compat mode that runs identical commands, and we add a CI check that runs the fastdev dry-run in a nightly job to detect drift. - risk: contributors rely on the stub as authoritative -> mitigation: call out in README that Toolchain.toml is a local convenience. Related PRs & notes: this PR intentionally avoids changing release automation and focuses on developer feedback loops; follow-ups will propose integrating a thin prebuild cache and a remote runner for fast-path checks.
commits: a3d9b47: tools/fastdev: initial runner scaffolding and basic cache layer
c4f2e11: dev: add Toolchain.toml with minimal pins and docs
b5f6a2e: ci: add dev-local workflow and sanity-check wrapper script
d1e8c33: chore: add Makefile fastdev target and pre-commit config entries
e7a9f55: docs: tools/fastdev README and usage examples
review_comments: Marco Ruiz (2026-02-16): Nice small surface area. Please add an explicit smoke e2e test that exercises --ci-compat mode against a small repo subset so we can run it in nightly drift detection.
Lina Chen (2026-02-16): Added a minimal smoke script and referenced it in the README. It runs pytest -k sanity and go vet in a tiny project fixture. See commit c4f2e11.
Priya Nair (2026-02-17): Can we ensure the tool doesn't hide flaky tests? Suggest adding a --no-cache flag to force fresh installs and a clear message when cache is used.
Lina Chen (2026-02-17): Added --no-cache and improved log lines when cache hits/misses occur in commit b5f6a2e.
Ethan Park (2026-02-18): Suggested pinning the go version in Toolchain.toml to avoid surprising local failures; also asked to avoid modifying production CI in this PR.
Lina Chen (2026-02-18): Pinned go and node entries in Toolchain.toml and verified that CI remains unchanged. Responded that release automation changes will be a separate follow-up.
Marco Ruiz (2026-02-19): Approving after small nits addressed. Marked approval in github UI.
Priya Nair (2026-02-19): Approved. Left a note to follow up on telemetry for opt-in usage.
source.merge_commit: f9b2c4a: merge main into feature/dev/fast-local-runner-toolchain-stub, squash and merge

source.title: add kernel-hotspot tracer, kvcache offset histogram, and continuous batch pressure probe
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.description: Motivation: It has been difficult to correlate kernel-level hotspots with KV cache layout and continuous-batching backpressure events in production traces. This PR introduces three complementary low-overhead probes and a small microbench harness to make cause-and-effect visible during perf investigations and automated regressions. Summary of changes: (1) kernel-hotspot-tracer: lightweight sampling probe that annotates scheduler and CUDA kernel entry/exit with a compact hotspot identifier derived from callsite and op-type. Emits NVTX ranges when available and falls back to short tracepoints for environments without NVTX. Includes FLOPS-adjusted counters to avoid over-weighting long-running kernels. (2) kvcache-offset-histogram: on-KVcache-access histogram recording offset distribution and small-window reuse counts to detect pathological stride patterns introduced by certain tokenizers or model layouts. Buckets are power-of-two with a dense region for accesses under 1KB. Exported as Prometheus-friendly counters and optional trace annotations for correlation. (3) continuous-batch-pressure-probe: emits an instantaneous batch_pressure gauge when the batch queue begins to back up and a sampled per-window pressure histogram for debug builds. Hooks into the batching controller earlier in the pipeline to capture producer-side pressure and includes a token-rate estimator to reduce noise from transient spikes. (4) simple_seq_bench microbench: executable to exercise variable-length sequences, stridey KV access patterns, and kernel-heavy ops; emits a compact trace and histogram CSV for offline analysis and dashboard ingestion. Design: probes are guarded by feature flags and sample rate knobs with defaults off in release builds. Histogram storage is ring-buffer backed and uses per-worker aggregators to minimize atomics. NVTX ranges are used when available with a fallback so traces are consistent across environments. Rollout: feature-gated behind telemetry.feature_flags.kernel_hotspot and telemetry.feature_flags.kvcache_hist, default disabled. Canary rollout plan: 5 percent traffic for one week then expand if metrics are stable. Tests: unit tests for histogram bucketing and ring buffer safety, stress test for multi-producer writes, and a short GPU integration job that runs the microbench in CI. Backwards compatibility: non-breaking and opt-in. Follow-ups: small observability ingestion parser update (observability-pack) and a future probe for kernel memory-bandwidth when cupi integration is feasible.
commits: a3d9f7e: add kernel-hotspot-tracer core probe and emitters
b41c02d: implement kvcache-offset-histogram and ring buffer storage
c7e4d81: hook batch-pressure-probe into batching controller and flags
f2b3a6c: add simple_seq_bench microbench harness and CSV exporter
d90f1be: tests: histogram, sampling, and multi-producer stress test
e14b9ad: docs: feature flags and bench quickstart
source.changes_summary: Introduce three low-overhead probes (kernel hotspot, KV cache offset histogram, batch pressure) and a microbench harness. Instrument batching controller and KV cache accessor; add unit and integration tests and docs for feature flags.
review_comments: Ethan Cole: Concern about atomic contention in histogram hot path. Suggest per-thread local buffer flushed to ring to reduce atomics and improve throughput.
Priya Natarajan (author): Added per-worker local aggregator that collapses hot path to a single atomic on flush and included a 16-producer stress test in the test suite.
Lina Fernandez: NVTX ranges are helpful; please ensure the perf dashboard includes a concise legend mapping hotspot ids to callsites.
Priya Natarajan (author): Added legend generation in simple_seq_bench and a README snippet showing how mapping is uploaded with traces.
Marcus Liu: Initial CI GPU run timed out on a long kernel; reran and it passed. Approving after CI green and minor nits fixed.
CI Bot: gpu-integration (short-run) rerun passed at 2026-02-19T03:14:22Z. All checks green.
ci_checks: gpu-integration (short-run) - fail initial timeout then rerun passed
merge: notes: Canary rollout configured for 5% with telemetry opt-in; observability ingest update scheduled in observability-pack PR ENG-8849
source.release_notes: Low-overhead kernel hotspot tracing, KV cache offset histogram, and batch pressure probe added. Microbench harness included for regression tracking. Feature-gated and disabled by default.
source.notes_for_oncall: If high batch_pressure rates are seen after rollout: disable telemetry.feature_flags.kernel_hotspot and telemetry.feature_flags.kvcache_hist via remote config, roll back canary, and collect sample trace from /var/tmp/simple_seq_bench.trace.srw and tag ENG-8724 on the incident.

source.title: per-channel requantization + kernel fusion with KV-cache hinting to improve INT8/FP8 steady-state throughput
source.author: Ethan Morales
source.release_notes: Improves INT8/FP8 inference throughput and p95 latency via per-channel requantization and kernel-level fusion; safe fallback to FP8 when per-tensor quantization quality degrades.
source.description:
Title: Per-channel requantization + kernel fusion with KV-cache hinting to improve INT8/FP8 steady-state throughput

Summary / Motivation:
We observed that for real traffic profiles (small interactive requests with hot prefixes) the current INT8 execution path suffers from uneven per-head/per-channel scaling which results in lower GEMM utilization and extra dequant/quant roundtrips around attention and MLP. This PR introduces a per-channel requantization stage inside the microkernel and fuses the dequant/scale operations into the attention + MLP kernels when safe. Additionally, it adds a lightweight KV-cache hint that allows the scheduler to prefer pre-warmed KV workers when requantization is detected, reducing cold-KV stalls for hot prefixes. The goal is to increase steady-state throughput without impacting worst-case correctness or model quality.

What changed (high level):
- Introduced a per-channel requantization pass in the inference runtime that produces per-channel scale/zero offsets used by fused kernels. This avoids re-quantizing to a single global scale across wide layers.
- Fused dequant + GEMM + requant hot-paths in the GPU microkernel for attention and first MLP matmuls; fallbacks exist to existing dequant/separate-gemm path when fusion preconditions fail (e.g., unsupported layout, odd channel count).
- Added a scheduler hint (kv_cache_hint) propagated from request metadata when prefix reuse is detected; scheduler uses hint to bias placement to nodes with warm KV caches. This is advisory only and respects capacity/policy boundaries.
- FP8 compatibility: added a transparent FP8 fallback where per-channel requantization indicates numeric instability from INT8 scaling; runtime will automatically switch to FP8 kernels for that layer and emit a metric.
- Extended kernel capability negotiation so the compilation pipeline can choose fused kernels at deploy time based on model/layout characteristics.
- New microbenchmarks and regression tests + updated continuous-batching integration tests to cover fused paths.

Why this is different from prior quant work:
Previous PRs optimized global/tensor-level requantization and scheduling around KV batching. This change targets per-channel variability and moves the fusion directly into microkernels to reduce memory traffic and kernel launches. The KV hint is a low-risk way to improve locality without changing core placement policies.

Benchmarks (representative):
Environment: 1xA100-80GB, Llama-2-13B quantized to INT8 using Redwood quant pipeline, synthetic traffic replay derived from production traces (mix of short interactive and streaming requests).
- Throughput (tokens/sec): +1.65x compared to baseline INT8 path at 1k token steady-state (microbatching enabled).
- Tail latency (P95 tokens/seq, 512 context): -22% (improvement).
- Token cost estimate: -11.8% due to fewer kernel launches and better utilization.
- FP8 fallback path triggered on ~2.4% of layers in stress tests; when triggered, throughput impact limited to -8% for that request while preserving quality.

Checklist / Testing:
- [x] Unit tests for per-channel scale extraction and validation
- [x] GPU microkernel unit tests (correctness vs reference FP32 for 64 random seeds)
- [x] Integration: continuous-batching + kv-cache hint end-to-end test
- [x] Perf: added benchmark harnesses and CI perf job (used to validate +1.65x)
- [x] Model quality smoke: token-level logprob deltas across evaluation prompt-set (no regression beyond tolerances)
- [x] Safety: fallback to non-fused code and FP8 if numerical checks fail at runtime

Commits (high-level):
- a3d9f1e add per-channel scale extractor and validation logic
- b7c2d1a implement fused dequant-gemm-requant microkernel (CUDA)
- c48f0d2 extend kernel registry and capability negotiation in compile pipeline
- d90b3aa add kv_cache_hint propagation and scheduler hooks
- e1f4b55 add perf harness + benchmarks for fused path
- f0a7b23 tests: unit/integration updates and flaky test fix
